summary: The flight crew experienced an uncommanded early descent while the aircraft was on autopilot and cleared for an approach[cite: 1]. The Captain immediately disconnected the automation and initiated a climb, receiving a minor GPWS warning during the recovery[cite: 1, 3]. The incident was attributed to an automation failure and documented for maintenance[cite: 2, 4].
rationale: This incident is classified as aircraft because the root cause was a mechanical or systems failure within the flight control and automation systems[cite: 1, 2]. Specifically, the autopilot initiated an uncommanded descent before intercepting the glide slope, which aligns with the "Flight Control & Automation Malfunctions" repeating issue for this category[cite: 1]. It is not classified as Procedure because there is no indication that the crew improperly configured the FMS or ignored SOPs; rather, the system acted without being prompted[cite: 1]. Furthermore, it is distinct from Software and Automation as the narrative points to a general system malfunction requiring a physical maintenance write-up rather than a specific database or software programming error[cite: 2].
text:
Narrative 1 - 'FMS/FMC/Autopilot Failure. I looked at the instrumentation to confirm glide slope intercept and immediately recognized that we were descending early. I went to announce it to the CA (Captain) (Pilot Flying) who also recognized the situation at the same time. The aircraft was on autopilot; flying level; with VOR/LOC activated after being cleared for the approach into ZZZ. The aircraft; without being prompted; began to descend before intercepting glide slope at some point. The Captain instantly disconnected the autopilot and auto throttles; initiating a full power climb back to the intercept altitude. We received a call from ATC and received a minor GPWS warning as we were climbing. I feel that crew coordination; confirmation of what the aircraft was supposed to be doing and the reaction by the Pilot Flying (CA) were proper and allowed us to detect the situation early on. Because it was an automation failure; the aircraft will be written up for maintenance.'
Narrative 2 - 'FMS/FMC/Autopilot Failure. It was a dark night; but severe clear. Being intimately familiar with the ZZZ topography; I am keenly aware of the high; rising terrain between the Final Approach Fix and Runway XXR. The airplane started descending without pilot input. I immediately noticed this and announced out loud: 'Something is wrong!'. The aircraft was on autopilot; flying level; with VOR/LOC activated after being cleared for the ZZZ ILS XXR approach. Before intercepting the glide slope; the aircraft began to descend on its own without being prompted. I disconnected the Autopilot and Auto-throttles and initiated an immediate climb. Within seconds after proactively initiating the climb; we received a single GPWS: 'Terrain'; and seconds later; ATC called and said: 'Check Altitude' and gave us the Altimeter Setting (which was set correctly.) I continued and landed safely. I believe that this was an automation failure of some kind and that the immediate action taken was just. Emphasis should be made; and recognition of; the excellent crew coordination by both myself and a superior FO (First Officer) ameliorated what could have been a potential negative outcome.'

summary: While relocating an airplane, the pilot experienced a loss of engine power and backfiring at 6,500 feet MSL[cite: 6, 11]. Despite attempting to troubleshoot by switching tanks and adjusting the mixture, the engine continued to run rough, prompting the pilot to execute a safe off-airport landing in a field[cite: 6]. A mechanic was dispatched to inspect the aircraft, which had been inactive for several years prior to its recent annual inspection[cite: 9, 10].
rationale: The classification of aircraft is appropriate because the primary problem was a total loss of power and rough running engine, fitting squarely within the "Engine & Powerplant Failures" subset[cite: 6]. The physical mechanical failure of the engine during cruise flight forced the emergency landing[cite: 6]. It is not classified as Incorrect / Not Installed Part because, although the aircraft had been inactive, there is no direct evidence provided of a specific botched maintenance action or wrong part installed prior to the flight[cite: 10]. It is strictly an aircraft hardware failure in the moment, differentiating it from Human Factors where pilot mismanagement would be the primary cause[cite: 6, 11].
text: Narrative 1 - 'I was taking delivery of an airplane that some friends had purchased and relocating it from ZZZ1 to ZZZ2. The first leg was to be from ZZZ1 to ZZZ3 via ZZZ4 to avoid restricted airspace. I completed the preflight and departed ZZZ1. I climbed to 6500 ft. MSL and about 35 minutes into the flight the engine began to lose power and backfire. I switched mags; switched fuel tanks; reduced power and adjusted mixture but nothing seemed to make any difference. At around 3;500 feet MSL the engine began to run smoothly again and I decided that I would land at ZZZ4 for maintenance. The engine began to run rough again and I opted to land in a field rather than overfly the city in hopes of reaching the airport. The off airport landing was made without incident. There was no damage to the airplane or myself. I secured the airplane and left a note on the nearby farmhouse door with my contact information. I was later contacted by the authorities and gave them the details over the phone. A passerby gave me a ride to ZZZ4 where I met a fellow pilot that gave me a ride back to ZZZ2. A mechanic from ZZZ1 is coming to inspect the airplane.I do not know what caused the problem at this point. The airplane had just completed it's annual inspection but had been inactive for a number of years prior. The preflight and engine run-up were normal and the airplane climbed well to 6;500 feet.I am happy with the outcome of my decision not to overfly a populated area with few options had the engine quit entirely.'

summary: While enroute at 40,000 feet, the aircraft lost both stabilizer channels and the associated trim[cite: 12]. The crew diverted and hand-flew the final 1,500 feet of the approach, requiring both pilots to apply 30-40 pounds of pressure on the yoke to maintain a normal glide path due to the aircraft being stuck in a nose-down trim configuration[cite: 12, 14, 15, 16].
rationale: This event perfectly aligns with the aircraft category due to the mechanical failure of the stabilizer trim channels, which is explicitly listed under "Flight Control & Automation Malfunctions"[cite: 12]. The physical malfunction forced the crew to manually overpower the aircraft's control surfaces[cite: 15, 16]. It is not classified as Procedure or Human Factors because the crew successfully followed the checklist and worked together to mitigate an unavoidable hardware failure[cite: 12, 17]. The root cause was an internal mechanical systems failure, not an external Environment factor or a Company Policy issue[cite: 12].
text: Narrative 1 - 'Enroute ZZZ-ZZZ1; Divert to ZZZ2. 40;000 feet; lost both stab channels and associated trim. Followed checklist; landed safe on long runway in ZZZ2. Stab was stuck at .2. Normal for landing is .6. As we slowed the autopilot could no longer keep up.  Hand flying the last 1500 feet to touchdown. With the airplane trimmed in a nose down configuration; the right seat pilot helped me by pulling the yoke back. Approximately 30-40 pounds of pressure was needed to keep a normal glide path.This was my SIC's very first leg. He did a very good job with running the checklist and assisting me.'

summary: During cruise flight, the crew received a steady Stab Out of Trim annunciator light and subsequently verified the trim was not operating[cite: 18, 31]. After isolating the fault to the A autopilot system, they lost RVSM capability but safely continued their flight at a lower altitude[cite: 20, 23]. The crew noted a previous recurring write-up for the same issue that had been blanket-deferred via MEL rather than properly isolated[cite: 21, 22, 27].
rationale: The aircraft classification is assigned because the inciting incident was a hardware malfunction, specifically the A autopilot faulting and a stabilizer trim system failure[cite: 23, 31]. This falls under the "Flight Control & Automation Malfunctions" criteria for the aircraft label[cite: 18]. While there are strong elements of MEL and maintenance tracking issues in the aircraft's history, the primary problem for this specific flight crew's event was the active mechanical failure of the autopilot and trim system[cite: 18, 27]. It is distinct from MEL because the crew was reacting to an in-flight avionics failure, rather than refusing the aircraft on the ground due to an inappropriate paperwork deferral[cite: 18, 19].
text: Narrative 1 - 'During cruise; a steady Stab Out of Trim annunciator light illuminated. We accomplished the appropriate checklist. Contacted dispatch and the [flight operations] and determined that it was safe to continue the flight to ZZZ. Thanks to the [flight operations] inputs we also notified ZZZ1 center that we were non RVSM (due to no autopilot) and with their approval we were able to continue at FL340. On arrival into ZZZ we wrote up the malfunction. The avionics tech informed us that the aircraft had the same malfunction on DATE. The remedial action for that malfunction was to MEL both autopilots. Our Avionics tech was able to isolate that the fault was indeed in the A autopilot system and that the B would operate normally. After completing the appropriate documentation we continued our flight back to ZZZ2. Being able to consult with Dispatch and the [flight operations] was a force multiplier; it was the [flight operations] who remembered that our system failure would result in our no longer being able to be RVSM and our dispatcher was able to provide us with the information that if we had to descend from RVSM airspace we would still be able to complete the flight at the lower altitudes.I find it a bit concerning that there was a prior write up on DATE and they were unable to determine the system fault but instead just MEL'd both A/P's and sent it on its way. Our avionics tech was able to show us the message clearly showing that it was A/P A that was faulting and not B. The previous write up was at an outstation so there was probably time pressure involved to 'get the jet out' but if someone had taken a safety pause to run the appropriate check they would have isolated the fault instead of just MEL'ing the entire system. There was a moment of 'question' when operating the checklist. The first question statement 1) Is the stabilizer trimming? Yes; No. A point of discussion among us was that since we were in cruise and the air was calm we weren't sure if the stabilizer was or wasn't trimming? We eventually determined that by reducing airspeed there should be a change in stab trim. It was by doing this that we were able to determine that the trim was indeed not operating. I would recommend exploring the option of including a statement in step one to verify the trim operation before continuing to the next step. After working the problem from event to resolution on the ground and return to flight I am wondering as to the operation of the stab trim system and selected autopilots. Was this as it appears to be a case of A autopilot faulting but giving us the indication of a stab out of trim? If so then wouldn't changing to B autopilot have also cleared the fault such as the return to service MEL? Does there need to be a revisit to the Stabilizer out of trim checklist to address if it's an A/P fault as opposed to a Stab Trim?'

summary: While configuring on short final, the crew received a Flap Asymmetry EICAS message and a LE Slat Disagree Light[cite: 37]. The Captain discontinued the approach, the crew requested priority handling, and they executed a successful ILS landing on their second attempt after running the appropriate checklists[cite: 37].
rationale: This report is classified as aircraft because the root cause was a clear mechanical systems failure, matching the "flap/slat asymmetries" condition explicitly listed under the "Flight Control & Automation Malfunctions" category[cite: 37]. The crew was forced to abort their landing due to the physical inability of the aircraft's flight control surfaces to configure properly[cite: 37]. It is not a Procedure issue because the crew followed proper protocols by executing a missed approach and running the checklist upon identifying the malfunction[cite: 37]. Furthermore, it is not a Software and Automation issue because flap and slat disagree warnings generally indicate a physical hardware misalignment rather than a pure software logic error[cite: 37].
text:
Narrative 1 - 'Configured on final and received Flap Asymmetry EICAS. Captain performed discontinued approach and briefed the crew on a plan. We requested priority handling and ran the checklist. Vectors to an ILS for an uneventful landing.'
Narrative 2 - 'On short final after final flap configuration (25); (as Pilot Monitoring) I noticed a LE Slat Disagree Light and verbalized it to the crew. The Captain announced that we were discontinuing the approach. We requested priority handling and ran the checklist upon completion of the missed approach procedure. We coordinated with ATC for extended vectors and came around for another ILS. The subsequent approach and landing were uneventful.'

summary: Approximately 450 miles from their destination, the crew received a GREEN RSVR LO LEVEL ECAM message and observed the green hydraulic system pressure cycling down to zero[cite: 38, 40]. The crew prepared for a gravity gear extension, held to configure the aircraft, and executed a safe landing with priority handling[cite: 45, 46, 48]. After landing, fire crews reported smoke coming from the #2 engine, prompting the crew to shut down the engines and have the aircraft towed to the ramp[cite: 51, 52, 55].
rationale: The aircraft label applies here due to a cascade of physical mechanical failures, beginning with a hydraulic leak and culminating in an engine smoke event[cite: 38, 40, 51]. These events map directly to the "Engine & Powerplant Failures" and "Landing Gear & Brake Issues" (via hydraulic loss) detailed in the reference guide[cite: 40, 51]. It is not classified as Manuals despite the crew noting missing ECAM procedural steps, because the missing text was secondary to the critical hydraulic failure that initiated the emergency[cite: 38, 39]. It is entirely distinct from Human Factors as the crew successfully managed a high-workload, complex hardware failure without committing handling errors[cite: 45, 46, 47].
text: Narrative 1 - 'Approximately 450 miles from ZZZ; ECAM message: GREEN RSVR LO LEVEL. Crew confirmed validity of message on overhead panel and on HYD Systems Page.Crew Complied with ECAM Actions:GREEN PUMP 1 OFF GREEN PUMP 2 OFFBRK - A/SKID ALTN/ON(At this time; no additional procedural steps displayed on ECAM; and PITCH FEEL 1 and SPLR 6 lights NOT illuminated) Note: ECAM indications cycled continually during remainder of flight.Crew reviewed QRH and AOM for additional information. (Crew noted that PROC: L/G GRAVITY EXTENSION was a requirement with Green System failure which was NOT displayed on ECAM). Crew observed HYD Pressure on HYD SYSTEMS Page continually cycling from approximately 2;400 psi to 0 psi. Crew transferred aircraft control to Captain. Crew established ACARS comm with company and unsuccessfully attempted voice comm. Crew established phone patch with company on ARINC and requested communication with System Matter Expert (SME). Communication with SME was not successful due to poor reception. Crew reverted back to ACARS comm with Company. Note: Eventually additional procedural step displayed on ECAM for: L/G GRAVITY EXTENSION in addition to PITCH FEEL 1 and SPLR 6 lights on overhead panel. Crew discussed situation and plan of action which would include Gravity Extension of Gear. Crew elected to request hold at 12;000 ft. approximately 25 miles from ZZZ airport in order to complete all checklists; brief approach; compute landing distance; consider fuel remaining and potential Go-Around; discuss brake usage during landing roll; and to configure aircraft early due to anticipated slow movement of slats/flaps. Crew elected to request priority handling; anticipating the possibility of having to stop on the runway. Crew elected to start APU during hold; anticipating shutting down engines following landing to allow towing of aircraft to ramp.ILS Approach and landing to Runway X ZZZ was uneventful and crew was able to clear the runway following landing. Crew established comm with fire/rescue crew. Fire Crew reported smoke coming from #2 Engine. With no additional fire indications in cockpit; crew elected to shut down the #2 Engine. Fire crew reported smoke subsided. Crew established comm with ZZZ Maintenance staff and then shut down #1 engine. ZZZ Maintenance staff directed crew to pull the #2 Fire Handle; without actuating fire bottles. Aircraft was towed to ramp.'

summary: During the takeoff roll at 80 knots, the aircraft rejected takeoff, and the Captain later informed a flight attendant that her cabin door handle had flown up[cite: 58, 59, 61]. Despite the flight attendants re-securing the door and requesting maintenance due to safety concerns, the Captain insisted on attempting a second takeoff and asked the flight attendant to physically hold the handle[cite: 63, 65, 69, 71]. The crew refused to fly out of safety concerns, resulting in the aircraft eventually being taken out of service by maintenance[cite: 72, 73].
rationale: This is classified as aircraft because the initiating event was a mechanical failure of the cabin door handle disengaging during the takeoff roll[cite: 61, 63]. This fits within the physical hardware and system failures encompassing the aircraft's physical structure[cite: 61]. While the narrative heavily features elements of Company Policy and Procedure regarding the Captain's controversial decision to avoid maintenance and the resulting crew conflict, the assigned aircraft label strictly reflects the underlying physical fault that triggered the aborted takeoff[cite: 61, 71, 75]. It is not Staffing or Human Factors as the root hazard was a defective door mechanism, not a lack of personnel or a pilot handling error[cite: 61, 73].
text: Narrative 1 - 'Final leg of our trip ZZZ-ZZZ1 Flight XXXX on the 737 aircraft. We closed the door on time; all procedures for arming and cross-checking the doors were done; all flight attendants (FAs) witnessed each other pushing down on the 737 door handle. All call was completed. We taxied to the runway; and we began to take off at 80 kts.; the plane slammed on the breaks and aborted take off. No information was relayed to the passengers or crew. Approximately 10 minutes passed before FAs received a call from the Captain confirming an aborted takeoff; and informed Flight Attendant A that her door handle had flown up during take off. Captain suggested Flight Attendant A double check her door handle. Flight Attendant A pushed door handle down about an inch and heard the handle/door re-secure in place. Flight Attendant A reiterated to Captain that the door handle was pushed down and armed correctly at the gate; and Flight Attendant C witnessed. Captain then advised that Flight Attendant A hold on to the door handle during the second take-off attempt to make sure it didn't fly up/disengage again. Captain then gave a PA explaining that the issue 'fixed itself' and that we would take off again shortly. After the Captain announced 'prepare for takeoff;' a passenger from the exit row rushed to the back and expressed he did not feel safe to continue to fly. Captain heard the conversation between Flight Attendant A and Flight Attendant B on the interphone and requested FAs convince the passenger to continue on. Passenger declined and we returned to the gate. Flight Attendant C entered flight deck and spoke with the Captain for several minutes requesting maintenance to check the aircraft due to the aborted landing. She explained she did not feel safe to continue and that there was a serious door handle issue. Captain continued to insist the aircraft was safe; but Flight Attendant C was not satisfied with this answer. As a result; the crew expressed a need to follow safety protocol and have the faulty door checked by Maintenance. After Maintenance checked the aircraft; the aircraft was taken out of service.The crew continued to express that we were not refusing to fly/work the flight; as it was important for us to get home. However; due to the Captain's original decision to avoid Maintenance and attempt a second takeoff; as well as his request to Flight Attendant A to hold on to the door handle during takeoff; the crew did not feel safe flying with him. Flight Attendant A called and requested new pilots. Request was denied and FAs were removed from the flight.Suggestions: We are suggesting pilots to follow safety protocol and support FA safety concerns.'

summary: A pilot broadcasted that she had lost all engine power and was preparing to land in a nearby field[cite: 78]. The local controller immediately cleared pattern traffic and began coordinating an emergency response[cite: 79, 80]. The pilot subsequently restarted the engine, returned to the field, and landed without incident[cite: 80, 82].
rationale: The aircraft label is assigned because the primary problem was a total loss of engine power, which is explicitly listed as a key indicator under "Engine & Powerplant Failures" in the reference guide[cite: 78]. The incident revolved entirely around the aircraft's mechanical inability to sustain thrust[cite: 78, 80]. It is not classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because there is no mention of external factors like bird strikes or fuel starvation causing the failure[cite: 78, 83]. It is also not a Procedure event, as both the pilot and ATC executed their emergency protocols effectively to resolve the hardware emergency[cite: 79, 81].
text: Narrative 1 - 'The pilot called the local position up 'in the blind' stating that she had lost all power to the engine and would be landing in a nearby field. I ended training and begin landing all pattern traffic while clearing out departures. Began calling 911 to start towards the aircraft. It was about that time the aircraft called that she had restarted the engine and believed she could make it back to the field. I cleared her to land Runway XX and asked she required any assistance on the ground; in which she responded to the negative. She landed without incident. I don't believe any kind of recommendation could have prevented this occurrence.'

summary: During two separate visual approaches, flight crews received erroneous EGPWS terrain alerts at very low altitudes (30-50 feet) while fully configured and stabilized on the glidepath[cite: 83, 85]. In the first narrative, the crew executed an escape maneuver, while in the second, the crew landed normally without incident because they had the runway in sight and determined the alert was a mistake[cite: 84, 85].
rationale: Based on the updated reference guide logic, this is classified as aircraft because the erroneous EGPWS alerts are attributed to an unverified hardware sensor malfunction or radar altimeter fault rather than a known software programming error[cite: 83, 85]. Both crews verified they were on the correct glidepath with no obstacles, isolating the issue to the aircraft's internal warning systems[cite: 83, 85]. It is not classified as Software and Automation because there is no evidence of a systemic database error or logic flaw[cite: 83, 85]. It is also not Human Factors because the crews were flying stabilized approaches and were not responsible for triggering the false mechanical warning[cite: 83, 85].
text:
Narrative 1 - 'We received a EGPWS 'TOO LOW; TERRAIN' Alert at 30-50 feet. VMC Day PAPI OTS (out of service) flying ILS XXL ZZZZ. We adhered to SOP by taking positive action until the alert stopped. We actually didn't have any obstacles in our path since we were stable on the GS; maybe it was erroneous as the alert sounded once. The Aural Alert was not due to incorrect Flaps or Landing Gear configuration; and since it was a stabilized approach; I'm not sure what warranted a EGPWS alert. SOP X.X.X EGPWS RECOVERYA. In day visual conditions with terrain and obstacles clearly in sight; immediately take positive corrective action until the EGPWS warning/alert stops; terrain clearance is assured; and advise ATC.Suggestions: Things that we could have done better was just doing the EGPWS Escape procedure and tried the approach again.'
Narrative 2 - 'We received a EGPWS 'TOO LOW; TERRAIN' alert during the flare to runway XXL at ZZZZ. It was day VFR; the aircraft was approximately 30-40 feet and over the touchdown zone. The aircraft was in the landing configuration with the landing gear down and flaps 5. The CA and I did not perform the EGPWS procedure as we were directly over the runway and on centerline; there was no need for the EGPWS procedure or a go-around to be called as that would created a bigger threat. We believed the alert went off by mistake. The plane landed fine without incident.'

summary: During the initial climb after a normal takeoff roll, a flight instructor and student experienced continuous engine RPM surges from 2300 down to 1000[cite: 90, 91]. The instructor immediately took control, declared an emergency, and executed a 180-degree turn to land safely on a parallel runway[cite: 92, 93, 94]. Post-flight maintenance discovered a leak in the fuel line[cite: 98].
rationale: This incident is classified as aircraft because the root cause was a physical fuel line leak that led to engine power loss and RPM surges, directly aligning with the "Engine & Powerplant Failures" category[cite: 91, 98]. The mechanical failure of the engine hardware forced an emergency landing[cite: 94, 98]. It is not classified as Incorrect / Not Installed Part because the report does not indicate the leak was caused by a botched maintenance installation prior to the flight[cite: 87, 98]. Furthermore, it is not Human Factors because the instructor executed a flawless transfer of controls and emergency return, proving the issue was entirely mechanical[cite: 92, 95].
text: Narrative 1 - 'At approximately XA:30 pm my student and I were departing from ZZZ to practice commercial maneuvers. After a normal runup and following all procedures as prescribed in the POH; we lined up on Runway XXL. My student was pilot flying at the time. She applied full throttle and we both verbally confirmed 'Engine instruments in the green' meaning and confirming that oil temperature; oil pressure; fuel pressure; and RPM were all within normal range. We were achieving approximately 2300 RPM on our takeoff roll and RPM was steady. After rotation and during our initial climb we experienced RPM surges from 2300 down to 1000 and back up to 2300 continuously. I immediately took control and verbally called out 'my controls' which were confirmed through a three way positive exchange of controls. I called Tower and announced that we were experiencing RPM loss. I made the decision to do a 180 back to the parallel Runway (XYR). I followed all emergency procedures for Engine Power Loss in Flight as prescribed in the POH and confirmed that oil temperature; oil pressure; and fuel pressure were still 'in the green.' I [requested priority handling] with Tower as I turned a right crosswind/base off of XXL for XYR and was cleared to land on XYR. We landed safely on the runway and maintenance found that there was a leak in the fuel line.'

summary: A TRACON controller experienced a loss of separation between an arriving and a departing aircraft because an adjacent Center sector failed to climb the departure. The controller cited excessive coordination demands and confusion surrounding the facility's Letter of Agreement (LOA) as primary contributors to the breakdown.
rationale: This falls under Manuals because the primary breakdown in separation was directly tied to confusion, non-compliance, and excessive coordination dictated by the facility's written Letter of Agreement (LOA). The text-based directive governing inter-facility handoffs was either misunderstood or structurally inadequate to handle the traffic volume safely. It is not Company Policy because the incident centers on the tactical application of a specific written facility agreement rather than a broad management or HR directive. It is not Human Factors because the ambiguity and complex coordination mandated by the LOA actively induced the controllers' loss of situational awareness.
text:
Narrative 1 - 'I was working Radar. Traffic was steadily increasing; which always creates more coordination between facilities. I cleared Aircraft Y direct and had them contact Center as that sector had accepted the automated handoff. Shortly thereafter; I received the handoff on Aircraft X descending via the RNAV Arrival. The aircraft was at 16700 feet. I thought Center was going to climb Aircraft Y per the LOA (Letter of Agreement) but apparently that had not taken place yet.I went to Aircraft X and realized they were descending into Aircraft Y that was still level at 12000 feet northeast bound. I issued a left turn to heading 130 degrees and did not issue an altitude. Aircraft X was slow in reply but appeared to be turning but separation appeared to have been lost already. Aircraft X then replied they were responding to a TCAS and climbing. At that time they no longer appeared to be in a left turn but more like 170 degrees. Aircraft X advised me they would file a report and wanted me to provide them with the tail number of the other aircraft. I supplied them with a phone number to the watch supervisor desk. As far as I know; they never called the facility. I am told the closest proximity between the two aircraft was 1.42 miles and 900 feet vertical.Recommend better Staffing on each shift. There was no one available to work the assist position; so Arrival Data realizing the situation and the amount of traffic; left the data position and sat next to me to help with coordination and the extra set of eyes. But he did not catch the impending situation nor did I. There are many days that we have to work combined positions due to the poor staffing here.We have two other positions available to reduce workload; yet we do not have a body to put in the position. May not sound like much but there are several days each month where we have a total count of 1500-1800 operations and we do it with just 3 sectors. A place like the Center works 2500-3000 operations daily but do it with 8-10 sectors. Do the math; staffing is deplorable and services have to be denied or the controllers become stretched so thin that things like this happen constantly. It is only getting worse and morale is getting worse as well. I do not know where the Supervisor was during this; but I believe he was in the radar room. After it happened I reported the event to them. It is my fault; no one else's. I had other options and will exercise them in the future; like keeping both aircraft on my frequency and not depending on the receiving controller to climb the departure out of the arrivals way. We have pretty much gone stripless so I did not know where the aircraft was going until questioning the Aircraft Y. Probably not a great idea. Maybe create a departure procedure for aircraft departing the secondary airports that will ensure separation from the busy arrival procedures. We do not have RNAV departures; because apparently; the airlines do not want them? We also need to stop the excessive coordination with Center and other facilitates and follow the LOAs. Some days; Center coordinates every airplane. Tell them to realize; if you clear aircraft to the same fix; they will get there and then they ask Approach to bail them out with stacks over fixes; or headings other than the Letter of Agreement; etc.'
Narrative 2 - 'Aircraft X Was on an RNAV Arrival from the north. I switched Aircraft X to Approach. Aircraft Y checked on at 11;700 feet climbing to 12;000 feet. Aircraft Y was on a departure route from Approach. At this time Aircraft X was around 14;500 feet. I gave Aircraft Y the altimeter and told the pilot that he would climb in a couple of minutes for traffic. Aircraft X responded to a RA at 12;600 feet and climbed back to 13;000 feet. When the aircraft were clear I climbed Aircraft Y to 15;000 feet which was his requested altitude.According to the Center/TRACON Letter of Agreement the Transfer of Control Point(TCP) will be the lateral limits of Approach Control core airspace and the shelf as depicted. It states Approach Control must provide separation between departing aircraft and between departures; over flights; and all arriving aircraft which have reached the TCP. The loss of separation happened between 12;000 and 12;600 feet about 5 miles south of the TCP.'

summary: A flight crew and gate agent struggled to determine the proper stowage procedure for a passenger's lithium-ion mobility scooter batteries. The pilot consulted the Flight Operations Manual (FOM) but found it incredibly confusing, poorly indexed, and scattered across multiple sections, forcing them to call a dangerous goods hotline for clarification.
rationale: This is classified as Manuals because the core safety issue was a confusing, poorly indexed Flight Operations Manual (FOM). The fragmented layout of the document prevented the crew from quickly finding accurate text-based procedures for lithium-ion battery stowage. It is not classified as Company Policy because the underlying rule was safe and existed; the failure was the structural design and navigability of the manual's text itself. It is not Chart Or Publication because the FOM is a text-based operational handbook rather than a navigational chart or EFB moving map.
text: Narrative 1 - 'After coming back in the jetway after doing the walk around; the passengers were pre-boarding. There was a gate agent and a passenger with what I would have described as an electronic scooter getting ready to board.  They advised me that the device had lithium-ion batteries and asked me what to do with it. I told them that I would contact a ramp agent and follow up with my manuals. I told my student to continue to prep the A/C (aircraft) for departure and that I would handle this issue. I started searching the FOM for guidance when the ramp lead came to the flight deck. He told me that their policy was to remove the batteries(they were easily removable) and then stow both the device and the batteries in cargo bin. I asked if they secured the batteries in anything; and he said no. This didn't sound correct to me; so I continued to search the FOM. The first thing that I found in the FOM was under a  Load Closeout notification. I had this bookmarked; for when I teach my students about the load closeout. It says 'NOTE: Dry ice and wheelchair batteries loaded as passenger-checked baggage are shown as XX and XY on the load closeout and do not require a NOTOC.' Notice that this does not say what kind of batteries. I did not believe that lithium-ion batteries qualified for that; so I continued to search. I searched wheelchair batteries and that returned the same result of what I'd just read. I then searched lithium-ion batteries; and read through the long list of restricted articles; and came to a page ; with several lines of information on lithium-ion batteries. At the bottom was the entry for Lithium-Ion(Wheelchairs/Mobility Aids/Stroller) in Device or as a spare; which is hyperlinked to lithium-ion batteries. This listed 'When Battery Is Easily Removable.' This section seemed like the logical choice; but I found the whole thing confusing. I decided to call the dangerous good hotline for advice. They told me that I need to talk to someone else; and gave me the number. I called them and they told me that my choice was the correct one.  I also contacted ZZZ Chief Pilot since he was once a duty pilot and I have his number in my phone. He helped me with my search. I called back the ramp agent and he told me that they never handle those devices like that; that they always put both the device and the batteries in the bin. He stated that as a matter of fact; that was the only way for him to scan the device as it went in the bin.  I told him to scan it that way; but we were storing the batteries in a bag by the customers seat as advised. On the close out; we received the XY notification; but we departed anyway since we knew where the batteries were. Cause: Task saturation with a late arriving aircraft and doing OE (Operating Experience) with a new CA (Captain); that was also struggling to find the answers. FOM design.Suggestion: Better; more consistent training for gate agents. Improvements in the FOM to make information easier to find. The phase of flight construction of the FOM does not help that. Too many subject are scattered over too many areas. If you don't know the exact word to search; for example wheelchair vs. mobility device; you may miss information. In hindsight; I should have started with the duty pilot.'

summary: While retracting the landing gear to perform door maintenance, the gear bellcrank rotated backwards and snapped, causing structural damage to the aircraft fuselage. The mechanics noted that the Aircraft Maintenance Manual (AMM) instructed them to ensure the bellcrank was "overcenter" but completely failed to provide diagrams or definitions explaining what that looked like.
rationale: The Manuals label applies because the hazard was directly caused by an ambiguous, poorly detailed Aircraft Maintenance Manual (AMM) lacking critical diagrams. The absence of a clear textual or visual definition for the "overcenter" requirement in the maintenance manual led the mechanics to make an incorrect assessment. It is not classified as Equipment / Tooling because the gear retract mechanism did not spontaneously malfunction; it was damaged due to a maintenance action guided by a flawed manual. It is not Human Factors because the mechanics followed the written instructions to the best of their ability, failing only because the manual itself was deficient.
text:
Narrative 1 - 'The Aircraft was in for Service and an A check inspection. The LH MLG (Main Landing Gear) door hinge bushing had been written up for excessive play so the door needed to be removed for bushing replacement. I went to the aircraft and inspected the LH gear so it could be retracted to aid in easier removal of the LH MLG door. I called over a technician to assist me by holding the door during retraction. We both looked at the gear linkage and all the hardware was secure and the bell crank appeared to be in the correct position for gear retraction. The door was disconnected previously at the bellcrank on the door strut attach side. I went up into the flight deck and powered the aircraft and pressurized hydraulics. I raised the gear handle and the LH gear began to retract. A short time later before the gear was fully retracted I heard a whistle followed by the technician saying 'Gear Down'. I immediately returned the gear handle to the down position; waited for the gear to extend; depressurized the hydraulics and went to investigate the problem. When I arrived at the LH gear bay we found that the airframe to bellcrank linkage was broken. Upon further investigation we noted that the bellcrank had rotated backwards during retraction and caused the linkage to break and be pressed up into the up-lock assembly support frame area of the fuselage causing a dent and tear in the structure.In the maintenance procedure CRJ 200 AMM 32-12-00 for retracting the gear; with the door disconnected; it states to disconnect the door in the location that the door had been previously disconnected. There is also a caution in the section that states to make sure the bellcrank is overcenter to avoid damage to the aircraft. Both myself and another technician looked at the bellcrank and determined that it appeared to be in the correct position for retraction. During the retraction of the gear the bellcrank rotated backwards and caused the damage to the aircraft explained above. The Aircraft Maintenance Manual (AMM) needs to be revised showing an image of what you should expect to see for the note that says the bellcrank needs to be overcenter. It doesn't explain overcenter which way or how it should look overcenter. I don't believe this would have occurred if there would have been a better explanation of which position the bellcrank should have been in to avoid damage. Both I and the other technician are experienced mechanics and we both had determined at the time in our best judgment that it was in the correct position for retraction.'
Narrative 2 - 'On the hangar floor; I was asked to assist my coworker in retracting the gear for gear door work. We both observed bellcrank and arm; discussed it and came to the conclusion that when the gear retracted; we should achieve correct overcenter of the bellcrank and there would be no problem. I held the door open and my coworker put the gear up. All appeared normal until I heard a snap which was followed by the gear stopping short of being fully stowed. Blew whistle and yelled for gear down. Gear came down without incident. Then noted broken door arm and damaged airframe.MLG did not retract completely into the wheel well...Unfamiliarity with gear door direction of actuation.Whenever CRJ 200 MLG is retracted with the door disconnected; Door rods and bellcrank should be completely removed for the airframe and gear and left only attached to the gear door; No room for error if that action is taken.'

summary: A blind passenger requested a Braille safety guide and discovered that it was missing a cover and table of contents, making it difficult to find specific emergency information. Additionally, the passenger noted that the manual was written in an outdated Braille format rather than the current Unified English Braille standard.
rationale: This report is classified as Manuals because it specifically highlights deficiencies in the formatting and completeness of a published passenger safety guide. This maps directly to the newly added "Passenger Safety & Accessibility Guides" repeating issue. It is not Company Policy because the airline's policy to provide the guides is sound; the issue is the physical formatting of the text-based document itself. It is not Chart Or Publication because a Braille safety card is an operational safety manual rather than a navigational chart or aviation database.
text: Narrative 1 - '[One of] our passengers; who is blind; asked to read our Braille safety guide after his briefing. Passenger noticed two significant discrepancies that could impact the safety of our blind passengers.The first is there was no cover or table of contents. If he quickly needed to find specific information; he would not have been able to do so. He has read our Braille safety guides before and knew that they should come with these pages.The second issue was the Braille was written in an old format. He advised that it should be printed in Unified English Braille; as that became the adopted standard form of Braille writing in 2016.Recommendations are as follows: -Provide a protective covering to prevent pages becoming ripped or missing. -Include page numbers that flight attendants can read so they will know if they are providing a complete Safety Guide.-Print new Braille safety guides using Unified English Braille.Lessons learned include to always ask questions and give our guest opportunities to help us be safer and accommodating to all travelers.'

summary: Maintenance personnel discovered a popped circuit breaker and burn marks on a landing gear conduit, but an engineering review of the Minimum Equipment List (MEL) revealed absolutely no maintenance or operational procedures for the condition. The aircraft was dispatched but subsequently experienced an auto-brake fault and diverted, highlighting the danger of the MEL's lack of guidance.
rationale: This incident fits Manuals because the primary failure was the glaring omission of critical maintenance and operational guidance within the Minimum Equipment List (MEL) and Typical Repair manuals. The lack of documented text-based procedures left maintenance and engineering without a safe framework to ground the aircraft. It is not classified as Company Policy because the failure was specific to the text of the Airbus MEL documentation rather than a deliberate management mandate to ignore safety. It is not Aircraft because, while a wire was burnt, the systemic breakdown was the manual's failure to provide troubleshooting guidance for that specific hardware fault.
text: Narrative 1 - 'January 2015; I received a call from ZZZ Line Maintenance Technician X. An A320 Aircraft had a popped Circuit Breaker (C/B) L32; which was for Left gear brake fan. Technician X stated when C/B was pushed in; an [electrical] arc was seen on [Left] gear. ZZZ inspected the left gear conduit. I asked if the conduit was secure. He said it was secure; but with a burn mark. I said we need to contact engineering. We had a 3-way conference with company Engineer X. I had concerns from last year; many Airbus's had problems with landing gear conduits. I ask if we could dispatch aircraft in this condition. Engineer X checked his records and did not find any documents that we had to stop the aircraft. A Brake Steering Control Unit (BSCU) Bite check showed no faults. A review of MEL 32-48-01a; has no Maintenance (M) or Operations (O) procedures for this condition. C/B's L32-L35 were pulled and collared per Typical Repair (TR) 32-00-14; Brake Fan MEL 32-48-01a.On Takeoff out of ZZZ; A320 aircraft had an Auto Brake Fault. [Airbus] Web Airman showed Tachometer-1 fault. We had the aircraft divert to ZZZ1 for repairs; due to the fact it was going to a Non-maintenance station. Mechanics have pressure to move aircraft on Time. The aircraft was fully loaded and ready to go. The popped breaker was pulled and collared; but no thought was giving to other system wires damaged. Engineering was contacted. ZZZ did do a Bite check for other faults; but none found. We believed at the time the only damage was to the Brake Fan system. ZZZ1 maintenance found that the wire harness was damaged; which made Tachometer-1 fail. ZZZ1 [Maintenance] replaced the bad harness. The Airbus desk is very understaffed. Operations put great pressure on maintenance to move the aircraft. I have been told many times by dispatch; we do not change the Aircraft for maintenance convenience. The Typical Repair or the MEL should give more guidance [for] Landing Gear conduits. Maintenance Operations Center (MOC). Technician. Maintenance Control.'

summary: A mechanic deferred a damaged cargo compartment air conditioning duct louver using a specific Non-Essential Furnishing (NEF) code. The mechanic later learned that unwritten "tribal knowledge" restricted that NEF code to a different louver, prompting a recommendation to clarify the overly broad language in the manual.
rationale: This is classified as Manuals because the safety event was caused by overly broad, ambiguous language within the Non-Essential Furnishing (NEF) manual. The text-based document failed to specify which exact parts were eligible for deferral, forcing mechanics to rely on unwritten tribal knowledge. It is not Company Policy because the issue resides in the specific wording of the maintenance document rather than a systemic management scheduling failure. It is not Procedure because the mechanic followed the literal text of the manual perfectly; the manual's text was simply misleading.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I received a write up from the Flight Crew that the Ramp had reported a crack in the cargo bay. I took the aircraft out of service; and dispatched Maintenance to inspect the damage. Upon arrival; Maintenance discovered a crack in the air conditioning ducting. I queried the database for previous instances of this defect in order to develop a plan of action consistent with successful actions taken in the past. During this search I discovered NEF (Non-Essential Furnishing) XX-XX-XX-XX was used to defer an identical defect. It stated that 'Cargo compartment air conditioning vent louvers and screen may be damaged or missing.' I confirmed with the Mechanic on the ground that it was an air conditioning duct and that it was a louver that was damaged and proceeded to place the duct on MEL (Minimum Equipment List).Later the plane was taken out of service and a GMM (General Maintenance Manual) approved temporary repair was used to clear the NEF and return the aircraft to service. I was later advised that there existed tribal knowledge that back when this NEF was created it was intended to be applicable to a lower air conditioning duct (the one with a screen) but not the cargo compartment air conditioning duct damaged in this instance.Without the benefit of tribal knowledge; a plain language interpretation of NEF XX-XX-XX-XX would lead a reasonable person to believe that all cargo compartment duct louvers were eligible to be deferred and; in practice; have been deferred in the past. This distinction should be clarified in the NEF.'

summary: During descent, a flight crew noticed that their fuel transfer valves had failed to open automatically, leaving thousands of pounds of fuel trapped in the outer tanks. With no published QRH or ECAM procedure available for this specific failure, the crew successfully relied on their systems knowledge to manually cycle the circuit breakers to force the valves open.
rationale: This falls under Manuals because the primary hazard was the complete absence of a published emergency checklist, QRH procedure, or ECAM action for a critical systems failure. The flight manual's deficiency forced the crew to improvise a maintenance procedure during a critical phase of flight to avoid a fuel emergency. It is not Aircraft because, although the valves failed, the reporter's primary safety concern focuses heavily on the lack of documented emergency procedures to handle the hardware fault. It is not Human Factors because the crew reacted flawlessly; their only hurdle was the inadequate flight deck documentation.
text:
Narrative 1 - 'We experienced fuel transfer valve failure in flight. During the flight planning stages; I added 1000lbs of fuel for possible en-route turbulence and altitude flexibility. Thus; we were now planned to land with 7600lbs. If I remember correctly; ATC requested us to descend to either FL320 or FL300 early. Upon checking the fuel page when approaching the terminal area on downwind; I noticed the fuel transfer valves had not opened. I mentioned this to my First Officer (FO) and said let's re-check in a few minutes so they have a chance to open? We were now on an extended downwind; when we received a vector turning base leg. Upon rechecking the fuel page; it was still showing the valves closed. I clearly remember the left inboard tank was showing approximately 1500lbs and the right 1700lbs. We were down to approximately 6300lbs total fuel. The automatic valve transfer appeared to have failed - and without an associated ECAM. Therefore; we discussed the situation. Both of us felt they should have opened no later than 1700lbs. Therefore; we began reviewing viable options. Since we were on a 20 plus mile final and setting up for the approach per ATC; my mindset changed from thinking I had just over 6000lbs total usable fuel to potentially 2500-2800lbs. At 4000' and 20mi from the airport; it was not reasonable to message or phone maintenance. Both of us shared a safety concern and were uncomfortable about our apparent total usable fuel level (3000lbs current position). Over the years of being a Captain on this jet; I've never seen the transfer valves fail to open. Drawing on experience and system knowledge; I was aware of a maintenance MEL procedure which would force transfer valves open for known faults or other related issues. The MEL would direct flight crews during preflight to cycle Circuit Breaker (CB) A10-11 and M 23/24 in order to force them open. Since we were absent and ECAM; QRH or memory procedure for this situation; I suggested to the FO that we consider forcing those valves open using those CB's. These CB's only affected the transfer valve; not fuel pumps. This option seemed logical and safe option in light of the time constraints we had to rectify the situation. Moreover; I did not want to un-necessarily declare a fuel emergency if we could safely remedy the stuck valves. With the concurrence of the FO; I elected to re-cycle CB's A-10/11 and M23/24 to force those valves to open. The fuel transfer process did begin after the CB's were cycled. After landing we contacted both local and maintenance and explained that the transfer valves failed to open around the 1500lbs-1700lbs levels. Additionally; a maintenance write-up for the valves failing to automatically open and the CB cycle was entered into the ACARS. Maintenance control mentioned that sometimes the sensor in the tanks might not trigger if we were prominently in a level flight - i.e. extended downwind and not turning. Please note: Since safety is always paramount; the option of doing nothing and hoping for the best; did not appear to be the best course of action.'
Narrative 2 - 'On arrival we were descending via the assigned STAR and were assigned by ATC to runway XY which has a bottom altitude of 6000 feet and airspeed of 210 KIAS. ATC was running unusually long finals (that evening for having VFR weather); I only state that because that distance added about 60 extra miles to our flight at very low altitude and airspeed which requires higher thrust levels and consumes more fuel. On our base turn in the Captain and I both noticed that there were only 2 green ECAM MSGs on the lower left side of the Upper ECAM (SEAT BELTS and NO SMOKING); but no OUTER TK FUEL XFRD MSG. We checked our ECAM Fuel Page and saw that there was approximately 1710 lbs. of Fuel in the Left Inner Wing Tank but only about 1520 lbs. of fuel in the Right Inner Wing Tank. The amount of fuel remaining in the Right Inner Wing Tank should have triggered the Transfer Valves to Open (we basically had 3000 lbs. of fuel in the Outer Wing Tanks that we could not access). The Captain (the Pilot Flying) and I (the Pilot Monitoring) both came to the same quick conclusion that we had enough fuel to make the airfield but if we had to go around we would have been in a bad fuel situation. At That Exact Moment in time; the Captain stated he wanted to exert his emergency authority and asked me if I would cycle the Fuel Transfer Circuit Breakers to open the Transfer Valves to which I also agreed was the best course of action given we both knew there was no QRH procedure to correct that situation. After cycling the breakers the Transfer Valves opened and the entire amount of fuel in both wings was now available.'

summary: An ATC facility experienced a long-standing debate and a specific operational conflict regarding whether VFR aircraft require formal handoffs before entering another facility's airspace. The controller noted that the ambiguity in FAA Order 7110.65 between Chapter 4 and Chapter 5 causes systemic confusion and demanded the manual be explicitly clarified.
rationale: The Manuals label is assigned because the root cause of the operational confusion is contradictory and ambiguous text within the FAA Order 7110.65 rulebook. This matches the "ATC Directives & Letters of Agreement (LOA)" repeating issue for text-based operational manuals. It is not Company Policy because the conflict stems from the national FAA textual directive itself rather than a local facility management decision. It is not Procedure because the controllers are attempting to follow the rules, but the written rules themselves are inherently contradictory.
text: Narrative 1 - 'A long standing culture is that 'nobody cares' if a VFR enters another facility's airspace without a handoff/pointout.  This has been a source of debate at this facility for over 20 years.During OJT (On Job Training) a VFR flight-following [aircraft] was approaching the receiving facility but not in handoff status. OJTI (On Job Training Instructor) told the trainee to commence it or manually coordinate the handoff. The Controller on Flight Data asked; 'Does a VFR need approval to enter center's airspace? It's VFR. There is no separation standard.' OJTI stated that Chapter 5 of the 7110.65 states that no aircraft under your control can enter another facility/sector's airspace without a handoff or pointout; and that it does not differentiate between IFR or VFR.Aircraft X departed and climbing to 17;500 feet. NAS VFR flights do not auto-handoff and the OJT was distracted by an aircraft that was telling us about an unknown aircraft following it in close proximity. Tower called down to tell Data that Aircraft X was climbing unrestricted; OJT did not know that. OJTI saw Aircraft X approaching the top of our airspace and told the trainee to hand it off or stop the climb; the aircraft climbed into center airspace before it could occur. No loss of separation with other aircraft.The 7110.65 Chapter 4 is IFR. The 7110.65 Chapter 5 is radar. Chapter 5 of the 7110.65 states that no aircraft under your control can enter another facility/sector's airspace without a handoff or pointout; and that it does not differentiate between IFR or VFR. Some controllers maintain that VFR aircraft under one's control need no handoff/pointout to enter another facility/sectors airspace.I expect this to be unambiguously clarified; briefed; and enforced.'

summary: A flight crew dispatched on an overwater flight with one engine bleed inoperative, only to realize enroute that their Flight Manual contained absolutely no non-normal procedures for the loss of the remaining bleed source. The crew noted that the MEL was excessively long and the complete lack of documented emergency descent guidance left them with severe safety questions.
rationale: This incident is classified as Manuals because the primary hazard was the glaring omission of critical emergency guidance within the aircraft's Flight Manual and an overly complex Minimum Equipment List (MEL). The lack of written text-based procedures for a dual bleed loss left the crew without a safe operational framework. It is not classified as Company Policy because the specific failure point was the missing text in the Boeing/company flight manual rather than a management dispatch pressure tactic. It is not Equipment / Tooling because the dispatch software and aircraft hardware were functioning exactly as deferred; the hazard was purely documentation-based.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Our Boeing 777 return aircraft was dispatched with the right PRSOV (Pressure-Regulating Shutoff Valve) closed and locked. The entire right side bleed source was inoperative. This left us with the left engine bleed and the APU as the backup pneumatic source for pressurization. The MEL was over 2 pages long with multiple crew actions at different phases of flight. We weighed all the risks we could think of; called the [Operations Manager] and [Maintenance Control] and decided that we had enough redundancy and a plan to cross the open water to [destination]. Our thoughts were:1. One operating engine bleed2. 2 packs operating3. APU bleed for backup4. Fuel for APU operation for entire flight5. If the APU quit; we turn around.We fueled the aircraft for a loss of the other bleed and ran the APU the entire way. If the APU quit; we would turn back. After takeoff and level off at FL350; we both realized the seriousness of the situation. We are at FL350 and the APU will not start to pressurize until 22;000 ft. [Maintenance Control] couldn't answer question as to the procedure for losing the other bleed source. Our Flight Manual does not have any non-normal for bleed loss. When and how does the APU bleed open to give us air? How long does it take to descend from FL350 to FL220; which is APU operating range? The APU bleed has to open during landing to power the ADP's (Air-Driven Pump) to raise the gear during a G/A (Go Around). When and how does this happen? The MEL says to close both bleeds in the event of system failure. That's all we had to go on for landing. If the APU bleed fails to open; do we divert? We had more questions than answers. We both realized that we should have never taken the aircraft especially over open water. Even though this is MEL and ETOPS legal it wasn't the right thing to do. To summarize the concerns:1. No non-normal for losing both bleeds.2. How long does the cabin hold pressure with no bleed air?3. Can we descend in time before the cabin runs out of air?4. What happens if the APU bleed fails to open or the isolation valves fail to isolate the center for gear retraction on a G/A?5. What keys the APU to open the bleed on landing? The MEL says APU required to be used as air source for center system. How do we do this?There needs to be a rewrite for the loss of both bleeds and how to fly with one bleed inoperative. The questions that the MEL brought up and lack of information in the Flight Manual should be fixed so we have a better understanding as to the risk environment that crews will put themselves in with this type of deferral.'

summary: During a visual approach in clear conditions, the flight crew briefed an altitude restriction to maintain 3,000 feet before passing a specific ridge line, as recommended by company pages. However, the aircraft descended early to 2,600 feet, triggering an aural terrain warning. The pilot monitoring recognized that they should have provided more active support to the captain to maintain the structural altitude restriction.
rationale: This incident is classified as Airspace Structure because it involves an altitude deviation directly related to the physical terrain (ridge line) and the structural constraints of the approach path. This aligns with the category's focus on altitude deviations and terrain clearance limits inherent to the airspace design. It is not classified as Company Policy, despite the mention of company pages, because the root hazard is the physical interaction between the aircraft's path and the geographic terrain structure. It is also not Procedure because the crew did not intentionally violate an ATC directive, but rather drifted below the structural terrain boundary.
text: Narrative 1 - 'It was our go-home day and it happened on the last leg which was ZZZ1-ZZZ flight. Flight to ZZZ was delayed because the aircraft was over fueled. By the time we were ready for take off; we already briefed that we are going to land rwy XX at ZZZ. Under calm wind; VMC; we finally took off from ZZZ1 and began our flight to ZZZ. I was the pilot monitoring so I listened the ATIS and figured ZZZ is advertising rwy XY so I launched rwy XY ILS into the system and gave a visual approach brief for rwy XY to the captain. Weather at ZZZ at that time was absolutely clear and calm condition. We also briefed the company page saying that it is recommended to maintain 3000 ft before passing the ridge line however we went down to 2600 and got aural warning. As a pilot monitoring; I should have assisted the captain by reminding her that we should keep 3000 ft until passing the ridge line. Throughout this event; I figured that I'll have to pay more attention to what's written in the company page and reminded myself to become more supportive as a pilot monitoring.'

summary: A pilot flying an IFR approach cancelled IFR to fly a visual approach due to a low marine layer obscuring the uncontrolled airport. Another aircraft, likely a student pilot flying an illegal VFR pattern due to the marine layer ceiling, cut off the reporter on short final, forcing the reporter to execute a 360-degree turn to avoid a collision.
rationale: This report fits Airspace Structure because it details a traffic pattern conflict and a near mid-air collision (NMAC) at a non-towered airport. The reference guide explicitly lists "Traffic pattern conflicts / NMACs — near mid-air collisions from poor traffic pattern design at non-towered airports" as a key repeating issue. It is not classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because the conflict arose from structural traffic pattern overlap rather than an external object like a drone or bird. It is also not Human Factors, as the primary focus is the structural vulnerability of the uncontrolled pattern exacerbated by the overlying Class E airspace boundaries.
text: Narrative 1 - 'At the time; SMO was under about a 1;000 foot MSL marine layer (i.e. low cloud stratus layer) extending from the west to just east and encompassing both north and south of the runway. I was flying under an IFR flight plan and had already been cleared for the approach. Because the Tower was closed and because I could see that I could see the airport (coming from the east looking at Runway 21) I cancelled IFR and continued inbound on the visual making appropriate calls on the CTAF. As I got to a 1 mile final (which was called out on CTAF); Aircraft Y began turning base to final and asked where I actually was as he did not have me in sight. I advised him that I was at his 2 o'clock. It would appear that he never had me in sight as he continued his turn to final. He ended cutting me off ending up just left and about 50 feet above/in front of me. I informed him that he had cut me off; but he continued on course and descending. Because of the situation; I decided the best course to avoid a collision was to make a right turn. I also noted that the marine layer cloud edge extended out to the position where I made the turn (3/4 mile final) I then proceeded to continue that right turn into a 360 to rejoin the final and then landed. It is notable that because of the marine layer; the likely student pilot in the Aircraft Y; should never have even been flying the 'standard' pattern' (i.e. left traffic 21) as it was not legal to do so because the VFR requirements for the overlying Echo airspace could not be met (i.e. 1;000 feet above the clouds) due to the height of the marine layer. Thus; his descent to turn base at around the freeway clearly violated VFR minimums. I did inform the person who runs the school of the situation. He informed me that he himself had an NMAC just the very day before. Had the Tower been operating on normal or slightly longer hours at the time; such incidents would not have been able to occur. The currency reduced Tower hours should be extended ASAP; in order to prevent an accident as the airspace is very busy; and can be challenging in the event of a marine layer; which precludes the use of the pattern.'

summary: An airliner deviated from its assigned RNAV arrival track and subsequently descended below the Minimum Vectoring Altitude (MVA). The controller noticed the deviation, issued a corrective vector, and queried the pilot to ensure they could maintain their own terrain and obstruction clearance. The pilot later apologized for the navigational error that led to the MVA violation.
rationale: The classification is Airspace Structure because the event centers on an MVA (Minimum Vectoring Altitude) violation related to the RNAV STAR procedure's structural boundaries. The reference explicitly lists "MVA / Minimum Vectoring Altitude violations" and altitude deviations connected to SID/STAR structure under this category. It is not Procedure because the central hazard documented by the controller is the aircraft's physical presence below the structural terrain clearance boundary. It is also distinct from Chart Or Publication because the RNAV chart itself was not alleged to be incorrect.
text: Narrative 1 - 'A B737 came over on the RNAV arrival. I was working my other aircraft when the B737 called to ask for clarification on what he was supposed to be doing. At that moment; it was clear the B737 was off the ground track for the RNAV arrival. It wasn't clear at the time if it was his clearance for the arrival that confused him or what. I began to give him a turn when I realized he had already descended below the MVA (Minimum Vectoring Altitude). In the same transmission; I asked if he can maintain his own terrain and obstruction clearance. He said he could; so I gave him a vector and altitude he currently was at to maintain. An hour or so the pilot called to apologize; and say it was their mistake they had come off course from the RNAV STAR.'

summary: While performing air work in an assigned altitude block of 10,000 to 17,000 feet, a flight crew received an ADS-B traffic warning. The crew visually acquired an airliner slightly below their altitude and took evasive action by rolling wings level and climbing to avoid a mid-air collision.
rationale: This is classified as Airspace Structure because it involves a near mid-air collision (NMAC) and traffic conflict between an aircraft operating in a designated practice block and an airliner. This perfectly aligns with the "Airspace design issues at boundaries" and general NMAC themes, where structural airspace layout creates converging traffic hazards. It is not classified as ATC Equipment because the ADS-B functioned properly to warn the crew. Furthermore, it is not Procedure because both aircraft were presumably operating legally within their respective structural airspace assignments.
text: Narrative 1 - 'We were performing air work southeast of ZZZ in the 10;000 to 17;000 of altitude block. I had just given trainee nose high unusual attitude when we received traffic warning from ADS-B in/out avionics installed in aircraft. I looked out left side of aircraft (direction of traffic warning) observed airliner slightly below our altitude. I took control of aircraft and rolled wings level and began a climb to avoid airliner.'

summary: An IFR aircraft was routed directly through an active jump zone, which is a recurring safety issue reported by the controller. Although a Letter of Agreement (LOA) requires the TRACON to deliver aircraft on a heading parallel to the airway to avoid this hazard, the adjacent sector repeatedly routes aircraft through the dangerous airspace.
rationale: This is Airspace Structure because the core issue is an airspace design conflict where an active IFR route structurally intersects a special use airspace (jump zone). This exactly matches the "TFR structure / special use airspace conflicts" and "Airspace design issues at boundaries" listed in the reference guide. It is not classified as Manuals, despite the LOA mention, because the physical hazard is the geographic layout of the airspace itself. It is also not ATC Equipment because there is no mention of radar or automation failure causing the routing.
text: Narrative 1 - '[Aircraft X] was filed IFR. The route would go directly through a jump zone. We have in our Letter Of Agreement with TRACON that aircraft delivered via the fix shall be on a heading to parallel the airway(north bound). This is a situation that has been reported on several occasions before and hasn't been corrected. This is dangerous and will lead to loss of life if not addressed.[Recommend that] TRACON controllers are trained and reminded of the airspace and procedures required to their adjacent airspace.'

summary: While performing night currency at a towered airport, ATC extended a pilot's left downwind leg, putting the aircraft directly on a collision course with a 6,178-foot unlit hill. The pilot unilaterally turned to avoid the terrain, noting that the extended downwind over the obstacle constitutes a serious hazard for training flights at night.
rationale: This is classified as Airspace Structure because the established traffic pattern and ATC vectoring structurally conflict with rising terrain, endangering the aircraft. The structural design of the pattern extension puts aircraft in physical jeopardy, aligning with the category's focus on "MVA violations" and structural terrain conflicts. It is not ATC Equipment or Airport because the hazard is the geographic terrain relative to the airspace boundaries, not airport pavement or a broken radar. It is not Human Factors because the pilot successfully diagnosed and escaped the structurally flawed vector.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I was performing my night currency and on the last of 3 stop and goes. ZZZ Runway XX was in use. A few miles out directly in the approach path to Runway XY lies the unlit obstacle; listed at 6178 feet on the sectional. On left downwind; ATC extended my downwind. At TPA; I would have collided with the hill as I could not differentiate it from the inky black night sky. I patiently waited for ATC to call my base. When they did not; I turned left approximately 30 degrees and advised ATC I was turning to avoid terrain. ATC instructed me to return to original heading and to climb if necessary to avoid terrain. I complied and climbed to 7000 ft. to make sure I had adequate clearance.I think this obstacle is a hazard in the approach path of runway at ZZZ and that ATC should refrain from using it at night. I believe that in that particular situation I made the right call to deviate laterally rather than rely on my climb performance so close to the terrain. ZZZ is a primary training airport and many new pilots train there. The existence of extended downwinds over obstacle is a serious hazard at night and that the ATC should be more cognizant of the risks of mountain flying in and around rising terrain.'

summary: During an instrument departure, a flight crew received multiple traffic advisories and a TCAS RA to descend while they were in a steep climb, forcing them to arrest their climb to avoid another aircraft. The evasive maneuvers and ATC queries caused the crew to become distracted, leading to a minor speed violation above the SID's 250-knot restriction.
rationale: This fits Airspace Structure because the primary triggering event was a TCAS RA caused by conflicting traffic in structurally dense airspace during a Standard Instrument Departure (SID). This matches the "TCAS / Resolution Advisory (RA) events" and "VFR/IFR conflicts from airspace design" criteria. Although there are human factor elements regarding distraction and speed, the root hazard requiring immediate intervention was the structural traffic conflict. It is not Procedure because the speed deviation was secondary to the structural RA compliance.
text:
Narrative 1 - 'While climbing out on the SID; the Captain noted TCAS traffic to me at our 12 o'clock and about five miles. Immediately after; Tower handed us off to TRACON; and issued a left turn 190 for traffic. We turned; and while turning I picked up the general aviation aircraft visually and called traffic visually to ATC. Shortly after; we were clear of traffic [and] ATC asked us to turn to a 270 heading and issued a climb. He also issued us another traffic advisory of a twin aircraft moving past our 1 o'clock toward our 3 o'clock position and behind us. He stated they would not be a conflict. We also picked up this traffic visually.We determined that our course would keep us clear of traffic conflicts and that our climb rate was more than sufficient to allow us to out climb traffic. Seconds later; however; the TCAS issued an RA to monitor vertical speed and quickly issued a 'Descend' prompt. At the point that we received the RA our Vertical Speed rate of climb was high which caused the RA to go to a 'Descend' prompt quickly. The Captain immediately responded to the RA arresting the rate of climb and commencing a descent and I called the traffic out to him in sight. Seconds later; the RA resolved and we were clear of conflict. During that moment; I notified ATC that we were responding to an RA. The scenario was actually quite counter intuitive because we had traffic in sight and we had determined that our climb rate was the safest course of action; however; moments later the TCAS RA overruled our plan to remain clear and required us to take immediate action in order to comply; requiring us to arrest the climb and descend.Once clear of traffic and conflict; ATC questioned if Tower had advised us of traffic before issuing takeoff clearance; which Tower had not. Their query was somewhat distracting as we were trying to get back into the Green on the SID after responding to an RA. While we had briefed that; we would maintain 250 knots until advised by ATC; these multiple disruptions and distractions threw things off a little. After our level off at 13;000 feet and new issued higher altitude; I believe the Captain selected VNAV to climb; which would normally be fine; however; at that point the aircraft accelerated to a profile airspeed faster than 250 knots. When we checked in with the next Controller; he issued normal speed. Upon being issued normal speed; we both realized that we had been distracted from our previous briefing to maintain 250 until advised. ATC didn't make verbal notice of our airspeed being above 250 knots and there were no further issues.In the future when required by the SID; I will set 250 knots into the FMS Climb page so that we can fly in profile at 250 knots rather than having to remember to Speed Intervene and set 250 knots. It works fine when things are normal; however; under these distracting scenarios it would be a more thorough practice.'
Narrative 2 - 'On the instrument Departure; we had just rolled out on a 210 heading when I noticed an aircraft on TCAS about 4 miles ahead and 1000 feet above us. The Departure Controller directed us to turn to 190 heading and climb to 13;000 feet. We complied and when clear of the first traffic conflict; we were directed to turn to heading of 270. ATC then pointed out traffic at 12 moving to 1 o'clock. We were visually clear of the traffic and had established a climb when we received a TCAS RA to descend. We began a descent and the TCAS gave us a 'Clear of Conflict'. We were then turned north to continue the departure. On a side note; at 13;000 feet we were given further climb to 23;000 feet. The limit on the SID was 250 knots; which we complied with in the climb to 13;000 feet. I engaged the VNAV and the aircraft accelerated to normal climb speed. ATC gave us normal speed and we continued without further incident. In the future; I will program any climb limit speeds assigned by the SID.'

summary: A VFR pilot attempting to climb through overlying Class C and Class D airspace could not establish contact with busy TRACON controllers. To avoid a near mid-air collision with converging traffic, the pilot yielded the right-of-way and inadvertently entered a Class D surface area without communication, resulting in a possible pilot deviation.
rationale: This is Airspace Structure because the pilot was squeezed between multiple complex airspace boundaries (Class C, multiple Class Ds, and a Class B shelf) while avoiding VFR traffic. This structurally induced bottleneck directly aligns with "Airspace design issues at boundaries" and "VFR/IFR conflicts from airspace design" from the reference guide. It is not classified as Staffing, even though the controllers were busy, because the underlying hazard is the highly compressed geographic design of the airspace. It is not Procedure because the pilot was forced into the deviation by structural conflict avoidance.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I departed AJO straight out on a VFR flight in a Cessna 310. After 2 miles I turned northwest on course and away from rising terrain.
