With Ontario Class C airspace above me and Chino Class D to the north; I attempted contact with SoCal Approach on 135.4 seeking to climb above 2;500 MSL through both airport's airspace. I called SoCal approach 3 times unsuccessfully. They were busy and didn't acknowledge any of my calls. My Garmin 430 indicated multiple traffic contacts in the vicinity which kept my focus outside. The most pressing contact was at 12 o'clock; westbound within 2 miles and 500 feet. Once I gained visual contact it became apparent that we were converging. The contact appeared to be a Cessna high-wing (possibly departing Chino) in the climb out at a much lower airspeed. That aircraft had the right-of-way since it was on my right. I elected to turn behind it to avoid closer contact.The turn put me in edge of the southwest sector of Chino's Class D airspace as I was informed later. Not having any luck raising SoCal Approach on 135.4; I quickly switched to 135.05; the first frequency I found on the Sectional Chart with a quick glance. I was able to make radio contact with SoCal on 135.05 on the first or second attempt. Once checked in with a squawk code and was identified; I was issued multiple traffic advisories and limited climbs. Within the first two minutes I was issued 4 different traffic advisories. My course to avoid Brackett and El Monte Class D airspace took me towards the congested practice area; the source of a few of the traffic advisories. During the multiple traffic advisories SoCal informed me of the possible Pilot Deviation and requested I copy the number for Chino tower. Obviously this was a bad time to be heads-down in the cockpit. I copied the number and eventually read it back. I was eventually approved for a climb out of the low-level area.I contacted Chino tower with the number provided and they indicated the information would be forwarded to the local FSDO and I might be contacted for follow-up.I radioed to SoCal that I had traffic in sight only to be informed of other targets close by. My decision to yield right-of-way took me into the Class D airspace and I chose to attempt contact with the TRACON in an effort to climb through Class C and D airspace instead of getting stuck hopping from Class D tower to tower in low level (Class D) congestion. At the time it was more important to keep my head outside instead of inside the airplane looking for Class D tower frequencies of Chino; Brackett; and El Monte.My flights to the central valley of California on a Northwest course sometimes feel like trying to thread a needle through airspace with Ontario's Class C above; Chino's Class D immediately to the north; and Los Angeles's Class B shelves nearby to the west. I'm normally able to get established with TRACON quickly but it was a busy period for the controllers and they didn't have time for me. The focus on avoiding multiple traffic contacts and observing right-of-way rules put me into a tough situation. My attempts at radio contact were initially unsuccessful which caused an airspace violation.'

summary: An aircraft being vectored to intercept a localizer shot through the final approach course and was observed descending into the 110-foot Minimum Vectoring Altitude (MVA). Despite an instructor taking over and issuing a correction heading, the aircraft was allowed to continue descending below the MVA.
rationale: This is classified as Airspace Structure because the core safety event is a descent below the Minimum Vectoring Altitude (MVA) during an approach intercept. This maps exactly to the "MVA / Minimum Vectoring Altitude violations" repeating issue specifically delineated for this label. It is not Procedure because the focus is strictly on the structural minimum altitude boundary rather than a specific SOP violation. It is not Human Factors because the structural layout of the MVA is the focal point of the controller's safety report.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Aircraft X was on a vector to intercept the runway 17 localizer. They were issued a 140 heading to join. Aircraft X shot through and was seen descending into the 110 foot minimum vectoring altitude. The instructor took over and issued a heading to reintercept. Aircraft X reported in a 30 degree bank. The aircraft was still allowed to continue descending below the MVA.'

summary: While being vectored to intercept an ILS approach and descending to 3,000 feet, a flight crew received a TCAS RA to climb due to rapidly converging, unobserved traffic. The First Officer executed a steep 45-50 degree bank and climbed to avoid the target, which passed within 200 feet, causing an altitude deviation before the crew successfully resumed the approach.
rationale: This report is classified as Airspace Structure because the primary hazard was a near mid-air collision and subsequent TCAS RA triggered by conflicting traffic during an approach vector. This fits precisely within the "TCAS / Resolution Advisory (RA) events" and "VFR/IFR conflicts from airspace design" sub-categories. It is not Procedure because the conflict arose from the structural overlapping of traffic paths, not from a crew's intentional rule violation. It is also not Human Factors as the crew executed a successful escape maneuver without committing an error.
text:
Narrative 1 - 'We had a near miss on approach!  Flying a Westerly heading the Approach Controller told us to 'turn right 010; intercept the localizer; descend maintain 3;000 feet until established cleared ILS'.  In our turn and descending; the Approach Controller came on frequency and said; 'TRAFFIC eleven o'clock climbing'!  [While] looking for the traffic; the target turned red on our TCAS. First Officer disconnected the autopilot autothrottles and started a climbing right turn.[While starting] the maneuver; we got an RA.  The closure rate of the target was so fast and not being able to obtain a visual I told First Officer to 'Increase Bank; Increase Bank; Increase Bank'!  He rolled the aircraft in to a 45-50 degree bank!  Not obtaining visual and with the azimuth on the Navigational Display it is hard to judge just how close mileage wise (it was close) the target came to us; but TCAS said it came within 200 feet!  The whole episode lasted about 10 seconds!Once the RA was over and we were 'Clear of Conflict' we descended to our assigned altitude turned left joined the localizer; made the approach and landing without further incident!   In debriefing we were in agreement the other aircraft must have been fast as the closure rate was very fast.  We also believe the other aircraft took off as satellite airport; which is just west of the approach course. For the Controller not to instruct us to descend 3;000 feet and join the localizer when he was advised we were flying an RA!'
Narrative 2 - 'On base leg [while] receiving vectors from approach control in visual conditions; we were issued at TA by TCAS. My best guess is that the initial TA was inside 5 miles and at our 12 O'clock position. It was also in a climb about 500 feet below us. The next ATC call was to maintain 3;000; a turn to intercept the localizer; an approach clearance; and was immediately followed by a traffic advisory about this target. Almost simultaneously; we received an RA to climb at this time the target was at a mile or likely less; and showing 200 feet below and climbing. I disengaged automation and pitched to the TCAS recommendations; well as banking steeper than normal to adhere to the RA. This resulted in an altitude deviation that may have moved us up to 3;400 feet. At this point; ATC replied for us to maintain 3;000; our reply was we were complying with an RA. We never saw the target visually; but were advised it had passed behind us and we were clear of it. This entire event happened at the end of a very long day; and it happened remarkably fast.'

summary: Trees are obstructing the Tower's view of the ramp, creating a visual blind spot for the controllers. The reporter requests the removal of the pine trees to eliminate the need for additional radio transmissions to verify aircraft locations.
rationale: This is classified as Airport because the core issue is the physical landscaping and obstruction of the airport environment. It aligns directly with the "Inadequate clearance / obstruction" repeating issue, where vegetation blocks vital sightlines. It is not classified as ATC Equipment because the facility's radar and radio systems are functioning correctly; the problem is strictly environmental landscaping. Furthermore, it is not Airspace Structure as the issue is limited to the physical surface observation of the ramp, not flight paths.
text: Narrative 1 - 'There are trees that block the view of the ramp. This creates a visual blind spot for the Tower. This hazard leads to additional transmissions to ascertain aircraft location that could be eliminated if the Tower could see the entire ramp. Please remove the pine trees to provide an unobstructed view of the ramp.'

summary: An aircraft being pushed back encroached on an active taxiway because the stop spot and hold lines were not visible from the cockpit. The pilot noted that the taxi lane layout and painted ground markings were extremely confusing, poorly positioned, and not properly depicted on the airport charts.
rationale: The Airport label applies because the primary safety hazard was confusing, unmarked, or poorly visible ground markings and taxi lanes on the airport surface. This fits perfectly with the "Faded or missing runway/taxiway markings" and "Confusing or absent signage" repeating issues. While the pilot mentions the charts were confusing, the root problem encountered during the live operation was the physical layout and lack of visible hold short lines on the pavement itself. It is not Equipment / Tooling because the tug driver did not experience a mechanical failure, but was simply navigating a poorly marked ramp.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I have not been to MIA for many years except for a few times recently and not in the X terminal pocket at all. We were pushed from gate X to spot Y. When the tug turned us loose all I saw was spot Y still ahead of us. Ground control told us to wait for an aircraft to pass; then taxi on taxiway P to [Runway] 8R. I thought it was prudent to continue to spot Y while we wait for the aircraft to pass. On the way to Y I noticed we were encroaching on taxiway P. I stopped to let the other aircraft pass. I did not understand how I could have gotten to close to taxiway P while still short of spot Y.  I was using the 10-9 page and did not see an error. Upon further review using 10-9 page I saw there are three taxi lanes that are only depicted on this page. Spot Y lane to the North; spot Z lane to the South and one in the middle that does not lead to either spot. There is a large Y painted on the ground further out that is not depicted on any chart. When the tug turned us loose I believe we were stopped on the middle taxi lane very close to the hold line. The hold line and the close in spot number were not visible from where the tug stopped us. Neither of us saw them. The Y painted further out is the only one visible from my vantage point. If not familiar it is a very confusing situation. Even though I reviewed terminal and taxi charts this still happened. Next time into Miami I will be more aware of ramp layout.[Suggest] depicting the hold line on 10-9 chart and tug driver not stopping aircraft so close to hold line that it cannot be seen in wide body aircraft.'

summary: During pushback from the gate, an aircraft's left wing struck a building, causing a loud noise and breaking off a piece of the wingtip. The aircraft was returned to the gate, the passengers were safely deplaned, and a replacement aircraft was assigned for the flight.
rationale: This is classified as Airport because the incident involves an aircraft striking a physical piece of airport infrastructure (a building) due to inadequate clearance. This directly corresponds to the "Inadequate clearance / obstruction" sub-category where ramp congestion or layout creates a collision hazard. It is not Equipment / Tooling because there is no indication that the tug itself broke or malfunctioned. It is not Human Factors because the report highlights the physical collision within the airport's layout rather than detailing a specific crew distraction or cognitive error.
text: Narrative 1 - 'During pushback I heard a loud noise then the plane stopped. I looked out the aft door window from my jump seat to see that the left wing had hit a building. The wingtip was damaged and a piece on the ground. No passengers or crew were hurt. The Captain announced what happened and that he would provide more info once he knew more. After several minutes we returned to the gate and deplaned passengers. We were given a new gate and plane and eventually took off for ZZZ1.'

summary: While executing a VMC go-around due to converging traffic at a non-towered airport, a flight crew climbed to 1,500 feet AGL to maintain what they believed was standard turbine pattern altitude, inadvertently encroaching on the overlying Class C airspace. After landing safely, the crew discovered the A/FD mandated a non-standard 1,000-foot AGL pattern altitude for all aircraft, which conflicted with their expectations.
rationale: Based on the updated reference guide logic, this is classified as Airport because the conflict stemmed directly from non-standard, airport-specific traffic pattern altitudes published for that specific field. The unique local traffic pattern rules for this environment created the hazard when the crew applied general rules of thumb. It is not classified as Airspace Structure because the overarching Class C structural boundaries were correct, but the crew was unaware of the localized airport pattern altitude requirement. It is not Procedure because the crew was actively attempting to avoid a collision rather than willfully disregarding a known rule.
text:
Narrative 1 - 'I am pilot in command typed in the HA-420 - second in command required - and hired as a second in command at my company. Today I was pilot flying and there was a company pilot in command who was pilot monitoring. On a no passenger; reposition flight from ZZZ1 to ZZZ we conducted a go-around due to a traffic conflict after cancelling IFR and potentially encroached into the ZZZ2 Class C airspace. After being cleared for the VOR/DME XX approach and the field was in sight; we cancelled IFR and continued VFR to ZZZ.We made two radio calls prior to being on a 5-mile final; noticing there were 2 other aircraft in the left downwind for Runway XX. Noting that no one was on base or final we elected to continue down and attempt to land. Soon after; the number 1 aircraft in the downwind; a Caravan; announced and turned base. At this point we were approximately 1300 ft. MSL / 1200 ft. AGL and on about a 3-mile final. Seeing the traffic conflict; I smoothly added power and climbed to a safe altitude away from traffic and normal turbine powered aircraft pattern altitude of 1600 ft. MSL / 1500 ft. AGL to join the left pattern in the upwind. The traffic passed underneath by approximately 600 ft. and landed. Once clear of conflict; we cleaned up the airplane and it was time to turn crosswind. During this time we both remembered the ZZZ2 C airspace was nearby and I asked for the moving map to be zoomed out as to check our position. It was in this moment we realized the Class C airspace began at 1600 ft. MSL. We had already drifted below 1600 ft. MSL and remained at 1500 ft. MSL until ready to descend for landing. After landing we noticed in the airport A/FD Chart Supplement the pattern altitude for all aircraft. I realize we potentially violated the Class C airspace at ZZZ2. I also realize that traffic pattern altitude for all aircraft may be different depending on airport needs. To prevent this in the future it would be wise to; on the approach brief; also brief how to enter the traffic pattern into an airport when going IFR to VFR; especially at non-towered airports. It would also be wise to brief how a VMC go-around would differ from the missed approach instructions for that approach.'
Narrative 2 - 'Utilized VOR XX approach at ZZZ. Approach was uneventful and was largely conducted under VMC conditions.Cancelled IFR approximately 8 NM southeast of the airport and had planned a straight-in landing on [Runway] XX subject to traffic. As we got closer to the airport it became apparent there were multiple aircraft in the pattern. On about a 3-mile final an aircraft cut in close to do a tight base leg to final in front of us and we were forced to go around.Turbine aircraft generally use 1500 ft. AGL in the traffic pattern so we climbed straight ahead to that altitude; which turns out to be 1600 ft. MSL. We simply remained in a left-hand pattern and landed on the next attempt.After landing the crew reviewed the A/FD entry for ZZZ; and discovered that the pattern altitude for ALL aircraft is 1000 ft. AGL. We would not have flown that low with another aircraft passing underneath us. However; if I had pre-briefed the VFR missed approach more thoroughly we would have realized at least that the base of the ZZZ1 Class C airspace was at 1600 ft. MSL and after resolving the potential traffic conflict we would've descended below that.We were both under the impression that turbine aircraft must always use 1500 ft. AGL as the pattern altitude; and there is an FAA Advisory Circular that says just that. However the regulation in Part 91 which stipulates it only mandates it for aircraft landing in Class B; C; and D airspace. NOT Class E airports; which is the airspace surrounding ZZZ. We did not climb above 1600 ft. MSL at anytime during the event.'

summary: While taxiing at night, a flight crew had to make an abrupt stop to avoid colliding with a passenger bus that crossed the ramp directly in front of their moving aircraft. Both the crew and the ramp controller were surprised by the bus's unexpected movement, highlighting the need for better ground vehicle control.
rationale: This is classified as Airport because the central issue is a conflict with a ground vehicle operating unsafely on the airport ramp. This maps perfectly to the "Ground vehicle conflicts" repeating issue where vehicles fail to yield or cross in front of taxiing aircraft. It is not classified as Human Factors because the focus of the report is on the systemic hazard of the airport surface environment and vehicle control, rather than a pilot's cognitive error. It is also not Procedure because the narrative focuses on the physical presence of the hazard rather than documenting a specific company protocol violation.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Clear night; approaching the ramp from the north and observed a passenger bus from the new terminal cross the ramp in front of our taxiing aircraft. We made the abrupt stop safely. Ramp Control was as stunned as us the bus crossed in front of our moving aircraft. Better ramp/bus control needs to be addressed or someone will get hurt or metal will be bent.'

summary: A pilot reported that bright LED lights mounted on a new jetway blinded them while taxiing into the gate, making it exceedingly difficult to see the marshaller. The pilot recommended shielding the lights, aiming them away from the cockpit, or returning to lower-lumen bulbs to prevent blinding flight crews.
rationale: The Airport classification is appropriate as the hazard is caused by physical airport infrastructure (jetway lighting) creating a visually unsafe operational environment on the ramp. This fits perfectly within the scope of "Poor airport lighting" or lighting hazards detailed in the reference guide. It is not Equipment / Tooling because the light is a fixed, permanent part of the airport gate infrastructure, not a malfunctioning mobile ground support tool. It is not Environment - Non Weather Related because the light source is a deliberate installation of the airport, not an external factor like a billboard or laser.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Bright lights shining in pilot's eyes while taxiing on the ramps and into gates have been increasing system wide however; newer bright lights on the jetway for a gate in PHX reached a new level. The bright light bulbs on the jetway next to the marshaller; made it challenging to watch the marshaller and became worse approaching the jetway as I took steps to block the lights from shining in my eyes. Lights should not shine into pilot's eyes any more then taxi lights should shine in the marshaller's eyes. It appears to me that ground operations do not recognize lights that shine into pilot's eyes. I discussed the lights with with the jetway driver afterwards and she said this gate has a new jetway and the lights are required to be on to operate the jetway. Turn off jetway lights while the aircraft taxi's into the gate. Shield and aim bulbs so that they do not shine into pilots eyes. Return to lower lumen bulbs previously used.'

summary: While taxiing to the runway for departure, a flight crew inadvertently entered the runway approach area due to missing or faded hold short bars and a confusing diagonal taxiway layout. The crew realized the error, held short of the runway alignment, and safely departed after noting the lack of clear surface markings and "hot spot" designations.
rationale: This report falls under Airport because the primary cause of the runway incursion hazard was missing or faded hold short markings and a confusing taxiway layout on the pavement. This matches the "Faded or missing runway/taxiway markings" and "Confusing or absent signage" repeating issues explicitly. It is not Chart Or Publication because the core problem was the physical lack of paint and signs on the pavement where the crew was actively operating. It is not Human Factors because the crew was actively looking for the markings that were simply absent from the airport surface.
text: Narrative 1 - 'At Hollister airport; we were taxiing from the FBO / apron to Runway 31 for run up and departure. My memory of Hollister Airport is there was a long displaced threshold for Runway 31; so I intended to use that entire displaced threshold distance for takeoff (and for demonstrating a soft field takeoff). We taxied from the apron via the apparently-unnamed diagonal taxiway. Eventually that taxiway intersects taxiway A; then turns and becomes a taxiway in the approach area of Runway 31; aligned with the extended runway centerline. Either the hold short bars do not exist or are worn or faded; or in any case we did not see them; and as we continued to taxi realized there was no displaced threshold; and the taxiway was actually in the approach area of Runway 31. We stopped before making the turn to align with the runway; did a quick pre-takeoff check; checked for traffic and communicated on the CTAF; taxied to the threshold; and departed; but it seems ultimately we should have been holding short of the approach area at some other point that we never saw and had inadvertently taxied into the approach area. Although we stayed outside the area aligned with the runway; this presents a hazard when an aircraft is on final; and indeed an aircraft turned final over us as we performed pre-takeoff check (in my estimation with sufficient separation since it was landing well downfield beyond the threshold of Runway 31). There is no mention of this odd configuration in the chart supplement; although the notes do say takeoff is recommended beyond Taxiway B. There are no NOTAMs relating to these taxiways; and there is no FAA airport diagram and no hot spots defined for the airport. I feel that this area should be a hot spot given its confusing configuration and the risk of runway incursions; and it should be confirmed that the hold short bars for 31 / APCH are clearly visible from all areas where an aircraft could taxi; and/or that closed taxiways are clearly marked.'

summary: A pilot reported that a specific set of taxiways lacked necessary ground markings and signs, making it very difficult to navigate the surface at night. Additionally, the green taxiway centerline lights did not adequately mark the turn.
rationale: This is assigned the Airport label because the core issue is the complete physical absence of necessary surface markings, signs, and inadequate lighting for taxiways. This aligns exactly with the "Faded or missing runway/taxiway markings" and "Poor airport lighting" repeating issues. It is not Chart Or Publication because the deficiency is purely in the physical airport environment, not a charting error on an EFB. It is not ATC Equipment because surface paint and signage are the responsibility of airport operations, not the air traffic control facility.
text: Narrative 1 - 'There are no ground markings or signs to mark these taxiways making night taxi difficult. In addition; the green taxi center lights do not mark the turn.No markings to show M1; M2; N1; 2; 3 from ramp side on W.'

summary: An aircraft taxiing at a very slow speed lost traction and slid sideways on a taxiway that had been swept of snow but not sanded, revealing an underlying sheet of ice. The aircraft slightly exited the taxiway into the grass but sustained no damage and was towed to the FBO for a successful inspection.
rationale: This is classified as Airport because the root cause was the physical condition of the airport surface, specifically untreated ice on a taxiway. This maps directly to the "FOD and surface condition" repeating issue, which explicitly includes black ice on taxiways causing aircraft to slide. It is not Weather because the active meteorological event (snowfall) had passed; the hazard was the residual, improperly treated surface condition managed by airport ground operations. It is not Aircraft because the slide was caused by external physics, not a failure of the aircraft's internal braking or steering hardware.
text:
Narrative 1 - 'After a non-eventful landing at BED started our taxi. Made the turn from a snow covered taxiway onto a taxiway that had just been swept but not sanded; all 3 wheels lost traction simultaneously and we began to slide sideways to the edge of the taxiway. Only one main (right) and the nose-wheel barely entered the snow and the left main was still on the taxiway. Due to the existing conditions the rate of taxi was slower than normal which prevented the aircraft from sliding at a fast rate or traveling far resulting in zero damage to the aircraft. [Ground crew] was able to successfully tow the aircraft out and to the FBO where a Mechanic did a thorough inspection which verified no damage and the aircraft was returned to duty. I believe the sweeping of the taxiway revealed the underlying ice sheet; had it been sanded or not swept at all I believe the incident could have been avoided.'
Narrative 2 - 'Taxiing in at BED went from snow covered taxiway to one ice covered that had been broomed but not sanded. We were taxiing very slowly; but in the turn went into a slow slide into grass. No damage was done as only one main was in snow bank;and nose was just off taxiway. [Ground personnel] came out and pulled us out. We had mechanic inspect everything; and we were on our way the next day.'

summary: A pilot taxiing to the runway was informed by Ground Control that they had entered a controlled movement area and might be subject to a pilot deviation. Upon further inspection, the pilot verified that there were absolutely no taxi hold lines or signs painted on the ground to indicate the boundary of the movement area.
rationale: The Airport label fits perfectly as the inciting problem was the physical absence of painted hold lines or signage to demarcate a controlled movement area. This aligns directly with the "Confusing or absent signage" and "Faded or missing runway/taxiway markings" criteria. It is not Procedure because the pilot could not adhere to a hold short instruction for a boundary that did not physically exist on the airport surface. It is not Human Factors because the error was induced entirely by the lack of infrastructure rather than a pilot's failure to observe existing markings.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I was taxiing from a ramp area to the runway; I was calling the Ground Controller and watching for a taxi hold line. There is no taxi hold lines painted on the area nor any taxi signs present in this area. The Ground Controller began calling [another] aircraft instead of responding to my initial call and began to tell me that I had entered a controlled movement area and needed to call the Tower on the phone. I copied the number and called them from the cockpit on my phone as soon as I could taxi clear of the movement area. He told me of the possible pilot deviation. I have since checked the area on the ground and from aerial photos and airport diagrams. There are no taxi hold lines or signs present in the area as depicted in the AIM or airport diagram legend. There was no other traffic on the taxiway and no traffic conflict occurred.'

summary: While taxiing, a pilot noticed that an airport sign depicting a turn onto another taxiway remained incorrectly illustrated as a 135-degree turn instead of the actual 45-degree turn. The pilot expressed frustration that airport personnel had not corrected the known physical error despite multiple previous reports.
rationale: This is classified as Airport because the core issue is the physical presence of incorrect signage on the airport surface. This maps directly to the "Confusing or absent signage" repeating issue detailed in the reference guide. It is not classified as Chart Or Publication because the error is painted on a physical sign on the field, not within a navigation database or EFB chart. It is not Human Factors because the pilot correctly identified the hazard rather than committing an operational error.
text: Narrative 1 - 'While taxiing E on H from Z; I noticed that the airport signage was still incorrect. The sign showing the direction of turn from J onto SS is incorrectly depicted as a 135 degree turn; instead of the actual 45 degree turn necessary. Airport or city personnel's failure to correct this error in a timely manner; since I first reported the issue to Tower on [date 1]; and again on [date 2]. Figure out who is responsible for correcting the error; and implement a more proactive and timely method for correcting the error.'

summary: A flight crew arriving at an airport was informed by approach control that the main runway was closed for painting, despite no NOTAM being issued for the preplanned closure. The controller advised that it was the airport's policy not to issue NOTAMs for this type of closure, forcing the crew to quickly reconfigure for a different runway.
rationale: Based on the updated reference guide logic, this is classified as Airport because the root cause is the local airport management's explicit policy to not issue NOTAMs for preplanned surface closures. This administrative failure at the airport level creates an ongoing operational hazard for inbound crews. It is not classified as Chart Or Publication because this wasn't a simple charting omission, but a deliberate localized operational practice by the airport authority. It is not Procedure because the crew managed the last-minute change successfully without violating SOPs.
text: Narrative 1 - 'We were operating the flight to MLI under day VFR conditions.  The ATIS indicated that runway 9 was in use; but since the winds were light I asked the FO/ PM to request 27 since that would make for a shorter flight; which is consistent with our policy to request the such when available. Quad Cities approach informed us that runway 9/27 was closed for painting and would not reopen for 30 minutes; and asked if we could accept runway 13.  I will note that our flight was on-time and arrived very close to our scheduled arrival. Knowing that I had reviewed the NOTAMS on the dispatch release but fearing that I had missed a big one; I had the FO query them about the NOTAM status.  Approach control informed us that no NOTAM had been issued for this closure; even though it was a preplanned closure; and that it is the policy of the airport is not to issue NOTAMS for this kind of thing.We got performance numbers for 13; determined that we could use it; set up for; briefed and flew the visual approach to 13 and landed without further incident. At the gate I phoned dispatch to advise them of the closure; and they confirmed that they had no knowledge of it. Sugestions: 9/27 is the main runway at MLI. If a preplanned closure of that runway at the scheduled arrival time of a 121 operator isn't worthy of a NOTAM; then I'm not sure what is. Clearly this should have been a NOTAM.'

summary: While taxiing at an unfamiliar, unlit uncontrolled airport at night, a pilot turned onto the runway too early because the taxiway lights did not extend all the way to the threshold and a corner runway light was burnt out. The aircraft's propeller struck the damaged light, causing damage to both the light and the propeller.
rationale: The Airport label is assigned because the primary hazards were nonexistent ramp lighting, incomplete taxiway lighting, and a burnt-out runway light on the physical surface. This aligns precisely with the "Poor airport lighting" repeating issue in the reference material. It is not Human Factors because the pilot was actively exercising diligence, and the root cause was the physical deficiency of the airport infrastructure that removed critical visual cues. It is not Aircraft because there was no mechanical failure prior to the collision with the physical obstruction.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I was taxiing in an extremely dark; remote location at an uncontrolled airport I was unfamiliar with. Lighting was nonexistent in the ramp area. I pulled up to the runway hold short line and activated the runway lighting system. I was diligent about checking the markings and my surroundings. After making my call on UNICOM; I started to taxi on to the runway. The blue taxi lights don't go all the way to the runway; and I'm fairly certain the runway light at that corner was burnt out. Because that light wasn't visible; I turned on the runway too early (essentially cutting the corner); and my prop struck the burnt out runway light. The runway light was damaged; and the prop sustained damage as well. The runway light will need to be replaced; and the prop may be able to be repaired. The engine ran fine after the strike; and I was able to taxi.The reason this incident occurred is because of a burnt out runway light and the fact I'm a new pilot at an unfamiliar airport in very dark conditions. I should have continued straight onto the runway instead of 'cutting the corner'; but the vast majority of my experience thus far has been on much larger (wider) runways; so I'm used to making an arcing turn as I turn on the runway. I know now in limited-sight conditions to go straight out to the runway and not make a 90-degree turn until I'm clearly in the center of the runway.'

summary: A flight crew departing a busy non-towered airport had to abort their takeoff plans and exit the runway when a twin-engine aircraft pulled out onto an intersecting runway without making any CTAF radio announcements. The crew had to taxi back and re-sequence for departure after the non-communicating traffic took off.
rationale: This incident is classified as Airport because it involves an aircraft operating unsafely without communication at a non-towered field, creating a surface runway conflict. This maps perfectly to the "Non-towered airport hazards" repeating issue, which explicitly covers conflicts from non-communicating traffic at uncontrolled airports. It is not Procedure because the non-communicating aircraft is treated as an environmental hazard typical of this specific airport layout rather than a documented SOP violation by the reporter. It is not Airspace Structure because the conflict occurred on the physical airport surface runways rather than in the airborne traffic pattern.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Our flight was planned to depart runway 35 in MTJ back to ZZZ1. After getting our takeoff clearance from Denver center we were cleared to depart. Made a CTAF call and pulled out on runway for departure. Upon lining up with the runway the FO stated is that an airplane down there" I agreed with him and continued the roll to take the next runway exit. The aircraft that pulled out onto 17 after we pulled out on 35 did not make any CTAF announcements. We tried queuing them after pulling off with no answer. Another aircraft on CTAF who was doing pattern work stated they could see the twin engine down by 17. After the twin took off we taxied back around for a 35 departure again. The other aircraft never made a call.Busy non towered airport with an aircraft not communicating on CTAF. Build a tower and aircraft should be communicating on the field."'

summary: After a gate change, a flight crew found that the taxi lane paint ended prematurely, providing virtually no guidance to safely park the aircraft at the assigned gate. The pilot noted that the lack of painted lines forced the aircraft dangerously close to other parked planes, presenting a severe collision hazard at night.
rationale: This report falls under Airport because the inciting problem is the physical absence of painted taxi lane guidance on the ramp surface. This aligns directly with the "Faded or missing runway/taxiway markings" repeating issue. It is not Equipment / Tooling because the hazard is a lack of infrastructure (paint), not a broken piece of ground equipment like a tug or tow bar. It is not Human Factors because the risk is created by the airport's layout deficiencies rather than pilot misjudgment or distraction.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Gate changed to B1. Taxi lane paint stops before B1 and available concrete is dangerously close to aircraft parked on B2 - there is virtually no guidance from abeam B3 to the last gate; B1. Likelihood of contacting another aircraft is compounded at night. Inadequate airport taxi markings to assure separation from aircraft parked at the gate. Paint (continue) the taxi line. Or; provide tow-in; or require marshals from point of taxi line ending.'

summary: A pilot reported that a portion of a runway and associated taxiways had been permanently closed for two years, yet the airport diagram and chart supplement had never been updated to reflect the physical changes. While NOTAMs were published, the pilot argued that relying on NOTAMs for years instead of updating the permanent publications is an unacceptable safety risk.
rationale: Based on the updated reference guide logic, this is classified as Airport because the airport management completed long-term physical modifications but neglected to coordinate the update of the physical diagrams over multiple years. This creates a persistent, hazardous mismatch between the physical airport configuration and its long-term documentation. It is not Chart Or Publication because the charting authorities can only update what the airport officially submits, making the root failure an administrative gap at the local airport level. It is not Procedure because the pilot is documenting a systemic infrastructure hazard, not a specific rule violation during a flight.
text: Narrative 1 - 'In the Summer of 2022; the eastern 353 ft. of TTD's runway was permanently closed along with Taxiways A8 and B8. While NOTAMs are published regarding the closures; the airport diagram and chart supplement have not been updated in the two years since the changes. I believe this is a safety of flight issue. Even if made aware of the changes via the NOTAMs; pilots might forget about the NOTAM when later referring to the airport diagram. While this risk in inherent whenever a change such as this is made; not updating the normal flight planning publications for two years is unacceptable.'

summary: While waiting for a gate, a flight crew observed other aircraft starting engines and taxiing directly adjacent to active construction areas on the ramp. The resulting jet blast blew massive amounts of dust, debris, and plastic coverings around, endangering construction workers and creating an unsafe ground environment.
rationale: This is classified as Airport because the hazard stems from construction activities occurring dangerously close to active taxi operations, creating a massive FOD and jet blast hazard on the ramp. This perfectly matches the "Construction zones without adequate notice" and "FOD and surface condition" repeating issues. It is not Environment - Non Weather Related because the debris and workers are tied to physical airport infrastructure construction rather than a random external event like a drone or bird. It is not Equipment / Tooling because the primary danger is the airport's uncontained construction materials, not a failure of a specific ground vehicle.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Concourse A South has a 90-degree pushback requirement. There is also construction on [Taxiway] AS. While waiting for our gate to open; we saw 2 aircraft directly adjacent to the construction areas startup and single engine taxi out. We observed lots of dust and debris; workers turning around so they wouldn't get blasted in the face; plastic coverings getting blown around; and workers trying to hold things down. This did not appear to be safe and I would not feel comfortable being on the ground in such close proximity to an aircraft taxiing out.'

summary: A flight crew was cleared to taxi via a specific taxiway but inadvertently turned onto a different taxiway because the surface signage was not clearly visible. After ATC queried the crew and corrected their route, the crew recommended that the airport make the physical sign for the missed taxiway more prominent.
rationale: The Airport label applies because the primary safety hazard was poor visibility of physical taxiway signage on the airport surface. This fits exactly with the "Confusing or absent signage" repeating issue listed in the reference guide. It is not Human Factors because both pilots were actively looking for the sign, and the error was induced entirely by the physical inadequacy of the airport's infrastructure. It is not Procedure because the crew did not intentionally deviate from their clearance; they were led astray by the physical environment.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Departing gate; we used the yellow line to exit the ramp; the clearance from Ramp Control was taxi to Spot X and contact Ground. Approaching Spot X; we contacted Ground and the clearance given was taxing to [Runway] 26R Via C. Instead of taking the second left onto [Taxiway] C we took the first left onto [Taxiway] Q from the yellow line. Once on [Taxiway] Q; ATC queried us about being on the incorrect taxiway and cleared us from [Taxiway] Q to join [Taxiway] C for [Runway] 26R.In the debrief we noted neither myself or the Captain saw a sign for [Taxiway] Q and only saw signs for [Taxiway] C and [Taxiway] B. Since the first sign we saw was [Taxiway] C it led us to believe the first Taxiway was C. Recommendation would be to make the sign for [Taxiway] Q more visible.'

summary: A flight crew missed their designated runway entry taxiway during an intersection departure because the physical signage was darker than expected and two taxiways were located in very close proximity. The crew had to stop, cancel their takeoff clearance, and receive a reroute to the opposite side of the airport.
rationale: This is classified as Airport because the root cause of the taxi deviation was poorly illuminated, dark taxiway signage combined with a confusing physical layout. This aligns tightly with both the "Poor airport lighting" and "Confusing or absent signage" repeating issues. It is not Chart Or Publication because the issue lay entirely with the physical illumination of the signs on the pavement, not a charting or navigation database error. It is not Human Factors because the crew was actively running their checklist and the visual deficiency of the airport infrastructure caused the oversight.
text: Narrative 1 - 'On taxi out; cleared to taxi via Delta to Runway 23L. Tower cleared us for takeoff well prior to D2. Pilot Flying (PF) called for takeoff below the line. While running checklist; we passed D1; the last entry point for Runway 23L on the east side. Both noticed too late. Stopped the aircraft and informed tower. Takeoff clearance was canceled and rerouted taxi to Delta; November; Papa to other side of airport. Once on the other side; immediately given takeoff clearance again. Flight continued without incident after that.Cause - Two taxiways very close to each other and signage was darker than expected.Suggestion - All taxi lights were on for the aircraft but still hard to see. Better airfield lighting for taxiway signage.'

summary: A pilot reported that the ramp at an airport had absolutely no lighting, making it impossible to see parking centerlines and rendering external walkarounds extremely dangerous due to tripping hazards. Ramp personnel indicated they had been unsuccessfully begging the airport for lighting improvements for an extended period.
rationale: This report is classified as Airport because the core issue is the complete physical absence of lighting infrastructure on the ramp. This is explicitly covered under the "Poor airport lighting" repeating issue in the categorization framework. It is not Company Policy because the lack of lighting is a physical deficiency managed by the airport authority, rather than an airline management directive or pressure tactic. It is not Equipment / Tooling because the hazard is a permanent lack of infrastructure, not a broken mobile light cart or ground support tool.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Lighting on the ramp at JAN isn't just inadequate it's completely absent. There is no ramp lighting at all. Parking center line is impossible to see. I imagine doing a walkaround is dangerous for a Pilot due to lack of lighting and potential tripping hazards. Ramp Personnel explained to me that they have been begging the airport for lighting improvements since the station was reopened.  It needs addressed immediately.'

summary: A mechanic deferred a wing light and printed a new maintenance log but became distracted and forgot to sign the Airworthiness Release (AWR). The flight crew assumed the paperwork was complete and departed without a valid AWR.
rationale: This is classified as Logbook Entry because the primary safety event was the omission of a mandatory signature on an Airworthiness Release. This maps directly to the "Missing Signatures & Forms" repeating issue for this category. It is not classified as Procedure because the physical maintenance and checks were executed properly prior to the distraction. It is not Human Factors because, within this taxonomy, specific failures of official aircraft maintenance record-keeping are explicitly routed to this label.
text: Narrative 1 - 'My partner and I were assigned an aircraft. This aircraft was scheduled to sit and have an Aircraft Service (ASE) complied with. We complied with the part of it; and was going to finish up the ASE portion of it after our second aircraft was taken care of. We found a wing light inoperative and wrote that up; then went to aircraft Y. Aircraft Y came in and we complied with the [required checks] on this aircraft. We had a few write ups; nose tire change; cargo seals pulled out; O2 PSI low. While complying with these write ups; aircraft X was called up. I helped finish up the second aircraft and went to pull [deferred maintenance] logs for both aircraft. Gave my partner the [deferred maintenance] log for aircraft Y and I took aircraft X's [deferred maintenance] log back to aircraft X. Once there I deferred the wing light and finished up the [required] check and signed the book for those items. I decided to pull a new [deferred maintenance] log for aircraft X since I had time and add a MEL to it instead of writing it into the previous [deferred maintenance] log. I did not sign the Airworthiness Release (AWR) at this time until I had the new [deferred maintenance] log. I printed the new [deferred maintenance] log and filled it out and placed it into the book and I failed to sign the AWR. I went back to aircraft Y and we kicked out this aircraft. I went back to aircraft X and waited for the crew to show up. When the crew came I waited for about 10 to 15 minutes so they could get their preflight finished. I went up the stairs where I was met by the Captain heading down the stairs. He stated 'I was just coming down to see you; logbook looks good.' so I turned and did not go up and look for myself; went back down to wait for the loading to be completed and check the aircraft for doors prior to departure. I worked the head set and launched the aircraft without an AWR.'

summary: A flight crew noticed a logbook sign-off indicating that pilots had cycled the water service door. The reporter pointed out that this is physically impossible without assistance due to height constraints, suggesting the logbook entry was falsified or improperly credited.
rationale: This report is classified as Logbook Entry because the safety concern revolves around a potentially falsified or inaccurate maintenance sign-off in the official aircraft log. This aligns with the newly added "Falsified or Out-of-Sequence Entries" repeating issue. It is not Company Policy because the hazard is a specific, inaccurate written record rather than a broad management directive. It is not Procedure because the issue is the fraudulent documentation of the task rather than a tactical physical error during the servicing itself.
text: Narrative 1 - 'While flight planning crew noticed a sign off about the water service door being cycled by the crew. At 5'10' this reporter could not reach said door and with just under 4% of the population is reported over 6'3'; (guesstimate of size required to reach) one must assume the pilots could not reach the door without some assistance. This is forbidden under FOM (Flight Operations Manual) guidance Pilot Accomplished Tasks. Just want to ensure [Operations Management] is not requesting actions of pilots that are forbidden.'

summary: A Captain made an aircraft maintenance logbook entry for a broken lavatory seat, expecting maintenance to process it sequentially. The Captain later discovered that maintenance had improperly documented an additional deferral for door lights on an out-of-sequence page from a previous calendar day, causing severe dispatch confusion.
rationale: The Logbook Entry label is appropriate because the primary hazard was the confusing and improper sequential documentation of maintenance actions within the official aircraft logbook. This exactly matches the "Falsified or Out-of-Sequence Entries" classification criteria. It is not classified as MEL because the root dispute was the physical page placement of the written entry rather than the legal restrictions of the deferral itself. It is not Procedure because the physical maintenance was not the issue; the administrative record-keeping practices were flawed.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Upon arrival in ZZZ found lavatory seat detached and FA (Flight Attendant)-A advised that Air Stair door lights were not working. Contacted Maintenance Control for those items.While making log book entries I found that the Air Stair door lights were working and advised FA-A on how to operate the push down button to activate the lights. I then made the Lavatory seat AML (Aircraft Maintenance Logbook) entry. Maintenance showed up some time later and took the broken seat and AML.While waiting for Maintenance to return I called my Dispatcher and she advised Maintenance was going to MEL the LAV and inquired about the Air Stair door lights. I advised her there was no AML entry for the lights as they were working properly. She then consulted Maintenance Control on her end and provided the Lavatory Inoperative MEL Dispatch release amendment over the phone to my dispatch release. We then boarded and I reviewed the MEL provisions with FA-A and the First Officer. As boarding was winding up; Maintenance showed up and performed the Maintenance actions required to MEL the lavatory. I reviewed the last page of the AML to verify my write up was complied with. And off we went.After takeoff we received a message to contact Dispatch. After contacting; I learned that Maintenance had put the air stair door lights on MEL during this process.After reviewing the AML more closely I discovered that Maintenance had made a Maintenance write up and MEL of the air stair door lights on a page prior to my lavatory entry. They were able to do this due to daily checks from the previous days still having white pages to write on. I did not expect this. My assumption is not use white pages with daily checks from previous calendar days for AML entries and to make AML entries sequentially.As a side note; I find it odd that Maintenance did not even ask me to make the additional AML entry for the Air Stair door lights and they did not verify the operation of the lights prior to the MEL.Lesson learned; do not try to get ahead of the game by amending the dispatch release with MEL's until the log book is back in your hands. Let the process work. This will make a great example of swiss cheese and expectation bias when conducting Captain OE.'

summary: An aircraft owner reviewing their logbooks months after a major engine change discovered several confusing, erroneous, and potentially altered maintenance entries. The owner reported the documentation errors to the maintenance facility for immediate corrective action.
rationale: This fits the Logbook Entry classification because the sole focus of the report is the discovery of erroneous and altered historical records within the aircraft's maintenance logbooks. This aligns completely with the "Falsified or Out-of-Sequence Entries" and general documentation flaws repeating issues. It is not Manuals because the flawed documents are the specific historical logs for that individual airframe, rather than a general mechanic's reference manual. It is not Procedure because the reporter explicitly states they do not attribute a subsequent mechanical failure to the physical maintenance work, leaving only the paperwork in question.
text: Narrative 1 - 'This report relates to a post-maintenance flight of my aircraft on Date. It has recently come to my attention that maintenance performed prior to that flight may not have been accurately or timely recorded in one or more of the aircraft's air frame; engine; and propeller logbooks. I have entered the date of the flight (Date) as the 'Date of Occurrence' at the top of this report; but I consider this report to be timely because I have only recently become aware that the confusing maintenance records it addresses could be problematic.On Date 1; I delivered my aircraft to an aircraft maintenance facility for an annual inspection; engine change; and related maintenance. During Month 1 and Month 2; I observed portions of the annual inspection and related maintenance work as that work was performed; and I observed portions of the work performed in changing the engine.After all work (including ground running of the newly-installed engine and ground testing of other aircraft systems) was completed in Month 2; maintenance personnel advised me that the aircraft was ready to fly and should be flown to assure that its performance was satisfactory. Maintenance personnel asked me to fly the aircraft and I did so; remaining in the immediate vicinity of the airport. During that flight; an alternator bearing failed. I do not ascribe the bearing failure to any deficiency in the maintenance performed. Engine power was not affected and I conducted a normal landing upon observing the illumination of the aircraft's ALT OUT annunciator.The engine; propeller; and propeller governor were subsequently disassembled; inspected for contamination and repaired (or overhauled; in the case of the propeller governor); and re-installed in the aircraft. Following that work; the aircraft and its logbooks were returned to me by the maintenance facility on or about Date 2. Those logbooks had remained in the continuous custody of the maintenance facility from the time I delivered them to the maintenance facility on Date 1; until they were returned to me on or about Date 2.A maintenance-related event occurred with respect to the aircraft on Date3. It was the subject of an ASRS report submitted by me. I have discussed that event with the owner of the maintenance facility. I have also reviewed the confusing logbook entries for the inspection and maintenance work described above. In the course of those discussions and that review; I have become aware of what appear to be errors; inconsistencies; or alterations in those entries; and I have brought them to the attention of the owner of the maintenance facility; for such corrective action as may be necessary or appropriate. Yesterday the owner of the maintenance facility agreed that corrective action may be necessary with respect to those entries; and I am therefore submitting this ASRS today.'

summary: After experiencing a total loss of brakes and steering, a pilot safely exited the runway into the grass and shut down the aircraft. After maintenance secured the aircraft, the pilot flew the plane to a repair facility but realized they had failed to obtain a required Special Flight Permit.
rationale: This is classified as Logbook Entry because the regulatory violation was the complete failure to obtain and document a Special Flight Permit prior to repositioning a damaged aircraft. This explicitly maps to the "Missing Signatures & Forms" repeating issue regarding ferry permits. It is not classified as Aircraft because the mechanical failure had already concluded safely; the subsequent hazard was an administrative flight without proper documentation. It is not Procedure because the pilot simply forgot the administrative permit rather than making a tactical physical error during the flight.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Upon landing touchdown I applied [brakes] to slow the aircraft then shortly thereafter I realized that I didn't have brakes or nose wheel steering. The aircraft was heading off the right side of Runway 4 and nothing I did could change the aircraft direction or slow the aircraft. I elected to emergency shutoff both engines feathering the props and preparing for runway departure.  I pulled the elevator aft to put more pressure on the aft gear and coasted to a stop just outside the runway safety zone in the mowed grass. There were no injuries to passengers; nor damage to the aircraft or airfield. I was met by Airfield Safety who help me get a tug for the airplane and he took my information for all reports.  The airplane was towed to the FBO and remained there until I returned on date.  I returned to the FBO with my normal maintenance personnel and we determined that the cause of my issues was electrical.  The aircraft was made safe for a flight to ZZZ1 where further inspections could be preformed. The mistake I made was not getting a ferry flight permit before flying the airplane to ZZZ1.'

summary: A mechanic investigating a Mast Moment Exceedance cleared the fault code and refused to complete the required physical inspection, attributing the alert to moisture. The mechanic then falsified the maintenance logbook by signing off that the required inspection had been completed, allowing the aircraft to fly multiple times.
rationale: This report falls under Logbook Entry because a critical safety violation was the intentional falsification of an official maintenance logbook sign-off to bypass required work. This directly aligns with the "Falsified or Out-of-Sequence Entries" repeating issue. It is not Procedure because the mechanic's deliberate fraudulent sign-off supersedes the physical skipped steps as the primary systemic failure. It is not Aircraft because the exceedance was a known, recorded event on the ground that was administratively covered up rather than an unprompted in-flight failure.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Crew was dispatched for [a flight]. An Eng Chip CT Caution precluded launch and flight was cancelled. PIC called on-duty Mechanic to inform him of the maintenance issue. The Mechanic asked me to pull the Eng Chip CT circuit breaker to see if it would extinguish the light. Upon returning to acft and turning on the battery; I noticed the light was extinguished. However; I also noticed a Red Fault light with the letters 'LMT' adjacent to it. I turned the page selector to the maintenance page and saw that the limit exceeded showed a code of 'MMEXC 0061'. I called the mechanic back and let him know that the ENG CHIP CT had resolved itself; but now there was a Mast Moment Exceeded Fault. He informed me he was enroute to take a look at it. [Later;] Mechanic texted me and told me he was at the base. I walked out to the aircraft and noticed the mechanic was sitting in the pilot seat and that the battery was on. As I climbed into the co-pilot seat; the Mechanic said he didn't see the red fault light and LMT light on the CDS I had told him about earlier. I explained that it was there and asked him when and why he cleared the fault. He mumbled that he didn't clear the fault and he never saw it. I then let him know that I had taken a picture of the fault and showed it to him. He asked me to send him the photo and I did. After researching the fault code; the Mechanic explained that he would have to disassemble the rotor head and inspect the bearings; mast and other components and that he'd have to remove the seal. He also explained that the aircraft would be out of service for at least 24hrs because after reassembling everything; the sealant would need 24hrs to cure. I then briefed the medical crew; called operations and apprised them of the situation. A few hours later; the Mechanic approached me and told me he spoke with his supervisor and other maintenance personnel and that it was agreed upon that he could just complete an external visual inspection of the mast; blade cuffs and other components and that would be sufficient; since the Mechanic believed that it was 'probably moisture' that caused an erroneous fault indication. I respectfully disagreed since this particular aircraft recently had a new second-generation seal installed and there were no subsequent erroneous Mast Moment indications. The Mechanic said he already completed the required suggested visual inspection and it was 'good to go'.  Later in the morning; the on-coming pilot approached me and asked to see the fault on the CDS. I told him that the Mechanic already cleared the fault but he could still see the MMEXC 0061 code on the maintenance page. After seeing the exceedance code; the pilot then mentioned that he now probably looks a bit suspicious since he had asked me at shift change the evening prior; 'how does the T1 record exceedances?'; a fact that didn't register with me when I saw the fault code later in my shift. The pilot also mentioned that it was possible that he could have inadvertantly displaced the cyclic while on the ground but honestly didn't recall seeing or hearing anything unusual. I told him if there was an exceedance; that it wasn't a huge issue and that the required inspection had to be done and if nothing is found; we'd back in service.  In the middle of this conversation with the on-coming Pilot; the Mechanic approached us and said that he spoke with the Maintenance supervisor and other leadership; and that all were in agreement that it was probably moisture which caused the erroneous exceedance and that he only needed to conduct an external visual inspection of the blade cuffs; and other components. He said that if a 'true exceedance' occurred; that there would be visual damage to the inside portion of the blade cuffs. I respectfully disagreed but told him that if the Maintenance supervisors were all in agreement with the plan of action then that was their decision. The Mechanic kept asking the other Pilot and I; how we 'felt' about the aircraft and the procedure he proposed. I explained that I didn't 'feel' there was anything unsafe with the aircraft; but if a maintenance action was supposed to be completed; then it should be done. He then asserted that the maintenance team all agreed that the visual inspection he performed was appropriate. I then departed the base. On the way home; I contacted the Aviation Service Manager (ASM) to inform him of what happened and told him that the Mechanic said he agreed with the maintenance plan. The ASM denied saying that he was good with any specific plan but that he said to conduct whatever the required inspection was.  I sent the on-duty Pilot and the Mechanic a message letting them know that my official stance is that we don't 'know' if moisture was the culprit for the Mast Moment exceedance and that I didn't think it wise to not follow maintenance action procedures based on our 'feelings' or beliefs that it could be this or that. I said I felt it best to err on the side of caution and proper maintenance procedures rather than guessing.  The Mechanic then called me in a very heated tone and asked me what my concerns were. I explained that my concerns were that 1. We don't know if moisture caused an erroneous exceedance. 2. The on-duty Pilot asked how exceedances were recorded at shift change the day prior; and admitted at least there was a possibility one could have occurred. 3. The Mechanic stated that there have been no erroneous Mast Moment 'moisture' issues since the new '2nd generation' seal was recently installed. 4. And finally that he (the Mechanic) cleared the fault before I came out to the aircraft and told me he didn't see the fault. At this point; he vehemently denied clearing the fault upon arrival. I told him he cleared it before I climbed in the aircraft. He denied it again and claimed he didn't clear it until he returned to the base from the Base. I reminded him that he cleared it prior to that and said he didn't see what I was talking about; at which point I told him I took a picture of the fault and that he had asked me to send him the picture; which occurred at XA:39. He then began yelling; telling me he wasn't going to argue with me about what time he cleared the fault. Additionally; he said he wasn't going to sit there and let me question his work ethic. I then reminded him of the multiple times I've called him while he was on call and he complained that this wasn't his base or his aircraft. He said that he was hired to work at his base and nowhere in his contract does it say he was to work at the other bases and that he's 'tired of fixing (expletive) that the on-duty Pilot breaks'.  In a hostile tone; he kept asking me what I wanted him to do. Did I want him to take the aircraft out of service or keep it in service? He said he needed an answer from me ASAP because he had another aircraft to deal with that was stuck in ZZZ. He said that he was the only mechanic available and that he'd have to complete the required inspection 'taking everything apart' by himself and that if I wanted him to take the aircraft out of service; that he would look like a [expletive] because he already completed the write-up and put the aircraft back in service. I explained that I wasn't worried about how he looked; I was only concerned that the proper maintenance action was completed; the aircraft was in an airworthy state; and the entire crew was safe. He then received an incoming call and our call ended. It was discovered that the Mechanic wrote up the fault; and corresponding corrective maintenance action as completed. However; it was found that he did not complete the required correction action per the [maintenance manual]. Four separate flights totally 3.3hrs were completed after the Mechanic signed off the Maintenance Logbook indicating the required inspection was completed. I believe the Mechanic knowingly misrepresented the circumstances surrounding the maintenance event in orderto avoid having to complete the required maintenance action per the manual. To reiterate; Mechanics and Pilots the importance of following maintenance procedures by the book. To not base maintenance actions on feelings or assumptions but to ensure maintenance is done as required.'

summary: After an emergency exit overhead liner fell during a flight, the crew discovered upon landing that maintenance had temporarily secured it with tape. The pilot noted that there was absolutely no logbook write-up or Non-Essential Equipment Furnishings (NEF) deferral documented for this makeshift repair prior to the flight.
rationale: This is classified as Logbook Entry because the primary safety breakdown was the complete lack of required logbook documentation or NEF paperwork for a physical maintenance repair. This perfectly matches the "Undocumented Maintenance" classification criteria. It is not Equipment / Tooling because the tape itself held; the hazard was the crew's blindness to the repair due to missing records. It is not Procedure because the primary violation highlighted by the pilot was the administrative failure to write up the NEF deferment prior to dispatch.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I received a call from the flight attendants shortly after takeoff. The Flight Attendant said that the cabin overhead liner at the over wing emergency exits had fallen down. It was the overhead liner that had the 'EXIT' sign attached. I transferred the controls and radios over to the First Officer and consulted with the flight attendants on the nature of the problem. The concern was the possible blockage of the emergency exits if an evacuation had to be initiated for whatever reason. However; there was a Company Flight Attendant commuting home sitting in the emergency exit row who offered to assist if necessary. The flight attendants determined that it would be safe to proceed; consultation with the company through ACARS said that there was no need to return to the field. As there seemed no additional risks; I agreed to continue the flight. On landing; it was noticed that the panel was held up by tape; not dissimilar to the type used in the cargo bays and other temporary fixes. A Field Mechanic stated that one of the two securing grommets was too large to hold the securing pin. It was further noticed that there was no write up for the NEF (Non-Essential Equipment Furnishings) deferment. Prior to the flight; a Company Mechanic was working on the cabin lights in that vicinity. I thought it was associated with that maintenance procedure. I did not ask the Mechanic what was done as there was a push to keep the flight on time. The Field Mechanic who inspected the fallen panel stated that it was unlikely to be associated with the cabin lights procedure. He proceeded to NEF the panel.[My suggestion is to] resist the pressure from gate to board early. I personally feel that the 'auto-boarding' procedure from main-line adds unnecessary pressure on the flight crew to get things done in a hurry. Every crew member that I have talked to says that they feel pressured to rush tasks when passengers are waiting at the doorway as preflight procedures are still being completed or when catering is still in the galley stocking as passengers as boarding. Take time to talk to the Mechanic and ask what tasks were actually performed. If I had done this; I would have known if they were the ones who applied the tape and not someone else. Refuse to accept the abnormal as normal. The proliferation of temporary quick-fix tape within the fleet should not be disregarded as just another normal day. Each temporary taping should be examined to see if it complies with company operational procedures. Again; time pressures can muddy the waters some.'

summary: An unqualified contract mechanic replaced a Secondary Power Control Unit (SPCU) on an aircraft but completely failed to document the installation in the aircraft's logbook. An investigation later forced the station to correct the historical logbook records.
rationale: The Logbook Entry label applies because the primary regulatory violation was an entirely undocumented major component replacement in the aircraft's official maintenance log. This directly matches the "Undocumented Maintenance" repeating issue. It is not Procedure because the specific hazard being rectified by the station was the missing written record of the SPCU installation. It is not Company Policy because the omission was a specific mechanic's documentation failure rather than a broad management directive suppressing maintenance records.
text: Narrative 1 - 'The station received an e-mail to correct the logbook of Aircraft X. Contract maintenance; NAME; (NUMBER); at ZZZ replaced the SPCU on DATE. He failed to enter the item in the logbook. Upon further investigation; I found NAME unqualified on Company planes and logbook in Software.DATE; TIMEUpon clarification from tech; the SPCU was documented in the logbook. However; the galley battery meter module was not documented in the logbook. We're confirming the part number and performing ops check for the module.'

summary: While completing an IDG oil level check, a mechanic improperly signed off both their own task and the required secondary verification block for the fan cowling latches.
rationale: This incident is classified as Logbook Entry because the safety concern revolves around a single mechanic's ability to improperly sign off a secondary verification block in the maintenance records. This aligns with the improper sign-off repeating issues where documentation accountability fails. It is not Procedure because the physical check of the latches is secondary to the administrative flaw allowing one person to sign for two. It is not Human Factors because this taxonomy explicitly routes specific failures of official aircraft maintenance record-keeping and signatures to this label.
text: Narrative 1 - 'While working IDG oil level and DPI check Task Card; I was able to sign off a warning to add second mechanic to verify my work securing latches of fan cowling; as well as the original item.'

summary: A flight crew conducting a walk-around noted loose, vibrating acoustic panels on an engine, which a mechanic then physically broke off to secure. It took the crew three verbal requests to force the mechanic to actually document this physical action in the maintenance release document.
rationale: This is classified as Logbook Entry because the core conflict was a mechanic's persistent refusal to document physical maintenance work in the aircraft's official release records. This directly aligns with the "Undocumented Maintenance" repeating issue. It is not Procedure because the mechanic performed the physical repair safely when prompted; the failure was entirely in the administrative record-keeping. It is not MEL because the dispute was about logging a new physical action rather than interpreting the legal restrictions of the existing Carry Forward deferral.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Aircraft had Carry Forward (CF) Number 1 engine damage as acoustic panels missing. Walk around observed portions were extended beyond honeycomb attached to it; leaving potential for it to vibrate and break free. Wrote Maintenance Report Message (MRM) contacted maintenance and they came to flight to tell us it was good to go. I replied I knew it was CF but wanted us to go to engine and inspect. Upon inspection as mechanic wanted to demonstrate it was solid; one of the protruding portion he grabbed broke free. I asked all portions needed to be filed flush with honeycomb as to prevent pieces breaking free. He concurred and work was performed. Upon completion new [release document] did not reflection action work done but simply stated was already a CF. It took three attempts verbally and additional write ups to get maintenance to document on MRD the work they did. They showed me history in their paperwork; but I had great difficulty getting them to simply give us a MRD showing work they did.'

summary: A reporter noted that certain gates at an airport feature jetbridges that overhang the Operational Safety Zone even when properly stowed. This creates a collision hazard for the Number 1 engine of arriving aircraft.
rationale: This is classified as Equipment / Tooling because the primary hazard is the physical design and stowage footprint of ground support equipment (a jetbridge) encroaching on the safety zone. This aligns perfectly with the "Ground Support & Cabin Equipment (GSE) Failures" criteria. It is not classified as Airport because the hazard is a piece of movable ground equipment rather than a permanent runway or taxiway infrastructure defect. It is not Company Policy because the issue is the physical tooling design rather than a specific management directive or operational timeline.
text: Narrative 1 - 'There are gates in SFO where the jetbridge overhangs the Operational Safety Zone when the jetbridge is stowed in the designated stowage box. This results in the Number 1 engine within close proximity to the jetbridge on arrival.'

summary: A heavy ground power cable hoist broke and crashed to the ground just inches away from an employee immediately after being disconnected from an aircraft. The reporter highlighted the lack of regular safety inspections for this heavy ground equipment.
rationale: The Equipment / Tooling label applies because the safety event involved the catastrophic mechanical failure of a piece of ground support equipment. This maps directly to the criteria covering ground power units and related servicing hardware. It is not classified as Airport because the failure was of a specific tool used for aircraft servicing rather than the airport's pavement or lighting infrastructure. It is not Procedure because the employee was operating the equipment normally when it unpredictably broke, meaning no procedural violation caused the failure.
text: Narrative 1 - 'It was brought to my attention that the Ground Power cable hoist broke and came crashing to the ground seconds after it was disconnected from the aircraft and was fully retracted. It fell inches away from where the employee that retracted it stood. Only because of the employee's quick reaction this did not result in severe injury or possible a fatality. These ground power cables are already pretty heavy. By compounding the height to which they are usually stored plus the metal bracket to which they are attached to; increases the velocity to which they fall to the ground. Now imagine if this cable had fall directly on that employee's head and the metal bracket would've impaled his skull. Hence the severity of what could've occurred this morning. This was not the first time one of these ground power cable hoist broke and crashed to the ground here in EWR. I'm not sure how often these cables hoist are being inspected or if they are being inspected at all!'

summary: A baggage handler's hand was caught in a moving belt loader while attempting to retrieve a bag according to new policies. The handler was unable to immediately stop the machine because both the safety sensors and the emergency stop button had been removed from the equipment.
rationale: This report is classified as Equipment / Tooling because the severe safety hazard was a direct result of missing safety sensors and broken emergency stop mechanisms on a baggage belt loader. This aligns precisely with the hazard definitions for defective or modified ground support equipment. It is not Human Factors because the employee was following standard loading instructions and was victimized by the deliberately modified, unsafe machinery.
