It is not Company Policy because the critical point of failure in this specific event was the broken machinery itself rather than the overarching loading policy.
text: Narrative 1 - 'As I was loading inside the rear cargo  [bin] I reached for a bag per new policy. As I reached for the bag my hand was caught underneath the moving belt. As my hand was trapped I reached for the sensor which were removed from the belt loaders; then I attempted to hit the Emergency Stop button; but [that] was also removed from the equipment. I finally reached across my body using the opposite hand. My hand was stuck and almost pulled my entire body out of the aircraft. I managed to dislodge my hand from the belt and immediately exited the aircraft and went to Supervisors office.'

summary: A flight attendant reported that catering personnel consistently load broken service carts without functioning brakes onto the aircraft, creating a severe hazard for both crew and passengers. Despite tagging the carts as defective and filing multiple reports, the broken equipment continues to be cycled into service.
rationale: This is classified as Equipment / Tooling because the recurring hazard involves defective non-flight hardware, specifically galley service carts lacking functional brakes. This fits directly into the "Ground Support & Cabin Equipment (GSE) Failures" repeating issue newly established for this category. It is not classified as Aircraft because the carts are movable cabin accessories rather than integrated structural or mechanical systems of the airplane. It is not Procedure because the flight attendants are correctly tagging the carts; the primary failure lies in the continued circulation of broken tools.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I have written to you in the past in regard to Company placing broken service carts on board the aircraft. These service carts have no brakes and are a safety hazard as well as being dangerous to cabin crew and passengers. I have recently submitted three reports to the company about 4 broken carts with no resolve. In these reports; I have provided the company with pictures and serial numbers of the carts involved. In one incident; while serving; two passengers had to assist me with controlling a run-away cart. My most [recent] report involved 2 carts without brakes; 1 cart without brake pedals on one side of the cart. I wrote a report about this cart on [before]. I tagged this cart as broken without brake pedals; yet in just a little under 1 month; the cart shows up on the aircraft without brake pedals. The continuance of negligence in regard to placing carts without brakes on the aircraft is a safety hazard to fight attendants and passengers as flight attendants cannot control run-away carts; it is extremely difficult to maintain control of a cart without brakes at 35;000 feet. The blatant disregard of Company in placing carts without brakes on the aircraft is horrific; there are carts without brakes placed on airplanes every day. When is it going to stop? I am writing to you as a last resort for all else has failed; broken carts are so prevalent that flight attendants have just given up. We place the red stickers on the carts in hopes that they will be fixed but the same carts show up time after time without being fixed. I am earnestly asking you to look into this matter. Thank you for reviewing my report.Catering should check the carts for working brakes before placing them on the aircraft.'

summary: A dispatcher reported that their primary dispatch software program crashed multiple times per shift over several consecutive days. These repeated crashes severely compromised safety by blocking access to reroute calculations, weather updates, NOTAMs, and ACARS communications with enroute flights.
rationale: This incident fits Equipment / Tooling because the primary failure point was the ground-based dispatch software and flight planning computer system. This directly aligns with the "Dispatch & Flight Planning Software Failures" criteria added to the reference guide. It is not classified as Software and Automation because that label is primarily reserved for the aircraft's internal FMS or autopilot coding rather than external corporate ground software. It is not ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because the failing software belongs to the airline's dispatch tooling, not an FAA or ATC air traffic control facility.
text: Narrative 1 - 'This is the 4th day in a row I have worked [this] dispatch desk and every day the dispatch program randomly crashes 4 to 5 times per shift. Each time it crashes I no longer have the ability to calculate a reroute; ACARS is unavailable; no access to amend releases; no access to weather; NOTAMs; field condition reports; no way to quickly calculate performance data. This can be a safety issue due to lack of available communication with enroute flights; ability to calculate reroutes or diversions.This issue needs to be address quickly by IT and determine the root cause of the issue and quickly develop a remedy.'

summary: Shortly after takeoff in gusty instrument conditions, a B737's flight director erroneously commanded a 30-degree right bank turn away from the assigned departure course. The pilot monitoring noticed the deviation, instructed the flying pilot to disregard the flight director, and successfully re-established the correct heading.
rationale: This is classified as Equipment / Tooling because the inciting event was a malfunctioning piece of aircraft navigation hardware, specifically the flight director generating an erroneous turn command. This matches the "Aircraft Navigation Equipment Malfunctions" sub-category where specific instrumentation provides misguided cues. It is not classified as Software and Automation because the narrative treats the flight director as an isolated equipment failure rather than explicitly citing an FMS database or software logic corruption. It is not Human Factors because the pilots successfully identified the equipment's error and corrected the flight path without committing an operational mistake.
text:
Narrative 1 - 'We departed in a B737.The weather was gusty winds with IMC off the end of the runway. At 400 feet AGL; we engaged LNAV and confirmed LNAV to a fix. Passing approximately 6000 feet; I was distracted by my duties during a very high workload environment. I was contacting Departure Control; setting the thrust levers to climb thrust; retracting flaps; setting gear handle to Off; and placing autobrake switch to off.I then looked at my HSI and noticed we were off course to the right approaching one dot deflection. I then looked over at the FO's (First Officer) PF's (Pilot Flying) attitude indicator and saw the aircraft was in a 30 degree right bank turn. The F/O (PF) was following his flight director which was commanding a 30 degree right bank turn. I immediately directed the F/O (PF) to turn left; explaining we were off course. He complied and banked left disregarding the flight director commands. As we were trying to figure out why the flight director was giving us erroneous commands; NorCal Departure told us we were right of course and to turn to a heading of 110 degrees. Once we selected HDG SEL the flight director gave us correct commands.'
Narrative 2 - 'We immediately went into the weather and were experiencing light to moderate turbulence (takeoff winds were 210/25G35). I was following my command bars and at approximately 6000 feet (approximately 1500 feet AGL) I realized I was in a right 30 degree bank turn going away from the course. At that moment; the Captain said we need to come back to the left. I immediately turned left to get back on the course (disregarding my flight director). Departure Control then asked if we were turning right and gave us a heading of 110 degrees. The Captain selected Heading Select and the command bars then showed a left turn. We got back on course and continued the flight. The command bars may have been trying to turn around back to ZEFFR or to the initial fix on the ZEFFR Departure. We reviewed all our procedures and cannot figure out what caused the command bars to turn right.Not sure if the cause was a B737 and the discontinuity of the SID Departure. Even though it was only a matter of seconds before I recognized there was a problem; then a few more seconds to decide what instrument was giving me the erroneous information; I could have done it quicker with the departure path in mind. In retrospect I will; in the future; bring the general heading of the path more to my crosscheck to back up the command bars. When flying the B737; into weather; into turbulence on takeoff into high terrain; be extra confident on approximate headings and departure track.'

summary: A dispatcher was unable to generate accurate fuel burn or critical fuel scenarios because the flight planning software refused to calculate the requested flight level and Mach number for a North Atlantic track crossing. The computer program continuously defaulted to multiple lower altitudes, preventing the dispatcher from releasing an accurate flight plan.
rationale: The Equipment / Tooling label is assigned because the operational bottleneck was caused by a limitation or glitch in the company's ground-based flight planning software tool. This maps perfectly to the "Dispatch & Flight Planning Software Failures" definition. It is not Chart Or Publication because the issue involved a dynamic software calculation tool failing to process a specific flight level, rather than a static charting error or published approach plate flaw. It is not Company Policy because the dispatcher was attempting to follow correct procedures but was blocked by inadequate computational tooling.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Crew advised North Atlantic (NAT) crossing on filed NAT A; but now FL380/M85 not the filed FL350/M85.  Unable (nor the two Chiefs) to get [computer software] to show FL380/M85; it gives multiple lower altitudes.  *I did not release this flight; but assume the dispatcher brought down to cross at FL350 because was running into same thing.  The flight was at FL380 before reaching coast out point.  Why would it not be able to maintain across the tracks?  I could not get M84 to give FL380 on NAT A as well without [computer software] giving lower flight levels.  Therefore; unable to give crew accurate burn as well accurate Critical fuel scenario.  This was not a track jumper; but the hypothetical what if he was filed on NAT B and got NAT A Fl380/M85.  I would not have been able to generate what the aircraft is actually capable of doing.'

summary: An aircraft crossed its planned rerelease point without receiving the required updated fuel analysis because the company's dispatch flight planning system had unexpectedly gone offline. Operations Control instructed the crew to continue past the waypoint without the legal calculations until the software system was restored and the numbers could be provided.
rationale: This report is classified as Equipment / Tooling because the root cause of the delayed rerelease and potential regulatory violation was the total failure of the dispatch flight planning computer system. The offline software tool left both dispatchers and the flight crew unable to access legally required fuel data. It is not classified as ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because the broken system was the airline's proprietary operational software, not a government ATC facility. While continuing past the waypoint touches on Company Policy or Procedure, the inciting primary problem explicitly designated here is the breakdown of the dispatch tool itself.
text:
Narrative 1 - 'As per our Operation Specifications 'Planned Redispatch or Rerelease En Route;' this flight was unable to obtain the Required Updated Fuel in Time from the Recleared Point IMRAD to continue on to the final destination. The aircraft crossed waypoint 'IMRAD' at XA48 at FL370 with 23;500kg of fuel on board. Required fuel was finally provided at XB04 for 20;578kg. Then another required fuel of 18;685kg came later through ACARS at XB07. Opsec Section 4 states the following: A new operational analysis must be conducted within 2 hours prior to the flight's arrival at the planned redispatch or rerelease point. In preparing the new operational analysis; the dispatcher or person designated to exercise operational control (other than the pilot in command) must: (a) Conduct an updated fuel analysis based on the current route of flight; wind conditions; and aircraft weight on the route from the planned redispatch or rerelease point to the intended destination airport and ay required alternate airports; and........ According to the dispatcher; the system was offline and was unable to compute a new Flight Plan from the recleared point. In summary the Recleared Flight Release was not issued until after crossing the Recleared Point. Not sure if this was legal; since the information should have been obtained before crossing the Recleared Point. The D.O. was in the Dispatch Center and instructed us to continue beyond the Recleared point until the system came back online. We therefore proceeded and obtained the official numbers 16 minutes past the recleared point. We then continued to our destination with no further incident. Dispatch did not have a backup system.  Dispatch should obtain a backup system to avoid any further similar incident.'
Narrative 2 - 'I was in the bunk bed for crew rest at the time of this event. The following narrative is what I was informed when I reported back in flight deck.At approximately 2:45 prior to estimated rerelease (RR) point; Ops Control sent a message via ACARS to the aircraft requesting estimated time; fuel on board; and altitude for the planned rerelease point. Information was provided as requested to Ops Control by the IRO's on the flight deck as the PIC and FO were crew resting in the bunks.At around 1:40 prior to the rerelease point; the crew queried Ops Control via ACARS about the destination and alternate weather; any notam changes; and the recalculated fuel required at the RR point. Ops Control replied stating that the flight planning software was currently down; and that the required fuel was unable to be calculated and that the crew should stand by until further notified by Ops Control. Weather for the destination and alternate airports was sent at this time; and no new notams were in effect.Around 30 minutes prior to the RR point; Ops Control was contacted by the crew via satphone and asked again about fuel required. The crew was told to call back in 10 minutes if no fuel number was sent by Ops Control via ACARS. The crew contacted the PIC out of rest to come to the flight deck at about this time.Ops Control was called again about 10 minutes later via satphone; the crew discussed the still inoperative flight planning system with Ops and the need to make a decision whether a diversion was to be planned; or if any other option was available such as a hold prior to the RR waypoint. Ops Control asked how much 'extra' fuel we had onboard as shown on the flight plan at the last waypoint crossed; which was around 1800 kilos. The crew was told that the DO was in the Ops Control area and was telling the Ops controller that we could proceed beyond the RR point to the next waypoint due to the fact of having this 'extra' fuel available. The crew questioned the legality of continuing beyond the RR point without actually having the required fuel calculation to continue; and was told that the DO was authorizing this.The crew was told to continue past the RR waypoint and six other waypoints several times towards the destination airport before finally receiving the recalculated fuel required for the rerelease 16 minutes after passing the RR point.'

summary: While flying an assigned STAR, ATC informed a flight crew that they were two miles off course despite the aircraft's FMS indicating they were perfectly on track. Although ATC later confirmed there was GPS jamming in the area, the controller suggested the deviation might have been caused by an equipment malfunction on the aircraft's side.
rationale: This is classified as Equipment / Tooling because the deviation was ultimately attributed to an unexplained malfunction of the aircraft's navigation equipment or FMS hardware during a suspected GPS jamming event. This aligns with the "Aircraft Navigation Equipment Malfunctions" repeating issue where the specific tooling fails to track correctly. It is not ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because the controller explicitly suggested the failure was internal to the aircraft's equipment rather than purely an external facility outage. It is not Human Factors because the crew diligently cross-checked all instruments and followed their available data, meaning no cognitive or handling error occurred.
text: Narrative 1 - 'It seems that the new SID/STARS for LAX have not been added in the database for LAX. I could not find the HUULL 1 STAR in the breakdown. We were assigned the HUULL 1 TOKIO transition into LAX. While on the STAR past TOKIO; ATC (LA Center) queried about our position. I answered that we were on course and ATC alleged that we were not. We checked the flight plan in the FMS and double checked; then checked our position again; and everything checked out. We were on course; as far as our equipment was concerned. ATC gave us a heading; and stated that there may be some interference with the GPS in the area. In a minute or less; he cleared us direct HUULL. In another minute or so; he gave us a frequency change and; at that time he apologized for the inconvenience; stating that indeed; there was GPS signal jamming in the area. He then apologized again; for the inconvenience. After landing in LAX; Ground Control gave us a phone number to call for LAX Center; stating there was a possible pilot deviation. I called after we got to the gate; and the LA Center controller that I talked to stated that we were two miles off course and that he had to file a report; and that we should file [a report]. I advised him of the events as explained above and I told him that as far as we could tell; we did nothing wrong. We concluded the conversation; and about ten minutes later; I got a call from the same gentleman who told me that he listened to the tapes; and also talked to the supervisor on duty at the time; who confirmed that there was GPS jamming at the time; but somehow; he was wrong about it. And he also suggested that it could have been equipment malfunction on our side (the aircraft). I thanked him for the info and that was the end of the conversation. Not sure of what the reason for the deviation was; but we double checked and triple checked and everything in the FMS was entered correctly and looked as it should; however; as far as ATC stated; we were off course. Again; not sure to the cause of this event; so I can't offer any mitigating solutions.'

summary: Because standard deicing equipment was unavailable at an airport, a crew and ground personnel attempted to remove snow from an aircraft using soft rubber squeegees. They then hired a helicopter to hover over the aircraft to blow the snow off the tail, creating a significant safety hazard until ATC intervened and stopped the operation.
rationale: This falls under Equipment / Tooling because the entire hazardous sequence was driven by the lack of proper deicing equipment, leading to the use of highly inappropriate makeshift tools. This directly matches the "Improvised or Inappropriate Servicing Tools" sub-category. It is not classified as Weather because, although it was snowing, the critical safety hazard was the dangerous, improvised mechanical method used to remove the snow. It is not Procedure because the crew knowingly invented an unauthorized, makeshift process to compensate for missing equipment rather than accidentally mishandling a standard operation.
text: Narrative 1 - 'It was snowing upon landing. Snow was not forecast to start till [later]; [Crew member and I] began to look into deice options. During the course of the morning we removed the melting snow from the aircraft with soft rubber squeegees. [We] secured a bucket truck and personnel to assist with precipitation removal from the tail as we were informed deice services were no longer available at the airport.Concurrently with the arrival of our passengers and aircraft owner; a helicopter service was secured to assist with the removal of melting snow. The helicopter flew over head as requested. The helicopter landed and the helicopter became airborne and approached the tail. We immediately realized that an additional attempt to pass overhead was about to occur. I made my way to the cockpit and contacted ground control requesting they contact the pilot of the helicopter to have him stop the operation. An additional attempt was made on tower frequency. It was shortly after this the helicopter landed and operations in this effort stopped.In reflection; this procedure was not an acceptable means of snow removal. Failure to immediately report these events was in poor form and I know this is unacceptable. I can not recall a time when I failed to maintain my 'safety first' standard and know it will not be repeated.'

summary: A flight crew received four different arrival clearances in four minutes due to systemic GPS jamming saturating the ATC environment. The constant changes and continuous FMS inputs led to task saturation, causing the crew to miss a crossing restriction.
rationale: This is classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because the external GPS jamming severely degraded and saturated the ambient airspace environment, forcing chaotic clearance changes. This maps directly to the newly added "Ambient Airspace Saturation" repeating issue where external interference disrupts the operational environment. It is not classified as ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because the facility's radios and radar functioned, but the external environment itself was rendered unmanageable. It is not Human Factors because the crew's missed restriction was the direct result of an externally overwhelmed environment rather than an unprompted cognitive lapse.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Center changed our arrival procedure 4 times; 4 different procedures in approximately 4 minutes. Our clearance was to fly to a fix at a specified altitude and due to the confusing clearances that Center was rattling off we missed the crossing restriction and had to be vectored in the descent. The problem was that the crossing restriction was for a clearance that changed. All of this happened because of GPS jamming going on in the area. I'm filling out this report so that you all know that it was EXTREMELY busy; ATC was making too many requests; one right after another; and all of this required continuous FMS changes; inputs; and confirmations. How could things have gone better; I'm not sure. We did the best we could but it became task saturated for them and us. They told us that it wasn't a problem; however; I'm still filling out this report to enlighten the team on how screwball things got with 4 different arrival changes in such a short period of time. I don't have any thoughts on how this could have been avoided.'

summary: An agent reported an unsafe working environment during the COVID-19 pandemic due to massive crowds, a lack of social distancing, and missing protective shields at the check-in kiosks. The sheer volume of international travelers and their families created an uncontrollable biological hazard in a confined space.
rationale: The Environment - Non Weather Related label is appropriate because the primary hazard is the ambient, physical reality of a crowded, infectious environment. This aligns squarely with the "Biological & Health Hazards" criteria where the ambient setting itself is the threat. It is not Company Policy because the specific danger highlighted is the uncontrollable volume and physical proximity of the passengers, rather than a corporate administrative rule. It is not Airport because it relates to a transient biological crowding threat rather than a permanent structural facility defect.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I have been called as shop steward by agents to report unsafe COVID work environment; and I noticed due to short staffing and limited area of check in point also the volume of travelers there is no social distancing and no shields to protect agents from possible exposure to COVID.  Most of international travelers are subject to document check and most are not familiar with kiosk checking; also each passenger is accompanied with several family members which makes it more difficult to deal with the crises. There were only 2 agents assigned for all the passengers and all the crowds with was out of control. All has been reported and requested for more manpower and full service to avoid direct and close contact with passengers.The issue of lack of manpower and enforcing passengers and agents to use the kiosk when there is no protection is serious safety issue during [the] COVID pandemic.I hope this report brings all the safety issues to your attention.'

summary: An aircraft was forced to declare an urgent situation and divert to an alternate airport because the destination ATC became completely saturated and stopped answering calls. The pilot later learned the local operational environment had been overwhelmed due to two uncommunicated bird strikes closing the airport.
rationale: This is classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because the precipitating event was an external wildlife hazard (bird strikes) that completely saturated the local operational environment. The physical environmental intrusion cascaded into an unmanageable airspace situation. It is not Staffing because the ATC unresponsiveness was due to a sudden, external environmental crisis rather than a routine scheduling shortage. It is not Procedure because the controllers were overwhelmed by the physical reality of the environment rather than simply forgetting to answer the radio.
text: Narrative 1 - 'This divert was due to VOBL's total ATC saturation. (It was later reported to me by the VOBL mechanic that they actually closed the airport for 2 bird strikes. ATC did not relay that information.) The [urgent situation] declared was due to lack of response from the controllers. It got to the point where they stopped answering calls from not just us; but other aircraft as well. My fuel planning as programmed in the FMC was conservative to be sure; but for good reason. India is experiencing growing pains with rapid air traffic growth. The weather can be somewhat unpredictable with fog.As we held in the second pattern; you could feel the tension rising with all inbound traffic. Multiple aircraft were expressing concern. My attention shifted to the possibility of lots of airplanes all wanting to head to Hyderabad. We continued to hold and tried to work ourselves in; but my confidence level was near zero. We were finally told that our expected touchdown time was XB39. It was XA56 with a hold limit of XA57. So; we requested clearance to Hyderabad several times and were either ignored or told to standby. Radio congestion was awful. It is unfortunate that in some corners of the world the only way to get the point across is to [advise ATC]. I felt this to be a prudent course of action given the circumstances.'

summary: Due to bad rides and turbulence, an aircraft requested a descent, which the controller granted without completing a required point-out to the underlying sector. The controller noted that a constantly changing sector configuration and lack of traffic had degraded their competency and situational awareness.
rationale: This falls under Environment - Non Weather Related because the precipitating operational trigger was "bad rides" (turbulence or wake), which is an external environmental condition forcing the descent request. This matches the category's focus on environmental disturbances altering flight paths. It is not Weather because "bad rides" are often treated as ambient aerodynamic or environmental conditions within this specific dataset's boundaries. It is not Human Factors because the primary initiator of the conflict was the sudden environmental disturbance that prompted the deviation request.
text: Narrative 1 - 'The rides were bad and Aircraft X asked to descend. My D-side called and requested control; but neglected to point the aircraft out to the underlying sector. I forgot about the shelf as well so I never double checked my D-side's work and I descended the aircraft. There is really no recommendations. The issues are a lack of competency on everyone's part. The D-side barely works as is; and I myself do not get enough quality time anymore because of the lack of traffic. Combined with a constant changing of sector configurations led to this issue.'

summary: During approach at approximately 1,000 feet, an aircraft encountered wake turbulence while flying at reference speed. The encounter triggered a momentary stick shaker warning before the crew recovered and continued the flight.
rationale: This incident is classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because the explicit hazard was wake turbulence generated by another aircraft. This maps directly to the "Wake Turbulence" repeating issue, representing a dynamic external aerodynamic hazard. It is not Weather because wake vortices are man-made aerodynamic phenomena rather than natural meteorological events. It is not Aircraft because the stick shaker functioned exactly as designed in response to the external aerodynamic disruption.
text: Narrative 1 - 'At approximately 1000 ft we got into some wake turbulence. We were on ref speed at the time. We got a 1 second or less stick shaker. We were still at Vref when I looked at the airspeed.'

summary: During a high-speed takeoff roll, an aircraft suffered a massive, direct bird strike to the Captain's windshield. The crew executed a high-speed rejected takeoff, utilizing reverse thrust and heavy braking, which required a subsequent tow back to the gate to allow the hot brakes to cool.
rationale: The Environment - Non Weather Related label fits perfectly because the emergency was caused by a sudden physical impact with wildlife in the operating environment. This aligns precisely with the "Bird/Wildlife Strikes & FOD" repeating issue. It is not Aircraft because the aircraft's internal systems were functioning normally until the external environmental impact forced the abort. It is not Procedure because the crew executed the emergency rejected takeoff flawlessly according to standard protocols.
text: Narrative 1 - 'At approximately 120 knots on takeoff at [a high elevation airport] we had a direct bird strike to the Captain's forward windscreen.  We were about 3000 feet down the runway and had 9000 feet remaining and were 35 knots below V1.  The impact was extremely loud and forceful so I rejected the takeoff. At approximately 100 knots with about 8000 feet of runway remaining I disengaged the auto-brakes to help avoid overheating the brakes.  I used reverse thrust; spoilers and the remaining runway to slow the airplane.  We exited the runway at the last high speed taxiway and brought the plane to a stop and did not set the brakes.  We requested fire/rescue support and ran all appropriate checklists.  Airport ops arrived quickly and notified us there was no fire or visible damage to the gear or tires; only some light smoke coming off the right gear.  Fire rescue arrived in about 10 minutes and set up cooling fans on the gear.  The brake temperatures initially climbed into the caution zone and then began to cool.  After coordinating with Maintenance and waiting for the brakes to cool we were towed back to the gate. After a maintenance check of the bird damage and brakes we were cleared and pressed on to our destination.'

summary: During a post-flight inspection, a flight crew discovered a 16-inch gash on the underside of the aircraft near the refueling door. The crew suspects the damage was caused by Foreign Object Debris (FOD) kicked up by the nose wheel during the takeoff or landing roll.
rationale: This report is classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because the damage was inflicted by Foreign Object Debris (FOD), which is an external, transient physical hazard in the operating environment. This maps directly to the criteria for external objects striking the aircraft. It is not Airport because the specific piece of FOD is a transient environmental object rather than a permanent defect in the runway pavement. It is not Aircraft because the structural damage was caused by external physical trauma rather than internal metal fatigue or system failure.
text:
Narrative 1 - 'During post flight and re-fueling at ZZZ crew noticed a gash in the aircraft skin just forward of the single point refueling door.  Gash was about 16 inches long and ½ inch wide and was symmetrical and squared at each end. The gash was directly in line and aft of the right nose gear.  It appears that FOD was flipped up by the nose wheel on takeoff or landing leading to damage.  However; no unusual noises or vibrations were noted by crew but takeoff and landings are noisy events.  Preflight inspection was performed by Pilot in Command (PIC) with no damage noted.  Both pilots performed pilot's walk around with no damage noted. Both pilots have a habit of either opening and inspecting fuel door or physically touching the door to ensure it is securely closed.  It would be virtually impossible to physically touch the refueling door and not notice the gash.'
Narrative 2 - 'Upon post-flight inspection in ZZZ; an indentation/gash of approximately one inch wide and 16 inches long was discovered on the underside of the aircraft. The cut did not appear to puncture deep enough to impact the pressure vessel. The length of the cut was in-line with the aircraft and appears to be in a prime location for an object (FOD) to have been kicked up by the nose-wheel on takeoff or landing. An independent walk around was performed by each crew member on the ground prior to flight; per SOPs. Due to the location of the strike near the single point re-fuel point; we are both certain the damage was not there prior to taxi-out. Upon discovery; a sweep of the runway in ZZZ was requested and no obvious object for the damage was found.'

summary: While flying a visual approach in mountainous terrain, an aircraft's radio altimeter called out 1,000 feet unexpectedly early due to a rising hill on the final approach path. The unexpected terrain proximity delayed the crew's configuration timing, leading to an unstable approach warning from the aircraft's automated reporting system.
rationale: This is classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because the unexpected proximity of physical terrain (a hill) directly interfered with the crew's altitude awareness and configuration timing. This aligns with the "External Obstacles & Terrain" repeating issue where natural topography intrudes on normal operations. It is not Chart Or Publication because the terrain is a physical reality of the environment, not a charting error. It is not Human Factors because the crew's delay was heavily induced by the physical topography triggering an unexpected automated callout.
text:
Narrative 1 - 'I was conducting a visual approach to [Runway] XXR backed up by the RNAV Y XXR. I was the Pilot Flying and the Captain was the Pilot Monitoring. We had briefed all the threats and the approach per the AOM prior to the TOD and were planning flaps full for the landing configuration. We were being vectored onto a base leg to join the final visually. I was faster than I should have been on the base for the distance we were out (180 selected speed I think) and so I had to delay calling for the flaps. I think the last altitude we were given was 8;000 feet on the base before we reported the field in sight and were cleared for the visual. The approach had been activated and I went to managed speed. We then set a lower altitude just above the altitude for the next fix after the one just ahead of us on the approach so I could use vertical speed to start down and stay on the path since the approach was not quite captured. I had armed the approach and it annunciated FINAL APP as we were joining the final and the aircraft continued descending on the path. I was using the auto pilot for lateral and vertical NAV until established on final. We extended the gear prior to joining the final to help with slowing the aircraft to flap deployment speeds. The vector had put us in closer than I had anticipated and I had also started slowing later than I should have. The speed did not come back very quickly; possibly because of a tailwind shifting to a headwind as were turning final; so I had to disconnect the autopilot and arrest the descent to get the aircraft slowed for flap deployment and also to prevent an overspeed. Both the Captain and I noticed the RA was close to 1;000 [feet] as we deployed final flaps; and our speed was still well above approach speed; but we were VMC and our assessment was that we had been in final configuration prior to 1;000 AGL. We continued the approach and were stable at 500 [feet] so we landed out of the approach within the required criteria. We were at the gate when the ACARS printed a Self-Assessment Flight Report. We weren't surprised to see Y for speed and descent; but we were surprised to see a Y for flap change. We discussed it; and although we were unstable at 1;000 [feet]; we felt that we had been configured and thought the report generated a flap change Y at 0974 RA because the terrain rises to the north and the spot elevations are around 300-600 feet higher than the airport elevation near where we selected final configuration. Regardless; the final configuration selection was closer to 1;000 AGL than it should have been and we decided we would file an report just in case. Normally; I call for gear down and managed speed at 2;000 AGL so as to have plenty of time to reach final configuration prior to 1;000 AGL; but I missed that cue because I was in the yellow due to being tighter and faster than I had anticipated; and I didn't realize it because I was busy trying to get back in the green slowing and turning final and descending to stay on the path. This is also a case where my experience exceeded my performance due to being new to being back in the right seat and not flying much in September. I also didn't say anything about being in the yellow; and so I didn't get as much help as I could have used. I also failed to brief that we may need to be configured on base depending on where the base leg is and to say something if I don't ask for the gear down by 2;000 AGL. The other factor was conducting a visual approach to a high-altitude airport where AGL altitudes happen at much higher MSL altitudes than were are used to and neither I nor the Captain had been into ZZZ in a long time. I will more thoroughly brief visual approaches regarding the gates I want for configuration and speed changes that provide plenty of margin for a stabilized approach.'
Narrative 2 - 'As we turned to intercept final and configure for landing with a near 0 VSI; a hill on final gave us an unexpected 1;000 ft. radio alt call. We where visual and comfortable with continuing the approach due to distance. I recall being fully configuration when the second 1;000 foot reading on final and on speed at 500 ft. We failed to slow down in time on base leg not taking into effect high alt difference and ground speed. We did not brief the ground speed difference and the delayed slow down or terrain around the base turn. Haven't been to ZZZ in years.'

summary: While pulling into a gate using an automated parking system, a flight crew was unexpectedly signaled to stop by a marshaller standing off to the side. The marshaller halted the aircraft because a catering truck from an adjacent gate had encroached into the safety zone, creating a collision risk.
rationale: This incident is classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because the hazard was a transient external object (a catering truck) physically intruding into the aircraft's immediate path. This maps to the "External Obstacles & Terrain" and transient object criteria. It is not Airport because the hazard was a mobile vehicle temporarily in the wrong place rather than a permanent, poorly designed gate infrastructure. It is not Equipment / Tooling because the catering truck did not break or malfunction; its mere physical presence in the environment was the hazard.
text: Narrative 1 - 'We were pulling into gate XX at ZZZ. Had engines at idle the entire time. The accu-park system was on and giving us proper guidance. With about 40 feet remaining on the sign; I noticed out of the corner of my eye a marshaller standing off to the left giving me the stop sign. We stopped with about 35 feet showing left on the parking system. We were getting conflicting information from the marshaller and the parking system. The marshaller was giving us the shutdown engine sign; which we did while querying ramp/ops if they had any information. After the marshaller plugged in; he said we would have hit a catering truck which was off to the right. The safety zone was clear when we were making the turn in and the FO (First Officer) said we were clear as we were nearing the gate. There was an aircraft at [the adjacent gate] with a catering truck at door 2; which could have moved giving the marshaller concern. I have 2 safety concerns about this evolution. One; if there was a truck in the safety zone; the system should have given us a STOP signal. The second; I barely noticed the marshaller giving us the stop signal; as he was off to the left and I was concentrating on the parking system. If in fact there was a chance of hitting anything; he should have been much more visible about getting our attention or been able to turn off the parking system. After we towed the rest of the way to the gate; I talked with him on the interphone and stated these concerns. He stated the shutoff for the parking was on the other side of the jetbridge; and he understood my concerns.'

summary: Shortly after crossing FL340, an aircraft encountered heavy wake turbulence from a larger jet 10 miles ahead, causing an uncommanded right roll that disconnected the autopilot. The pilot successfully manually leveled the wings and recommended that ATC provide better wake advisories for heavy jets.
rationale: This falls under Environment - Non Weather Related because the primary hazard was a severe wake turbulence encounter that physically displaced the aircraft from its flight path. This matches the "Wake Turbulence" repeating issue perfectly. It is not Weather because wake turbulence is an artificial aerodynamic disturbance created by another aircraft, not a natural meteorological event. It is not Aircraft because the autopilot disconnect was a designed safety response to the external aerodynamic upset.
text: Narrative 1 - 'We took off from TEB [and] we were cleared to climb to FL400. About to cross FL340 we experienced a very heavy wake turbulence trying to roll the plane to the right; so the auto pilot was kicked off. I tried to keep wings level and in about 15 seconds we were out of it. On the TCAS we had a plane on same airway; 10 to 11 miles ahead of us; and that's why I think air traffic controllers [should] advise pilots when a heavy jet is ahead [of] a lighter jet to avoid these situations.'

summary: After flying a coupled ILS approach to minimums, the flight crew broke out of the clouds to find they were 100 feet to the left of the runway centerline. The pilot manually took over to land the aircraft visually and recommended fixing the ILS critical area.
rationale: This is classified as Equipment / Tooling because the root cause was a degraded or bending signal from the ground-based ILS equipment. This aligns with the expanded definition treating ATC and ground navigation hardware anomalies as generic equipment failures. It is not classified as ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because this dataset specifically routes general ILS hardware performance issues into the broader tooling category. It is not Aircraft because the autopilot functioned correctly by following the erroneous signal it received.
text: Narrative 1 - 'We shot an ILS Approach to 31R at DAL down to 100 ft above mins. When we broke out we were 100 ft left of the runway. We were coupled until minimums and everything was lined up. I piloted over to land under visual conditions. Fix the ILS critical area.'

summary: A controller was unable to reach an aircraft flying at the Minimum IFR Altitude (MIA) due to poor transmitter frequency coverage in that specific sector. The aircraft was forced to climb on its own to regain radio contact and receive a reroute around terrain.
rationale: This incident fits Equipment / Tooling because the primary hazard was the physical limitation and poor coverage of the ATC radio transmitter hardware. This maps directly to the inclusion of ATC and ground navigation hardware limitations under the generic equipment label. It is not Airspace Structure because the physical boundaries of the sector were not the hazard, but rather the lack of functional radio equipment. It is not Company Policy because the issue is a physical hardware constraint rather than a management directive.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I took airspace from previous controller. Aircraft X wanted to stay low to limit oxygen and couldn't take longer routing to save on gas. Previous controller let Aircraft X stay at the MIA (Minimum IFR Altitude) per request. Previous controller told Aircraft X he was going to need to climb to avoid terrain; which he acknowledged. In the briefing I was told about an Aircraft X needing to climb prior to terrain. As soon as I took the airspace; I asked Aircraft X if he could take a reroute to avoid terrain. Aircraft X said Center transmissions were starting to breakup. I tried multiple times to relay to Aircraft X to get Aircraft X to either change to a frequency he could hear me on or climb to 11;000 feet. Aircraft X unfortunately was unreachable through relays or ground transmitters. The transmitters were unable to reach Aircraft X at 10;000 feet prior despite MIAs being 10;000 feet. Aircraft X eventually climbed on his own to 11;000 feet after flying through an 11;000 foot MIA for several miles. I was able to relay through another aircraft and get Aircraft X to change to a frequency I could talk to him on. Frequency coverage of one frequency at lower altitudes in this sector is poor when at MIAs. Unless more transmitter sights are put in; the coverage at low altitude will continue to be spotty. Changing aircraft's frequency at low altitudes over sooner would help due to another frequency having better coverage in some spots between sectors.'

summary: A pilot noted that the JeppFD-Pro Electronic Flight Bag (EFB) application incorrectly routes the MGM VOR to a similarly named heliport in Texas. The reporter requested that the software tool be updated to default to standard identifiers rather than names.
rationale: The Equipment / Tooling label applies because the hazard is a software logic or search function error within a specific EFB application used as a cockpit tool. This aligns with the "Electronic Flight Bag (EFB) & Dispatch Applications" repeating issue. It is not Chart Or Publication because the issue is the dynamic search algorithm of the software tool rather than a misprinted static chart. It is not Software and Automation because that category is typically reserved for the aircraft's internal, hardwired Flight Management System (FMS).
text: Narrative 1 - 'In EFB JeppFD-Pro; our route included MGM VOR.  When I enter it in the route section; it recognizes it as a Heliport (TS23) near Austin TX. It appears the heliport is also named MGM with TS23 as its identifier.  Jepp needs to either change the name of the heliport; or have the JeppFD-Pro default to identifiers rather than to names.'

summary: A flight crew received a Pre-Departure Clearance (PDC) that confusingly listed both their filed SID and a second, unexpected SID. This software generation error caused significant confusion during departure when ATC expected the crew to fly a different route.
rationale: This is classified as Equipment / Tooling because the root cause was a data generation glitch within the PDC dispatch software tool used to transmit clearances. This falls under the "Electronic Flight Bag (EFB) & Dispatch Applications" criteria. It is not Procedure because the crew and controllers were reacting to a flawed automated output rather than skipping a required step. It is not Human Factors because the confusion was systematically induced by the defective clearance generation equipment.
text:
Narrative 1 - 'We were departing DFW through a PDC (pre departure clearance) request. The PDC showed our filed flight plan SID (DALL2) and routing. We entered the DALL2 SID into our FMS. We then taxied to the assigned runway 35L. Upon departure we were directed to go to a fix which was not on the DALL2 departure. We informed departure control that we did not have the fix on the DALL2 SID. The controller then gave us a heading; which we complied with; and handed us off to another controller. We confirmed to the second controller that we were on the DALL2 SID. The second departure controller directed us to fly several vectors; all of which we complied with; and he then cleared us to SWB; our expected waypoint. The balance of the flight continued normally.We subsequently learned that the PDC also showed the MRSSH1 SID. I believe that having two SIDs on the PDC is a source of confusion and a safety issue. We have never had two SIDs on a PDC clearance previously.'
Narrative 2 - 'Upon receiving our Pre Departure Clearance (PDC) it contained our filed flight plan and SID. It also contained another SID. This caused some confusion upon departure.I think the PDC format is unclear and confusing.'

summary: A dispatcher evaluating alternate airports realized that the SABRE dispatch software failed to display a critical runway closure NOTAM that was active on the FAA website. The software's failure to pull the data could have led to an aircraft attempting to land on a closed runway with active construction.
rationale: This report is classified as Equipment / Tooling because the hazard originated from a data retrieval failure within the SABRE dispatch software tool. This perfectly matches the "Dispatch & Flight Planning Software Failures" and EFB application glitch definitions. It is not Chart Or Publication because the NOTAM was correctly published by the FAA; the airline's specific software tool simply failed to ingest it. It is not Company Policy because the omission was a software tool failure rather than a deliberate management decision.
text: Narrative 1 - 'While the flight was holding due to thunderstorms at ZZZ; the Dispatcher began to evaluate closer alternates. The Dispatcher checked SPS NOTAMs in SABRE and noted the Tower was NOTAMed closed but there was no runway closure NOTAMs in SABRE. Upon further evaluation and checking NOTAMs on the FAA website; the Dispatcher saw that there in fact was a NOTAM in effect for runway 15R/33L CLOSED that was not in SABRE. The runway was closed with work in progress. The NOTAM was sent to the NOTAM Management System but was never published into SABRE. Flight Planning published the NOTAM after becoming aware of the discrepancy. This could have been a dangerous situation if the flight attempted to land on this closed runway with possible work equipment on the runway; tower closed and at night time.'

summary: A technician discovered that a nitrogen servicing cart was loaded with bottles painted green, which is the industry standard color for oxygen. The technician warned that this mislabeled maintenance tooling could easily lead to catastrophic aircraft damage or fatalities if a system was serviced with the wrong gas.
rationale: This falls under Equipment / Tooling because the core hazard is improperly labeled and painted maintenance servicing gear. This aligns exactly with the "Maintenance & Servicing Tooling" repeating issue. It is not Human Factors because the technician successfully identified the hazard, which was created by the physical state of the equipment itself. It is not Aircraft because the dangerous bottles were external ground support equipment, not integrated aircraft components.
text: Narrative 1 - 'While procuring a nitrogen cart to perform aircraft servicing I noticed that the nitrogen bottles loaded into the cart were painted green whereas they are usually an off white color. I immediately suspected that the cart was loaded with oxygen bottles and had to do a quick search to determine that the bottles were in fact nitrogen but they were painted green. The bottles were labeled with a small nitrogen sticker that was on the underside of the bottle. Please see the attached picture. This is a very dangerous condition that would lead to extreme aircraft damage and or human injury/death. A technician could accidentally mistake the nitrogen cart for oxygen and service and oxygen system with nitrogen. Also; a technician could become used to green nitrogen bottles and accidentally service a tire or strut with oxygen. I recommend that these bottles either be taken out of service and repainted; or wrapped with a 360 degree label stating its contents.'

summary: An employee operating a hybrid aircraft pushback tug experienced a total loss of steering due to a drive-by-wire computer failure. The operator had to restart the tug to regain control, noting that this equipment failure could cause severe damage or injury if it occurred while towing an aircraft.
rationale: This is classified as Equipment / Tooling because the safety event involved the catastrophic mechanical and software failure of a piece of ground support equipment (a tug). This maps directly to the "Ground Support & Cabin Equipment (GSE) Failures" definition. It is not Airport because the failure was of a specific mobile tool used for aircraft servicing, rather than the airport's pavement infrastructure. It is not Procedure because the employee was operating the tug normally when it unpredictably malfunctioned.
text: Narrative 1 - 'After moving Aircraft from hangar spot to the gate; I was pulling away from aircraft; stopped to let my partner in tug; and then proceeded to leave gate. Had to stop because steering in the tug locked up; could not turn steering wheel. This tug is a Hybrid with drive by wire technology. I was able to regain steering by turning off tug and recycling the computer. The problem came up again some 50 feet later when I stopped again. Had this happened while towing an aircraft there would have been major damage to an aircraft as well as injury to employees.'

summary: During a fuel tank inspection, a maintenance worker experienced nausea because the ventilation hoses were deteriorated and leaked jet fuel fumes into the hangar. The worker was unable to measure the fume levels because the calibration gas for the multi-gas detector tool was empty.
rationale: This incident fits Equipment / Tooling because the hazards were directly caused by defective and unmaintained maintenance gear (deteriorated ventilation hoses and an uncalibrated gas detector). This matches the "Maintenance & Servicing Tooling" sub-category perfectly. It is not Environment - Non Weather Related because the toxic fumes were a byproduct of a specific maintenance task and broken venting tools rather than a random external environmental factor. It is not Company Policy because the specific failure point was the broken physical equipment rather than a broader administrative directive.
text: Narrative 1 - 'A Cessna CE-560XL aircraft was in for A-Check inspection and fuel tanks were drained and opened for Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) inspection. Fuel tank ventilation system installed to vent tanks to outside.  However; the hoses used; stretched from bay #3 to west garage door. Hoses become trip hazard and also do not seal properly; venting fumes into work area. Hoses appear to need maintenance due to deterioration.  Attempted to use the Sirius multi gas detector to see what level Jet A fumes were at; but was unable to calibrate unit due to calibration gasses being empty.  I have been in this area of the Hangar since XA:30pm and I am experiencing Nausea and Headaches. I recommend to install overhead vent system so that vent lines are not on floor and fumes vent out; or install an inline blower into the system now in use to provide positive airflow to push the fumes out of the hangar.  Venting open fuel tanks for NDT. Excess fumes from fuel tank venting system.'

summary: Shortly after takeoff, the flight crew detected an acrid smell and unusual smoke on the flight deck, prompting the First Officer to don an oxygen mask and the crew to run the smoke checklist. The fumes were later traced to a leak in the Auxiliary Power Unit (APU) system contaminating the air conditioning, which caused the crew to require medical treatment.
rationale: The Equipment / Tooling label applies here because the inciting event was a mechanical failure of auxiliary aircraft equipment (the APU) that required the use of emergency oxygen tools. This aligns with the "Auxiliary Aircraft Equipment (APU/ECS)" failures covered under the broadened equipment definition. It is not classified as Aircraft because this specific dataset isolates auxiliary environmental and power systems into the general tooling/equipment bucket. It is not Company Policy, despite the mention of local hospital protocols, because the root hazard that initiated the emergency was the physical equipment leak.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Just after rotation an acrid smell was detected followed shortly by unusual colored smoke/fumes mostly on the FO's side of the airplane. The FO immediately put on O2 mask because he didn't feel normal. On downwind; I called the FA's and number 1 reported that she didn't smell anything. We performed the QRC Smoke/Avionics smoke/fumes soon after detecting the acrid smell and this lessened the impact. After gate arrival; I was informed by flight attendants they were experiencing nausea and headaches plus the FO wasn't feeling well either. Oddly enough; I'm not aware of any passengers requesting medical assistance. After deplaning; FA's and FO's condition got worse so we requested medical assistance from airport EMT's. I know the EMT's administered meds to FO as well a IV bag plus the EMT's evaluated all crew then they released us to go to hospital. Since event occurred on the weekend; there seemed to be some confusion on what local facility could see and treat the crew for their medical condition. Also; local operations couldn't provide transportation for entire crew to the hospital. This caused some delay in getting the crew to a local hospital for treatment. The flight [crew] was taken by ambulance to hospital and I'm not sure who took FA's to a different hospital. For some reason the FA's were taken to a different hospital than the flight [crew] even though I requested we all go to same facility. Thanks to local chief pilot for making things happen in regards to getting us to local hospital for immediate care because I eventually started experiencing a headache and facial flushing. I don't believe I was functioning at a high level and this point. I was informed a day later that there was a leak in the APU system allowing fluid to get into aircraft air conditioning system. ZZZ needs to have a protocol in place for medical events that occur when their normal medical facility is closed. It shouldn't be up to the crew to determine what is a appropriate medical facility to go to for treatment.'

summary: A flight crew was forced to delay pushback because the ramp crew possessed four consecutive broken or uncharged communication headsets. The Ramp Supervisor attempted to pressure the Captain into using hand signals, but the Captain refused until a working wired headset tool was located.
rationale: This is classified as Equipment / Tooling because the operational delay and safety conflict were caused by a lack of functional ground support communication hardware (headsets). This maps perfectly to the "Ground Support & Cabin Equipment (GSE) Failures" repeating issue. It is not Procedure because the Captain correctly followed the manual by refusing to push back without the required equipment. It is not Company Policy because the airline's policy explicitly prohibited the hand-signal pushback; the failure was the physical lack of maintained tools on the ramp.
text: Narrative 1 - 'At push time we were unable to contact Ramp via headset. The Ramp Agent came to window and told us headset was INOP and did we want to use hand signals. I advised him we could not do that and that they would need to get a headset. Tried #2 still didn't work. Tried #3 still didn't work. Ramp Supervisor again asked us to just use hand signals. I told him we could not do that per the flight and ground operations manuals. He said 'Well then you will just have to sit there.' Tried #4; still didn't work. Asked them to try service interphone jack to ensure it was not an issue with the aircraft. Also asked if they had a regular wired headset and was told no they did not. I explained again to the Ramp Supervisor that pushing back without a headset was not an option. He again told me 'You will just have to sit there then;' and walked away. At that point I had the Operations Agent bring the jetway back to the aircraft so we could discuss the situation face to face to create a shared mental model. I explained to the Ramp Agents and the Ramp Supervisor that the flight and ground operations manuals both prohibit pushing back without a headset unless there is lightning or the aircraft interphone panel is INOP; (which would require an MEL). There is a discrepancy between the flight and ground operations manuals in that the flight manual does allow pushback without headset if ALL headsets are out of service (I think the flight manual should be brought in agreement with the ground operations manual). I also asked them how they had been pushing back aircraft all day with no operative headsets. He said the other Captains were just letting them use hand signals. At that point an adjacent gate was pushing with a headset so I told them to go get that headset when they were done. They returned with a wired headset which worked perfectly so we closed up the aircraft and pushed back. My concern is that the Ramp repeatedly tried to pressure us into accepting a risk that the company has chosen not to accept. The flight and ground operations manuals are very clear as to when the risk of pushing without a headset is acceptable. Our policies on No Headset Pushback should be reiterated to the flight and ground operations manuals. Also; Ground Ops should be reminded to ensure that the headsets are turned off between turns (several of the INOP headsets were just out of charge) and to treat the headsets with more care. (It is common to see the headsets tossed onto the tug). All stations should also have sufficient backup wired headsets that are well maintained to ensure a working headset is always available. I would suggest each gate or pushback tug be equipped with a backup wired headset. Perhaps in a weatherproof case mounted on the tug or jetway. I agree with the company assessment that pushing back without a headset simply because the headset is INOP is not an acceptable risk. I dislike doing it even when there is lightning; but I understand the reasoning behind it in reducing a greater risk to the ramp agents.'

summary: During descent at night, a flight crew realized their entire overhead panel was completely dark and inoperable. They discovered that maintenance had applied the wrong MEL for the defect, bypassing the correct MEL which strictly prohibited night operations.
rationale: This is classified as MEL because the primary safety hazard was the misapplication of a deferral code by maintenance, allowing the aircraft to be dispatched illegally for night operations. This perfectly matches the "Misapplication of Deferral Codes" repeating issue. It is not classified as Aircraft because the hardware defect was known prior to dispatch but improperly categorized. It is not Procedure because the critical failure was the specific legal and administrative deferral coding rather than a tactical checklist execution error.
text: Narrative 1 - 'During the arrival phase of flight. We both noticed the entire overhead panel was completely dark. As I was the first officer attempted to adjust the overhead lights. This was unsuccessful. We then reviewed the MEL. When we reviewed the MEL section prior to our 1st departure; we were under the impression that one portion of the cockpit lighting system was not working. It wasn't until the descent phase of this flight that we realized the entire overhead panel was completely dark. After we reviewed the MEL section again; it was determined that the wrong MEL was applied. The MEL below the one used carried the condition that the aircraft couldn't be flown at night. I can't imagine how anyone could see the condition of the overhead panel and apply MEL 33-11-XY. The technician that responded in ZZZ was able to fix the problem in ten minutes. This aircraft was in maintenance in ZZZ1 and ZZZ2. Why wasn't the simple fix accomplished on one of these visits instead of passing a potentially dangerous condition to the next guy?If there was an emergency or QRH procedure at night in this aircraft it could have had dire consequences.'

summary: An aircraft dispatched with an MEL for a center tank pump low-pressure warning light experienced a severe fuel imbalance enroute. The crew had to run fuel leak checklists and divert for an overweight landing, later suspecting the deferred pump was actually failing to feed fuel to the engine.
rationale: This falls under MEL because an active deferral masked a more severe mechanical limitation, resulting in significant operational degradation and a diversion. This aligns directly with the "Operational Degradation due to MEL" classification criteria. It is not Aircraft because the issue stems from the known, deferred component's unpredicted secondary effects rather than a completely unprompted new system failure. It is not Procedure because the crew diligently followed the checklists based on the symptoms presented by the legally deferred system.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Shortly after climbing through 10;000 feet and completing the Climb Check; we received a Fuel Imbalance caution. This was confirmed by the left fuel gauge indicating an imbalance of greater than 1;000 pounds and increasing. At that point; we had over 15;000 pounds of fuel in the center tank with all fuel pumps on and fuel crossfeed valve closed. We continued the climb to FL230 where we leveled to analyze the problem. During the climb; the First Officer opened up the QRH to the fuel section. Due to the rate of fuel loss from the left tank; we went to the Engine Fuel Leak Checklist. In the meantime; I advised the Flight Attendants not to start a service; as we were troubleshooting a problem. While following the checklist; we called back to the Flight Attendants to advise them of the problem; and to look at the aft left wing and engine area to see if they could detect a mist coming from either. It was already nighttime and they reported not seeing any mist emanating from the wing or engine. We dispatched with MEL Left Center Tank Pump Low Pressure Warning Light System.While following the checklist we surmised that an engine fuel leak could not be confirmed; so we called Dispatch via ARINC to connect with the Dispatcher and Maintenance Control. They were advised of the situation and a consensus was made to divert. I reminded Maintenance and Dispatch that we would be an overweight landing. By that time; the fuel imbalance grew to approximately 2;000 pounds. By our best estimates; it appeared that we were losing fuel in addition to the normal burn. I advised the Flight Attendants and Passengers of the situation and that we were diverting.The First Officer and I coordinated the arrival and approach and furthermore [advised ATC] due to the unknown nature of the problem. Enroute I turned off the center pumps and was able to get the wings in balance by the time we landed. I also noticed at that point that the fuel burn was normal during the balancing; and that the center tank pumps being off seemed to normalize the burn. We determined with the center pumps off the situation did not get aggravated. The landing was smooth and uneventful. After clearing the runway; we stopped the aircraft. The Fire Department looked over the aircraft and did not see any leaks from the engines or wings. We subsequently taxied to the gate without incident.A logbook entry was made for the fuel imbalance and overweight landing. Subsequently; Scheduling called with a reassignment for both pilots to fly one more leg; which we both acknowledged; but advised it would be a while after all parties were debriefed. A conference call was made to the Chief Pilot on Call; Maintenance Control and Dispatch to brief them on the situation. The Flight Deck Crew asked to be removed from the new assignment; which we were.Some takeaways from this situation. Both the First Officer and I had long days leading up to this assignment although we both felt fit to fly this leg. It made for a fairly long day and a busy divert. Upon further reflection after this event; I believe that the left center fuel pump may have actually had low output; regardless of the MEL; and that it may have not been able to feed center tank fuel to the left engine; if I'm analyzing the fuel schematic correctly. The pump in question did have an electrical draw when switched on. If this was the case; we should have run the Fuel Imbalance Checklist first. Although it would have lead us to the Engine Fuel Leak Checklist; had we taken the 'Fuel Leak Not Suspected' branch; and if in fact the center pump pressure or plumbing did not allow center fuel to get to the left engine; we might have been able to continue to destination with normal balancing and opening the crossfeed valve. This was not obvious at the time.'

summary: A flight crew reviewing their release for an ETOPS flight noticed an unresolved APU shutdown history that lacked a required verification flight or MEL deferral. The crew refused the aircraft, and it was discovered the plane had illegally flown at least four ETOPS segments with the undocumented defect.
rationale: Based on the updated reference guide logic, this is classified as MEL because the root cause was the failure of maintenance and dispatch to apply a required Minimum Equipment List deferral or verification flight for a known defect. The administrative omission allowed an unairworthy aircraft into ETOPS revenue service. It is not Company Policy because the specific failure point was the missing mechanical deferral documentation rather than a broad scheduling or management pressure tactic. It is not Procedure because the critical error was the legal dispatch categorization of the aircraft's status prior to crew assignment.
text: Narrative 1 - 'We were originally supposed to have nose number XXX/XXXX (737-800). Looking over the release I saw an entry that the APU had shutdown on [date] in LAX. It was cleared but as I was departing on an ETOPS segment I wanted some further information. I wanted to know why it had shutdown and if a verification flight was needed; and if so; had it been performed? I was patched through to Maintenance Control via Dispatch. It was discovered that a verification flight should have been performed for the APU to be re-established as on demand. Otherwise it should have been deferred under an MEL that would have required the APU to be left running until exiting ETOPS airspace until a cold soak start had been accomplished. As a result we were swapped to a different tail number which is another 737-800 and departed. It was discovered that the airplane flew at least 4 ETOPS segments from the initial problem to when we were assigned the aircraft.'

summary: An aircraft was dispatched with multiple conflicting MEL deferrals involving the APU and the Air Driven Pump (ADP), unnecessarily knocking out backup electrical systems. The crew returned to the gate to have the confusing and improperly applied deferrals amended by maintenance control to ensure a safe configuration for weather avoidance.
rationale: This is classified as MEL because the hazard was created by the improper combination and misapplication of multiple deferral codes, leading to an unsafe loss of system redundancy. This matches the criteria for misapplication and operational degradation due to MELs. It is not Manuals because the specific conflict arose from the active legal dispatch deferrals applied to that exact flight, rather than a generic flight operations handbook. It is not Company Policy, despite mention of schedule pressure, because the core technical resolution required amending specific deferral codes.
text:
Narrative 1 - 'Our flight had the following deferrals. 21-XX-XX; 29-XX-XX-XX; 49-XX-XX. The 29-XX-XX-XX deferral required the ADP (Air Driven Pump) to be turned on at certain times. The switch was turned on and we had got the C HYD DEM PUMP advisory message and the PRESS light on the hydraulic panel. We called the Local Maintenance on the radio; and found the Air Pump CB (Circuit Breaker) was out. They advised us to push it in; but it didn't solve the problem; and advised us to come back to the gate. When we returned to the gate the maintenance guys said that [Maintenance] was going to defer the whole ADP under 29-XX-XX; which knocks out the HMG (Hydraulic Motor Generator). With the APU deferred you can't have the HMG deferred if it's required. The APU was completely fine; but deferred because the ADP valve was leaking the bleed air; according to the mechanics. They stated that they have been asking [Maintenance] to change the valve for 2 weeks. On our conference with Dispatch; [Maintenance] and the duty pilot they wound up amending the APU deferral to pneumatics only. So; there wasn't a conflict between the deferrals.Once again I believe that this is a case of improper deferrals and undue pressure for crews to take the aircraft to maintain schedule integrity. I understand that the HMG may not have been required per se for our short flight; but when the APU and the ADP deferred we were down 2 back up electrical systems and flying through a line of thunderstorms and forecasted lower ceilings. Why would a perfectly good system be deferred just because another system that can be fixed hasn't been? I think we should be erring on the side of safety; and acting out of an abundance of caution in the current climate.'
Narrative 2 - 'Upon taxi out when complying with DDG (Dispatch Deviation Guide) 29-XX-XX-XX we got an ADP (Air Driven Pump) press light and an EICAS message was generated. This was not addressed or acceptable per 29-XX-XX-XX. An inoperative ADP was improperly deferred in conjunction with the APU. After gate return 29-XX-XX-XX was amended. Additionally; the APU was deferred with 49-XX-XX. This was due to a faulty air regulation valve as we were told by Maintenance. There seemed to be a conflict within the Deferral regarding necessity of the HMG (Hydraulic Motor Generator) regardless of if we were emergency operations or not.Multiple Deferral- items with multiple (excessive) effectivity made it confusing as to what is required and when. Maintenance Control wanted to argue about the applicability and at one point tried to tell us the RAT (Ram Air) would power the HMG. This did not instill confidence in us that anything they were doing was correct. There needs to be a little more clarity as to what equipment is required when if we are dealing with multiple deferred items with regard to emergency vs non emergency operations. The deferred items were amended and the aircraft was released with the APU deferral was 49-XX-XX minus pneumatic air and the ADP was completely deferred with 29-XX-XX-XX.'

summary: During taxi, a pilot discovered a high stage bleed valve failed closed and requested a specific MEL that allowed flight in icing conditions. Maintenance Control refused and applied a much more restrictive MEL disabling the entire left bleed system, which forced the crew to descend to 13,000 feet to avoid unforecast icing.
rationale: The MEL label applies because the core of the safety event is a dispute over the selection and operational consequences of a specific Minimum Equipment List deferral. The application of the overly restrictive MEL severely degraded the aircraft's operational capabilities and pushed it into an icing hazard. It is not Weather because the unforecast icing was only a threat due to the specific degraded configuration dictated by the MEL. It is not Company Policy because the conflict centered entirely on the mechanical and legal constraints of the deferral code itself.
text: Narrative 1 - 'During taxi out; I had to gate return for a high stage bleed valve failed closed that I discovered during my initial taxi out. I spoke to Maintenance Control about the issue and they refused to apply MEL 36-XX-A which would have allowed flight in icing conditions. Instead; he insisted on using MEL 36-XY-A disabling the entire left engine bleed system. After much discussion and delay and trying to explain my concerns about how MEL 36-XY-A which locked closed the PRSOV (bleed valve) limiting our flight altitude to FL250 and to avoid icing conditions; not to mention flying over high terrain on a single bleed source. I explained my concerns to no avail. The only reason that I accepted the aircraft was because I believed it to be a legal dispatch and I could operate the flight safely. Also; I did not want to delay my customers any further.From a systems perspective; this MEL 36-XY-A was unnecessary and provided for many more flight restrictions. Contract Maintenance locked out the PRSOV as called for in the MEL; but after pushback and engine start; I found the left engine bleed valve still open. This necessitated another gate return creating more stress for my customers and crew. The Contract Maintenance Technician made an error in the lock-out procedure and corrected it after gate return.I have [over 30] years flying experience at this airline; [over 15 years] on the 737; and I have had this same high stage valve failure before. There is no reason in my professional opinion that this aircraft could not have operated under MEL 36-XX-A only locking closed the high stage valve.MEL 36-XY-A requires operations avoiding known and forecast icing. When release was amended with this MEL; Dispatch advised me that weather was clear with no known; forecast or reported icing conditions.As we approached ZZZ at FL250; we were approaching cloud layers that constituted icing conditions. ATC then reported MOD rime icing. We descended to remain clear of clouds and icing. We had to descend to 13;000 feet to avoid all the icing.I know that I hold joint responsibility with Dispatch in this regard; but due to the very heavy work load I was experiencing working with Maintenance Control for this departure; I relied on Dispatch for the weather evaluation.'

summary: An aircraft was dispatched with an MEL for the cockpit door electronic lock, which required the crew to use the mechanical deadbolt in flight.
