He then stated; I can see you moving but can't hear you and instructed to begin takeoff roll or exit the runway.We taxied off the runway without ever powering up the engines to a takeoff setting. Once clear; we switched back to ground and the radios worked fine. Ground gave us the tower freq for runway 5R and to again taxi up to and hold short of 5L. We switched and tower could now hear our transmissions. Holding short of the runway a second time another aircraft ended up with the same issue and was given a takeoff clearance; but tower could not hear their response.Tower then instructed them to change frequencies to the 5L frequency which was the inoperative frequency for transmitting to tower. I corrected tower that 119.3 was the frequency we were on when we had that same experience and suggested he advise the aircraft to switch to 127.45; the 5R frequency. The other aircraft switched frequencies and proceeded to takeoff. We waited a few minutes and then completed our takeoff and flight. Once enroute; we back tracked the digital departure ATIS and on the last page it stated to use 127.45 to contact tower. The controller was just simply transmitting on both frequencies; but only listening to one and both aircraft must have missed the NOTAM in the departure ATIS on the last page.'

summary: During a visual approach, an aircraft's autopilot pitched the nose down excessively to follow a false, erroneous glide slope signal from the ILS. The First Officer disconnected the autopilot and leveled off after receiving a low altitude alert from Tower.
rationale: This incident is classified as ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because the root cause was a faulty ILS signal from the ground navigation facility. This is explicitly covered by the "ILS / glideslope anomalies" and "false or unreliable glideslope signals" repeating issues. It is not classified as Aircraft because the aircraft's autopilot was correctly following the data it received; the error originated from the external navigation facility. It is not Software and Automation because it was a physical signal anomaly rather than an FMS navigation database error.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Visual Approach with both autopilots on; (CAT III showing) at approximately 3;000 feet aircraft excessively pitched nose down approximately 7-10 degrees following a false glide slope. Pilot flying (First Officer) disconnected the autopilot and leveled off at 2;000 feet as Tower called a low altitude alert. The aircraft glide slope recovered and showed a correct indication from the final approach fix to touchdown.  was written up and documented after shut down. Aircraft did not descend below 2000 feet prior to the final approach fix. Final Approach Fix altitude is 2;000 feet.'

summary: A local controller observed a false target on the ASDE-X surface radar that improperly tagged a city operations vehicle with the callsign of a departed aircraft. The controller noted that these recurring ghost targets create significant safety risks during low-visibility operations.
rationale: This is classified as ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because the primary issue is a software or hardware failure within the ATC facility's ASDE-X surface movement radar. This exactly matches the "ASDE-X / surface automation errors" and "phantom primary targets on radar" repeating issues in the reference guide. It is not classified as Airport because the hazard is a failure of the ATC tracking equipment, not a physical deficiency of the airport pavement or lighting. It is not Equipment / Tooling because the vehicle itself did not malfunction; the radar system tracking it did.
text: Narrative 1 - 'While working the Local Control position during the night time session; I noticed a call sign of an aircraft; moving along the center taxiway in between runways. I looked and observed that it wasn't an aircraft that matched up with the target. It was a vehicle. A discussion ensued between myself and a supervisor regarding the previous events concerning false targets popping up on the ASDEX [Airport Surface Detection Equipment; Model X] matching up with a previous departure. The target would end up being a city ops vehicle patrolling and carrying out their regular duties.  We've also noted that when it has occurred on other occasions; the educated guess was that the city ops vehicle was holding somewhere west of taxiway hotel in the dirt when the false target began to float across the ASDEX. On this particular occasion; the target didn't just float across the screen; it actually tagged up with the city ops vehicle and continued to keep the tag of Aircraft X. The Supervisor directed me to ask the city ops vehicle to turn off their squitter for 1 minute and to then turn it back on. The city ops vehicle complied and the Aircraft X tag remained attached to the vehicle. The only time the tag disappeared from the vehicle was when the city ops veered off into the safety area to avoid exiting aircraft who just landed. While the vehicle drove east along Taxiway Hotel; the Aircraft X tag remained connected to the vehicle. The obvious safety issue is when the weather is not VFR and we aren't able to confirm the immediate identity of the false target. I recommend the active investigation on the matter to determine whether it's a software; hardware; or any other type of issue that is causing these false targets. Maybe it's a city ops squitter issue and not an ASDEX issue.'

summary: An air traffic controller suddenly lost all radio communication with a departing aircraft because both the main and standby frequencies went down without warning or notification. The controller had to scramble to find an alternate frequency to contact the aircraft, which had descended close to the Minimum Vectoring Altitude (MVA).
rationale: This report falls under ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because the inciting event was a total failure of the facility's primary and standby radio transmitters. This aligns perfectly with the "Frequency / radio outages" and "transmitter/receiver site failures" criteria. It is not classified as Procedure, despite the lack of notification about the outage, because the immediate safety hazard was the sudden physical loss of the communication equipment. It is not Airspace Structure because the MVA violation was a secondary result of the loss of radio contact rather than a built-in structural flaw.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I departed Aircraft X off of ZZZ with ZZZZZ as the next fix. I radar identified them and turned them to ZZZZZ and told them that I would climb as they made the turn due to the ZZZ1 arrivals. We were in radio communication at this point; and she took the turn. I went to climb her to 10000 ft. and she didn't respond. They were getting close to the 2500 ft. MVA so I did the low altitude alert and continued trying to get a hold of them. The Flight Data Controller came over to help as I continued to issue the low altitude alert and tried Guard to get a hold of the aircraft. I then plugged into the Orange jack after I heard the aircraft try to get a hold of me and I could not speak back to them. My handoff turned on the e frequencies and I unplugged from the orange jack and tried the e freq which is when I got them. The aircraft was at 2000 ft. in a 3800 ft. MVA. I immediately issued the low altitude alert for approximately the 4th time and climbed them. They advised me they had terrain in sight and I told them to maintain their own terrain and obstruction avoidance and continue the climb. We had a conversation about what happened and the Pilot and Co Pilot told me they tried to get a hold of me twice; which I heard both times; but they could not hear me at all. They added that if they had not gotten a hold of me when they did they would have initiated a climb on their own and had run this exact scenario in a SIM lab recently. We were told after the whole thing happened that my main and standby frequencies were posted out at that time and no one told us and it was not posted on our [form]. XXX.XX is a major frequency at Tracon and the most important in our area. The fact that we were never told it was going out and it wasn't posted is absolutely dangerous to anyone flying in the ZZZ2 area. This was an avoidable accident and I hope the reason the outage was never relayed to us is uncovered and new procedures are put in place because it can never happen again'

summary: An aircraft descending via an arrival procedure experienced traffic conflicts with unknown VFR aircraft entering the ILS approach path, leading to TCAS alerts. The pilot recommended expanding the local Class C airspace to protect final approach courses from non-participating VFR traffic.
rationale: This is classified as Airspace Structure because the primary issue is the structural design of the airspace, which places active IFR approach paths in close proximity to uncontrolled VFR traffic areas. It fits the "VFR/IFR conflicts from airspace design" repeating issue. It is not classified as ATC Equipment because no radar or facility failed, and it is not Procedure because the VFR aircraft was acting legally within the existing airspace rules.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Aircraft X descending via BRACKEN Arrival into RND. Two airports are just outside of SAT Class C airspace and are on the final approach to RND. This is an area of constant traffic alerts and TCAS events due to non participating aircraft climbing into ILS traffic landing RND. This unknown Aircraft Y was a primary only on RADAR; on the localizer path to Runway 15L at approximately 3;000 feet which is the same altitude for the ILS approach in that area. Traffic was issued 2 times approximately 7 miles apart and then again about 3 miles apart. Aircraft X reported the aircraft in sight and advised it appeared to be Aircraft Y approximately 200 feet below him. At this time Aircraft X was at 3100 ft. or 3200 ft. but should have been at 3000 ft. but due to traffic was not at assigned altitude. Suggestion: San Antonio needs a larger airspace to encompass the very closely spaced and congested airspace around San Antonio and RND which by flight operations is the busiest air force base in the US. Dodging unknown VFR traffic while trying to run simultaneous ILS approaches is not safe; especially in the case where they don't even have an operating transponder and are operating on the localizer at the altitude for the approach. Final approach courses should be protected from non participating aircraft.'

summary: While flying an RNAV approach, a flight crew received a TCAS RA commanding a climb to avoid maneuvering VFR flight training traffic near a VOR. The crew safely resolved the conflict and noted that the airport's airspace is becoming increasingly congested, suggesting improved charting or outreach to local flight schools.
rationale: This report is classified as Airspace Structure because the root problem is the airspace layout that structurally places IFR arrival traffic in direct conflict with high-volume VFR flight training areas, leading to a TCAS RA. This aligns with the "VFR/IFR conflicts from airspace design" pattern. It is not classified as Chart Or Publication; although the pilot requested a chart note, the underlying hazard is the physical airspace usage and structural overlap of traffic.
text: Narrative 1 - 'On the RNAV RNP Z RWY 05 Approach in visual conditions; SEA Center advised two targets maneuvering around the DSD VOR. One aircraft was no factor and clear; the other aircraft he said would present an issue. Neither [aircraft] seemed to be talking on the frequency. As we kept descending on the approach; near WONEK which is near the DSD VOR; we got the TCAS RA commanding a climb to avoid traffic off the left; maneuvering. After clear of conflict; we reestablished on the glide path outside the FAF; and landed in visual conditions.RDM is getting more chaotic with the increased flight training and air carrier operations. A note on the airport information chart would be appreciated about flight training. Also; maybe an outreach to the flight school advising them that if RDM is landing 05 and 29; be vigilant and be required to monitor SEA Center (128.15) if maneuvering near the DSD VOR.'

summary: A Temporary Flight Restriction (TFR) boundary overlaps with the only flight-tested IFR departure fix for an airport, forcing pilots to depart VFR and climb to the minimum vectoring altitude before picking up IFR clearances. The reporter notes this airspace configuration essentially denies IFR access in IMC conditions and advocates for flight testing new departure fixes outside the TFR ring.
rationale: This is Airspace Structure because the incident highlights a physical conflict between a TFR boundary and the published instrument departure routes, effectively blocking IFR departures. This aligns with the "TFR structure / special use airspace conflicts" repeating issue. It is not classified as Chart Or Publication because the charts are factually correct; rather, the physical airspace structure itself creates the operational barrier.
text: Narrative 1 - 'The TFR in place at Bedminster; New Jersey places airport 47N within the 30NM ring. This; of course; requires pilots to be on an IFR flight plan or have a VFR flight plan on file; and have a transponder code and communication with ATC. For those of us who operate at this airport; we have been told by ATC that we are unable to depart IFR; unless able to climb in VMC to the Minimum Vectoring Altitude (MVA) of 2000 ft. We have been told that the reason for this is that SBJ VOR is the only IFR departure fix that has been flight tested for this airport and SBJ is within the 10NM 'no fly' ring of the TFR. This restriction is causing undue burden on the pilots wishing or needing to depart in IFR conditions from 47N. This restriction is effectively denying access to the flying public from using their own tax-payer-funded public ATC system. With the vagaries of weather reporting; this restriction is potentially encouraging unsafe practices by encouraging pilots to depart in marginal conditions in order to pick up and continue on their IFR flight plan. Anecdotally; I understand that there are other nearby non-towered airports where this same problem exists. The solution to this is to flight test one or more additional departure fixes that lie outside the 10NM TFR ring.'

summary: The reporter filed an extremely brief narrative stating only that a near mid-air collision occurred.
rationale: This incident is classified as Airspace Structure as it involves a near mid-air collision, which is frequently the result of structural airspace design flaws, VFR/IFR corridor overlaps, or poor traffic pattern design. It is not classified as Human Factors or Procedure because the extremely brief narrative provides no evidence of pilot error, SOP non-compliance, or specific handling mistakes.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Near mid air collision.'

summary: After departing an airport under IFR, a flight crew experienced two consecutive TCAS RAs from VFR traffic transitioning through a nearby Special Flight Rules Area. The pilot attributed the conflicts to ATC releasing IFR departures directly into a busy VFR corridor where structural airspace overlapping creates chronic hazards.
rationale: This is classified as Airspace Structure because the primary hazard is the geographic design that routes IFR departures directly adjacent to or through a busy VFR Special Flight Rules Area, triggering the RAs. This fits the "Airspace design issues at boundaries" theme. While ATC vectoring played a role, it is not primarily Staffing or Procedure because the root issue is the inherent structural conflict built into the departure path.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Departing Runway 25 at HHR airport; we received our routine IFR clearance instruction to turn left heading 205 and to climb and maintain 3;000 feet. After taking off Runway 25; we complied with our initial turn & climb; and prior to handoff from HHR TWR to SoCal Departure; TWR adivsed us of traffic at 3;100 feet that was exiting the Los Angeles Bravo Special Flight Rules. I; the PIC; checked in with SoCal Departure and advised them that the aforementioned traffic was in-sight as the TCAS RA activated. Shortly after checking-in; and before SoCal TRACON (SCT) had a chance to give us a new vector; we elected to turn left; to avoid the traffic; on a southerly heading as we were traveling much faster than the other aircraft. With the traffic now behind us; SCT vectored us to a 090 heading and to 'climb and maintain 6;000 - reference that traffic.' As we roll out on a 090 heading and initiate the climb to 6;000 feet; the aircraft TCAS activated a second RA.  Confused because in our minds the traffic was now well behind us; we complied with the RA. Only then we realized that the second RA had been activated due to a completely different traffic (coming from the west); that was coming at a nearly head-on path towards us.  Shortly thereafter; SCT gave us a turn to the NW; and 'direct LAX VOR.'  At this point; we were clear of all traffic.  We experienced 2 RAs; from two different aircraft within a minute or so shortly after being IFR released and during initial climb out of HHR.It appears to me that SCT should not have released us out of HHR at that time. With traffic exiting the Los Angeles Special Flight Rules south of the LAX complex; our initial climb and heading put us very near that busy VFR corridor/route.  Also; it seems to me that some momentary confusion; loss of situational awareness or maybe task saturation caused ATC to give us a 090 heading that was in apparent direct conflict with a second VFR traffic coming from the West. SoCal is a busy airspace and looking outside the window is the only sure way to avoid traffic during VMC days; even when on an IFR flight plan. However; IFR departure releases should never be rushed to the point where loss of separation could become an issue.'

summary: During an RNAV GPS approach, the aircraft's instruments displayed an erroneous vertical path indication showing the aircraft was significantly below the proper glide path. The crew acquired the airport visually and manually flew below the indicated VNAV path to safely land, noting that remaining on autopilot would have resulted in a missed approach.
rationale: This report is classified as Airspace Structure because the erroneous vertical path indication stems from a poorly designed instrument approach procedure or an issue with the structural design of the RNAV glidepath itself. This aligns with the category's inclusion of "poorly designed procedures." It is distinct from Software and Automation as the event is logged as an approach design flaw rather than an FMS software crash or database omission.
text: Narrative 1 - 'During RNAV GPS approach Runway 27R to Chicago; aircraft had erroneous path indication showing almost full scale below. Acquired airport visually and flew below VNAV path to require proper glide path.  And we flown aircraft using auto pilot in the VNAV; I missed approach would've been required.'

summary: A VFR pilot operating below Class B airspace observed an inbound airliner at the same altitude, prompting a route deviation to avoid conflict. The reporter highlights a structural flaw in the airspace design that allows uncontrolled VFR aircraft to share altitude with heavy IFR traffic established on the ILS approach, advocating for airspace boundary modifications.
rationale: The classification of Airspace Structure applies because the incident emphasizes a geometric vulnerability where Class B and C boundaries do not adequately protect IFR final approach courses from VFR traffic. This directly matches the "Airspace design issues at boundaries" repeating issue. It is not classified as Procedure because both aircraft were operating legally according to current airspace rules, confirming the hazard is built into the airspace itself.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I departed so as to remain clear of FLL Class C airspace and below floor of MIA Class B airspace. An airliner was observed approximately 10 miles west of FLL inbound to FLL below the normally observed altitude of airliners inbound to FLL. The recent addition of ADS-B allowed timely visual observation of the traffic and we altered our route in a normal fashion without incident. There were several VFR aircraft in the area. I do not believe any FAA regulation were violated. This report is being filed to point out an area of potential hazard that could be addressed by modifications to the MIA 'B' &/or FLL 'C' airspace(s).I believe aircraft departing/arriving the area to or from the northwest should not be allowed to become possible conflicts with ILS Inbounds TO FLL. There exists an area 10 to 15 miles west of FLL where VFR aircraft can operate at the same altitude as aircraft established on the ILS approaches and no ATC clearance is necessary. Another area of concern is the portion of MIA Class B airspace 15 miles west of FLL where FLL ILS arrivals can be at 2500 feet and other inbounds are descending below the 4000 foot floor of the MIA Class B airspace and also into the previously mentioned Class E airspace when only see and be seen VFR flight rules are providing separation of aircraft established on published final approaches that are heavily used. I would like to see modifications as needed to provide a sterile protected environment to FLL instrument inbounds. Generally the inbound aircraft over 20 miles are of sufficient height to lessen the conflicts but when establish on the final I believe extra protection is warranted.'

summary: An aircraft on final approach received a TCAS RA and was forced to go around due to unknown VFR traffic transitioning through the Class Delta airspace in the opposite direction. The controller noted that the VFR aircraft was legally allowed to converge with the final approach course without altitude restrictions, concluding that a Class C airspace upgrade is needed.
rationale: This incident is classified as Airspace Structure because the root problem is the insufficient protection provided by the Class Delta airspace boundaries, which structurally allow VFR aircraft to conflict with IFR arrivals. This perfectly aligns with the recommendation for "Class B/C upgrade to resolve chronic conflict" detailed in the reference guide. It is not classified as Procedure because the VFR transition was legally permitted under the current structure.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Aircraft X was on final approach landing LGB. Unknown VFR traffic was transitioning through LGB Class Delta climbed to 1500 feet opposite direction just north of the final approach course. I issued a traffic alert to Aircraft X who promptly reported the traffic in sight. A mile later; they reported that they were responding to a TCAS/RA climbed to 3000 feet and were sent around. The VFR target later went through SNA airspace and I had them issue the brasher. The LGB Tower Controller was talking to the unknown VFR target but let them fly opposite direction in close proximity to the final approach course. They called traffic but let the targets converge and terminated communication with the VFR aircraft instead of giving them a frequency or ensuring that he would not be a factor. LGB needs a Class C Airspace or at least better VFR departure procedures.'

summary: While flying an RNAV approach and maintaining visual contact with reported traffic, a flight crew experienced a TCAS RA as the other aircraft encroached on their flight path. The crew disconnected the autopilot, executed a manual climb, and then completed a visual approach after determining the traffic was no longer a threat.
rationale: This is classified as Airspace Structure because the event centers on a TCAS Resolution Advisory (RA) triggered by conflicting traffic operating in structurally tight or converging airspace during an approach. It corresponds directly to the "TCAS / Resolution Advisory (RA) events" sub-category. It is not classified as Human Factors because the crew maintained visual contact and responded appropriately to the RA without committing a handling error.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Cleared for the RARYO at or above 3;000 feet with straight in RNAV 34L approach. Traffic reported by ATC and we reported traffic in sight to ATC. After sequencing RARYO in descent traffic in-sight was near our track and ATC advised and asked if we would like to continue. With traffic in sight and not in conflict; visually continued straight in to Runway 34L. Traffic encroached our path and initiated an RA. Autopilot was disconnected and flew manually to climb 200 to 300 feet; then followed by a level off. Report of RA was made to ATC. ATC asked if we were able to continue with the Visual Approach. Decision was made to continue with the Visual Approach with traffic in sight and being non-issue with associated traffic. Tower cleared us to land and landed 34L. Selection of TA in the MCDU versus TA/RA while on final approach with traffic in sight.'

summary: An approach controller managing combined sectors had to vector an arriving IFR aircraft off the localizer to avoid a non-communicating VFR aircraft tracking outbound near the final approach fix. The controller expressed deep concern that the airspace design allows VFR aircraft to transit a narrow, busy corridor without ATC communication, creating chronic and severe safety hazards.
rationale: This report falls under Airspace Structure because the primary safety issue is a structurally flawed VFR corridor that allows non-communicating traffic to legally mix with IFR arrivals near the final approach fix. This fits the "VFR/IFR conflicts from airspace design" pattern perfectly. While the controller was working combined sectors, it is not labeled Staffing because the explicit hazard identified is the physical airspace design and the lack of a mandatory communication boundary.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I was working arrival sectors combined. It was a busy and complex session with numerous BFI arrivals; lots of VFR targets; practice approaches in and through my airspace. Aircraft X checked in and was descended and positioned on the downwind for the ILS into BFI. The whole session I had been observing VFR aircraft in the vicinity of the BFI approach course. As such; I was proactively scanning to avoid potential conflicts; as such had already occurred about an hour before. As I turned Aircraft X to the base leg; I saw a VFR target (Aircraft Y) just northwest of the FAF tracking the localizer outbound and issued the traffic to Aircraft X. Aircraft X was looking; but very clear about not having the traffic in sight. I elected to vector Aircraft X across the localizer until the VFR was no factor prior to issuing the approach clearance for the ILS. Aircraft X landed without incident.Closest proximity between Aircraft X and the VFR was 1.59 miles laterally and 500 feet vertically. My decision to take Aircraft X across their approach course was mainly based on the fact that VFR targets positioned like this do not typically remain 500 feet below the crossing altitude at the FAF. As such; the only way I could ensure there was no conflict was to delay the turn to final for Aircraft X. If the VFR were receiving traffic advisories from ATC; we would at least be able to restrict their altitude; or have them inform us of their intention to remain at or below an altitude that does not conflict with our arrivals to BFI.Something needs to change. The VFR aircraft are transiting a very narrow; busy corridor of airspace and are doing so without any communication with ATC. It is simply unsafe. The VFR aircraft in this area at the very least need to be in communication with ATC so that we can assign; as necessary; altitude restrictions ensuring the safety of all the aircraft involved. The solutions are not hard and while they are potentially more restrictive to VFR aircraft the bottom line is that what happens day in and day out in that airspace as it exists and operates now will eventually result in a very bad accident.'

summary: While descending on an arrival, a flight crew encountered severe wake turbulence from a preceding Boeing 767. The encounter caused a 45-degree uncommanded bank and autopilot disconnect before the crew regained control and requested an offset.
rationale: This is classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because the explicit hazard was an encounter with wake turbulence generated by another aircraft. This aligns directly with the "Wake Turbulence" repeating issue within the updated reference guide. It is not classified as Weather because wake vortices are man-made aerodynamic phenomena rather than natural meteorological events. It is not Aircraft because the autopilot disconnect was a designed safety response to the external aerodynamic upset.
text: Narrative 1 - 'While descending on the WESAT2 arrival into FTW; we encountered wake turbulence from a Boeing 767 that was approximately 20 miles in front of us and traveling in the same direction. Our aircraft banked to the left approximately 45 degrees and our autopilot disconnected. At this point I took over the controls and righted the aircraft while still fighting more of the turbulence. After a few seconds we were offered an offset from Fort Worth Center of which we accepted and made a right turn to put us more upwind. Soon after we were cleared direct to COWTN near FTW and we exited the event.'

summary: A TRACON controller working a complex sequence of aircraft was severely distracted by a belligerent and verbally abusive relieving controller. The constant insults and hostile workplace environment caused the controller to miss critical read-backs and mismanage speed assignments, resulting in a near loss of separation.
rationale: Based on the updated reference guide logic, this fits Environment - Non Weather Related because the controller's ambient workplace environment became a chaotic, unmanageable hazard due to a hostile coworker. This maps directly to the newly added "Workplace / Control Room Distractions" criteria. It is not classified as Human Factors because the controller's errors were the direct, immediate result of severe external verbal abuse rather than an unprompted cognitive lapse. It is not Company Policy because the hazard was a sudden, localized interpersonal attack rather than an overarching management scheduling or procedural directive.
text: Narrative 1 - 'This is a new type of safety issue for me; but I felt compelled to report it to have more eyes review the information. I had Aircraft X; a VFR; call up for flight following from within my area. The pilot had a strong accent; and that along with what I suspect was the quality of his radio made it very hard for me to understand his transmissions. I also had some distraction from within the TRACON itself; which I will describe in a moment. It probably took me over 10 transmissions to get Aircraft X pilot's intentions; and successfully get him processed for flight following. He had also requested an IFR clearance; but after I informed him his requested altitude of 020 was likely to cause issues with MVA's and the subsequent Center sector; he elected to maintain VFR with flight following. There were multiple SIGMETs from all over Texas; and the weather at ZZZ was IFR; information I gave the pilot. I had Aircraft Y being vectored for an RNAV Runway XX Approach at ZZZ; followed by Aircraft Z; on the same approach from the west via the IAF of ZZZZZ. The following Aircraft Z seemed likely to catch Aircraft Y; so I began speed instructions for both aircraft. I was certain that Aircraft X was likely to encounter weather difficulty; possibly becoming an emergency. I instructed him to remain clear of ZZZ Class Delta; but due to TRACON distractions; I missed the read back. A controller plugged in behind me and started observing; in preparation to relieve my position. I told him I had an issue to work out with these two aircraft on final; and preferred him to wait a moment for me to handle it. He did. It took a considerable amount of work for me to end up sequencing the two aircraft; and I was having some issues getting them on the speed restrictions I was intending. I also had coordination to do with ZZZ1 Approach for a point out on Aircraft Y; and subsequently for the following Aircraft Z as well. [Relieving Controller] began to get pushy about doing the relief briefing; so when I thought I had everything reasonably handled I pushed the button and began the briefing. As I was doing the briefing; which was going to take a while considering the information I felt compelled to give him concerning the VFR aircraft in weather difficulty; and the two RNAV aircraft requiring close attention; I realized the speed I had issued appeared to not be enough to maintain required separation later on final. I stopped the briefing; and reissued new speed restrictions to both aircraft. As I was doing this; [the relieving controller] got impatient with the amount of time it was taking to complete the briefing and started becoming borderline belligerent with me because of it. He insulted my controlling; and cursed many times at me. His comments said things like; 'Are we going to get this briefing done; I've been here for six or seven [Expletive] minutes'; 'You only have four [Expletive] aircraft'; and 'Don't touch my [Expletive] headset'. He made other comments as well; some of which I didn't catch because I was still attempting to listen to; work; and adjust my seemingly imminent loss of separation on final. During this argument with him; I unplugged him; and asked him to leave and find the supervisor. I also missed a key read back and made a mistake; I found later when listening to the tapes. I missed the read back of Aircraft X when I instructed him to remain outside of ZZZ Delta; and I forgot to reissue a speed restriction to Aircraft Y when clearing him for the approach. That speed restriction was something I needed with the following Aircraft Z still faster; and Aircraft Y subsequently turned onto final and slowed down considerably. I am confident that had I not been constantly insulted and distracted in the TRACON; I would have not made this mistake. I saw the separation would not be maintained on final; and cancelled the approach clearance of the Aircraft Z; vectored him across final; and then gave him a hard turn back to get him on the approach. He was in IMC conditions. At no time did I lose separation. I'm not really sure what to expect of this report; and I have informed my ATM of everything that happened. I am certain the issue will be addressed. What does concern me; however; is that management seemed to be more concerned with the verbal abuse of me; and less concerned with the safety situation it caused for me while trying to run my sector. I am not worried about the personal confrontation so much; as the fact that it was causing an unsafe situation on my sector. I really do not have any idea what to suggest for this one. I honestly feel this is well above my position pay grade.'

summary: A Cessna 182 pilot flying near mountainous terrain encountered wake turbulence from a passing Boeing 737 that had been lifted by mountain wave activity. The aircraft violently rolled 120 degrees before the pilot successfully applied full opposite controls to recover level flight.
rationale: This incident is classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because the primary hazard was a severe wake turbulence encounter exacerbated by ambient terrain effects. This perfectly matches the "Wake Turbulence" and external aerodynamic hazard repeating issues. It is not classified as Weather because, although wind played a role, the violent upset was directly attributed to the man-made wake vortices of the 737. It is not Human Factors because the pilot executed a flawless recovery from an uncontrollable external aerodynamic disturbance.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I was flying to Denver with one passenger aboard; a personal flight. There was a strong wind from the northwest; and we were experiencing light to moderate turbulence. We were slowly descending to land at Denver. At approximately 7500; ATC advised that a 737 would be passing on the right; approximately a mile away; descending and at a slightly lower altitude than our altitude; on a visual to the north/south runway. I explained that to my passenger. The 737 soon appeared to our right and below us; a few hundred feet below our altitude (I'm guessing about 800 ft lower); and I pointed it out to my passenger. A moment later; our 182 violently rolled to the right; not quite fully inverted; but approximately 120 degrees or so. I applied full left aileron and left rudder and pushed on the yoke; and the airplane slowly rolled to the left to level flight again. I don't now recall whether I said anything to ATC about the event. Over the years; I've tried to analyze the event; and I believe that the mountain wave effect of the strong northwest winds blowing over the mountains lifted the wake turbulence of the 737; causing us to encounter it although we were at a higher altitude than the 737. Had I not recently completed the basic aerobatics course; I don't think I would have responded as well. The primary thing I did that was important was to push on the yoke rather than pull on it; in addition to applying left aileron and rudder.'

summary: After flying at 10,000 feet on a hot day for several hours, a pilot experienced symptoms of hypoxia and was unable to correctly read back a simple ATC route change. The pilot eventually recognized the physiological impairment, successfully programmed the autopilot, and requested a descent to a lower altitude to recover.
rationale: Based on the updated reference guide logic, this is classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because the pilot was incapacitated by a sudden physiological and medical event. This maps directly to the "Physiological & Medical Events" criteria where the human body's internal environment becomes a hazard. It is not classified as Human Factors because the pilot's inability to communicate was driven by a physiological oxygen deficiency rather than a simple cognitive misunderstanding or lack of proficiency. It is not Aircraft because the unpressurized aircraft functioned normally for the altitude flown.
text: Narrative 1 - 'A brush with hypoxia? A [senior] healthy pilot; on a flight from ZZZ to ZZZ1 at 10;000; just above the cloud tops on a very warm day; was contacted by Air Traffic for a simple route change during the last 45 minutes of the flight. After three tries by Air Traffic I was unable to repeat back change but was somehow able to program the change into the autopilot. I then connected the dots and requested lower to 6000. I was clearly having symptoms of hypoxia. A look back at the outside temperature during that flight was 15°C at 10;000 for a density altitude of 12;000 plus and I had been there almost 5 hours. I do have some additional thoughts... First; after three tries to copy the route change Air Traffic basically gave up trying to get me to read back correctly the routing change. After several minutes... I did get the two waypoints route change in the autopilot; but was unable to give Air Traffic a successful read back. Second; when I requested lower because I had connected the dots with possible hypoxia; Air Traffic required me to request lower three times. Each time saying; 'Please repeat your request Aircraft X; please repeat request;' which I understood to be a little hazing for my earlier inability to copy the routing change... Or maybe I was actually mumbling the request? And thought I was speaking clearly?Third. Maybe the Controller; hearing my inability to read back correctly; maybe because of unclear speech on my part… should have connected the dots? That I; the Pilot; may have symptoms of hypoxia; or other medical issues; and addressed that with me? Lastly. After descending to 6;000; I made a mistake by not informing the Controller that I may have had a case of hypoxia or another medical problem. It would have been a learning experience for both of us. I'm correcting that now.Additionally; prior to this at approximately 4 hours into the flight; I had an overwhelming desire to take a nap. I knew I was well rested; fed and hydrated before the flight. Aircraft X is equipped with CO2 detectors. I knew it was probably not that. I opened the vents which appeared to help. The aircraft was on autopilot for the entire trip; so Traffic Control only saw a nice flight on their scopes; and I was only tasked with monitoring engine temperatures. I own both flight oxygen and an oximeter. But both were on loan to a relative who was having heart issues and were not in the aircraft at the time.'

summary: Shortly after leveling off on an arrival, a flight crew experienced multiple wake turbulence events from a preceding Boeing 737 due to a tailwind delaying vortex dissipation. The turbulence caused uncommanded rolls of 15-20 degrees, prompting the pilot to disconnect the autopilot and manually maintain the course.
rationale: This is assigned the Environment - Non Weather Related label because the primary safety event was an encounter with wake vortices from a preceding aircraft. This aligns explicitly with the "Wake Turbulence" repeating issue defined in the category. It is not Weather because the tailwinds simply prolonged the lifespan of the man-made aerodynamic hazard rather than acting as the primary destructive force. It is not Procedure because the crew maintained proper spacing and followed ATC instructions, falling victim to invisible external forces.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Crew was nearing the bottom of the Hawks 7 Arrival in to Seattle and had descended to 5;000 ft. per ATC instruction. Shortly after leveling at 5;000 ft.; crew experienced multiple wake turbulence events from a preceding B737. The pilot turned off the autopilot. Roll rates were roughly 15-20 degrees with about 5-10 degrees of bank. We did not deviate from course or have any exceedance. At the time of the event; the pilot flying (Captain) had auto-throttles off for practice.On the STAR; we were made to slow down early due to the close proximity of the airplane ahead of us. And for metering. We also had tailwinds and were only about 5 miles behind the airplane in front of us. We suspect the tailwinds made it take longer for the wake vortices to dissipate leading to our situation. In the future; I will be more vigilant when encountering this type of threat and talk about it with the Captain. If needed; we can ask for a delayed descent or vectors to mitigate this.'

summary: An aircraft experienced continuous GPS jamming while transitioning through multiple international flight information regions. The jamming persisted even after the aircraft landed and was operating on the ground.
rationale: This incident is classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because external military or malicious GPS jamming saturated the ambient operational environment. This exactly matches the "Ambient Airspace Saturation" criteria where external interference disrupts normal operations. It is not classified as ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because the jamming saturated the airspace generally across multiple international FIRs and the ground, rather than being a specific failure of one local FAA facility. It is not Aircraft because the aircraft's internal GPS receivers were functioning properly but were starved of valid external signals.
text: Narrative 1 - 'This report is submitted in accordance with Pilot Bulletin. We experienced GPS jamming; no spoofing; in the HECC FIR; Cairo; briefly in the vicinity of DATOK; then continuously from TAKSU through the OJAC FIR boundary. After landing in OJAI; we were jammed on the ground.All pilot bulletin and operations alert guidance followed.'

summary: While descending through FL370, an aircraft encountered wake turbulence from an Airbus A320 descending eight miles ahead. The turbulence caused the autopilot to disconnect, threw loose cabin items around, and forced the aircraft into a 45-degree bank with a 3,500-fpm descent rate before the crew recovered.
rationale: The Environment - Non Weather Related label is applied because the core hazard was a violent upset caused by wake turbulence from a preceding aircraft. This perfectly aligns with the "Wake Turbulence" classification criteria for external aerodynamic disturbances. It is not classified as Weather because the turbulence was entirely man-made by the preceding Airbus rather than a natural clear-air turbulence event. It is not Aircraft because the autopilot disconnect and subsequent altitude loss were the result of the external physical upset overriding the aircraft's control surfaces.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Descending out of FL400 with a speed restriction of Mach .76; were directed to cross PURSE at FL340. We started to descend at 1;500 fpm. Around FL370; the aircraft suddenly banked to the left about 45 degrees and we experienced some negative G's. The autopilot switched off and we put power to idle and opposite control inputs. The aircraft then banked up to 45 degrees to the right. We leveled the wings; and noted an additional 3;500 fpm descent rate while passing through FL350. We gradually increased pitch angle to get back to a 1;500 fpm descent and leveled off at FL340. All three crew members were seated with seat belts fastened. No injuries reported. However; the interior was a mess from all the food; water; glasses; magazines; that got thrown around. Immediately after the event; we reported it to ATC and queried about preceding traffic. We were told that there was an Airbus A320 eight miles ahead that was in a descent. In order to avoid any further incidents; we requested to veer off the JANNY4 arrival; and paralleled the course.'

summary: A helicopter pilot conducting a departure from a confined church parking lot struck an unseen wire just after initiating a normal takeoff. The pilot immediately executed an emergency landing on the adjacent clear highway, safely securing the aircraft with no injuries to the passengers.
rationale: This report is classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because the aircraft struck a transient external obstacle (a wire) present in the ambient operating area. This maps directly to the "External Obstacles & Terrain" repeating issue. It is not classified as Airport because the landing zone was a makeshift, off-airport confined area rather than a published FAA-regulated airport environment. It is not Human Factors because the pilot had conducted thorough visual reconnaissance passes, but the invisible nature of the wire acted as an unavoidable external hazard.
text: Narrative 1 - 'This submission is not to report and accident; rather a serious incident under FAA and NTSB definitions. The day before the flight; my wife and I planned to attend a meeting in ZZZ1. I moved the helicopter out of the hangar and had it fueled to the 1/2 full point. We contacted the church where we were going and obtained permission and authorization to land on the property. Early the morning of the flight; I opened ForeFlight and briefed weather; winds; and the anticipated location; and sent a screenshot of the map and location to my rotorcraft instructor. He and I then had a conversation about the steps and procedures to assess the location once I arrived; and we discussed how to set up for the landing. At XA:00 my wife and I met our friends at the ZZZ airport; performed the preflight; calculated a weight and balance; loaded up in the helicopter and headed for ZZZ1. The weather was excellent with good visibility and cool temperatures in the 50's to 60's. After the 20 min flight; upon arriving at our landing zone; I circled twice as a part of our reconnaissance and did our steps of the 600 ft. recon: size; surrounding; surface; slope; safety and then the 300 ft. recon: wind; wires; way in; way out; where to auto. The landing was normal and I secured the helicopter and we attended the meeting and luncheon. After the luncheon; I went out to the helicopter and started my preflight. During my walk around; I walked out to the road next to the church and looked in all directions. I observed wires to the north across the highway; to the east I observed wires; the church building was to our south; and I cleared the area to the west visually; and saw it was open and unobstructed; the wind was from the west. Once the preflight was complete; I recalculated the weight and balance; all was good; we were more than 200 lbs. under the gross weight limit; and with the temperature in the mid 60s; performance showed to be real good; so we loaded back in the helicopter. After going over the passenger briefing and seeing that all passengers were buckled up and ready I started the helicopter. After all checklist items were completed; and the warmup was accomplished; I lifted into a stable hover; maneuvered up and out toward the highway. Once again I looked for any traffic; and noted that the intended flight path was into the wind; and to the west; was clear and unobstructed. As we were in the hover; I pushed forward cyclic and just as we started a normal takeoff; my front seat passenger said 'WIRE' and I suddenly saw a wire just ahead of us! I immediately applied aft cyclic and pulled up collective. I felt the impact with the wire; and I identified that the center of the highway was clear with zero traffic. I initiated an immediate landing. Approximately 2-3 seconds after not clearing the wire; we were on the ground; and I recall the landing as straight and level; firm and smooth. I shutdown the helicopter; made sure all passengers were out safely and well clear of the helicopter and off the highway. I then secured the helicopter. Next I installed the ground handling wheels on the helicopter and with the help of my passenger; we pushed the helicopter off the highway to the north; then 200 ft. up a side road and into my friend's side yard. At that point I was able to take a moment; thankful for all the help and assistance received; and beyond thankful for no injuries. To prevent a recurrence and to avoid this situation in the future: I contacted my flight instructor and walked through the incident scenario; and I have discussed how to avoid this situation in the future. This incident could have been avoided or prevented by driving by vehicle instead of flying. Also; this incident could have been prevented by performing and executing a confined area takeoff; thus avoiding areas that might have lines and wires. I did walk the area to be used for my takeoff path during my preflight; and I had already performed my 600 ft. and 300 ft. recon prior to landing; so by asking a larger area; and looking closer and further down the takeoff are; might have been of value.'

summary: While cruising at FL180 in clear weather, an unknown foreign object, suspected by the pilot to be space debris, impacted and fractured the outer glass layer of the aircraft's windshield. The pilot declared an emergency, received vectors, and safely executed an instrument approach to the nearest suitable airport.
rationale: This incident fits Environment - Non Weather Related because the emergency was triggered by an external, transient physical object impacting the aircraft in flight. This aligns perfectly with the "Bird/Wildlife Strikes & FOD" classification criteria for external physical hazards. It is not Weather because the pilot explicitly noted there was no convective activity or meteorological phenomena (like hail) present at the time. It is not Aircraft because the windshield fracture was the direct result of external physical trauma rather than internal material fatigue.
text: Narrative 1 - 'While cruising at FL180 enroute from ZZZ to ZZZ1 under control of ATC an unknown foreign object impact the pilot windshield fracturing the out glass layer. The windshield remained intact. I [advised] to ATC and requested vectors to the nearest suitable airport ZZZ2. An instrument approach was required with an uneventful landing. The incident resulted to no injuries to the pilot or passenger onboard. The unknown foreign object appears to possibly been some form of space debris for at the time of the incident the aircraft was not near any convective activity nor was the it experiencing any other weather phenomenon.'

summary: During a takeoff roll, a pilot observed several cranes land on the runway ahead, take flight, and then land back in the aircraft's path. The pilot executed a rejected takeoff but struck the birds, which caused fan blade damage to the right engine and scratched the windshield.
rationale: This is classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because the primary hazard was a sudden physical collision with wildlife in the operating environment. This matches the "Bird/Wildlife Strikes & FOD" repeating issue exactly. It is not classified as Airport because the hazard was moving, transient wildlife rather than a permanent defect in the runway pavement or lighting. It is not Procedure because the pilot successfully executed a rejected takeoff according to emergency protocols to mitigate the external threat.
text: Narrative 1 - 'During the takeoff roll while passing 60 kts I observed approximately 4 Cranes land about 1000 ft in front of me. They took flight and moved out of the way so I elected to continue the takeoff. Then at around 85 kts they landed back in the same spot. I executed a rejected takeoff and struck one; maybe two of them. The bird bounced off of the right-hand windscreen as we were stopping. I heard several other impact sounds as we plowed through the grouping. After we taxied back to parking and shut down and subsequent walk-around it was apparent that right engine had ingested the Crane. Fan blades were bent and there was blood and feathers all over the engine and adjacent hull. The windshield appeared to be scratched up from the impact glancing blow. We contacted company and our director of maintenance and filled out the wildlife hazard report with the airport manager.'

summary: An aircraft missed an amended taxi instruction because the crew never heard the transmission. The pilot noted that ATC communications are chronically sporadic or completely unheard in the vicinity of a specific concourse, suggesting significant radio interference or a communication dead zone.
rationale: This is classified as ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because the root cause of the missed clearance is suspected to be a physical dead zone, radio interference, or degrading communication infrastructure on the airport surface. This aligns with the "Frequency / radio outages" repeating issue for this category. It is not classified as Human Factors because the crew physically could not hear the transmission due to facility interference, rather than ignoring or forgetting it. It is not Airport because the hazard is related to electromagnetic radio waves and comms infrastructure, not a physical pavement or signage deficiency.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Aircraft X received taxi instructions from gate 'taxi via TANGO 4; BRAVO.' The clearance was read back correctly.  While taxiing on TANGO; passing T2 intersection; FLL Ground said 'looks like you missed T2...taxi Tango 4 Bravo' (which was our original clearance.) An amended taxi clearance was apparently issued by ATC; but not heard or responded to by the crew of Aircraft X.  Since there is no longer a Ramp Controller working traffic; I have noticed that ATC communications are sporadic or are not being heard at all in the vicinity of the A/B Concourse; especially during pushback calls. [I] suggest ATC explore possible radio interference near the A/B Concourse.'

summary: While distracted by a moose near the runway, a local controller cleared an aircraft for takeoff on a water lane without verifying it was clear. The controller noted that their view of the water lane was partially obstructed by a Tower Display Workstation (TDW) pole, leading to a loss of situational awareness and a near conflict with a crossing aircraft.
rationale: Based on the updated reference guide logic, this is classified as ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because the physical placement of the Tower Display Workstation (TDW) created a dangerous blind spot that obstructed the controller's view of the active canal. The physical ergonomics and layout of the ATC equipment directly contributed to the loss of visual separation. While the moose was a distraction, it is not classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because the structural inability to see the crossing aircraft due to the tower equipment placement was the critical point of failure. It is not Staffing because the issue was hardware layout, not personnel shortages.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I was working the Lake Hood position and observed 4 moose near the airstrip. I was busy looking at the strip to determine if a moose was inside or outside the fence near the strip. Aircraft X called ready to taxi. I did not hear Aircraft X say they were at fish and wildlife. (This required the aircraft to cross the active water lane). I cleared Aircraft X to taxi for a west via the slow canal.Aircraft Y called ready for departure on the west water lane. I cleared Aircraft Y for takeoff west. I briefly glanced at the water lane and did not observe any aircraft in the canal; my view was partially obstructed by the pole and the TDW (tower display workstation). I went back looking at the moose near the strip to ensure that he did not go on the strip. Aircraft Y departed and Aircraft X asked to verify that he was cleared to cross the water lane. I did not use the words cross the east/west water lane taxi via the slow canal as required. I told him to taxi for a west.I immediately notified the Controller In Charge (CIC) what had just transpired. Once Aircraft X was airborne I asked him where he was when Aircraft Y departed. He stated that he was in the middle of the canal. I estimated it to be around 4000 feet down the water lane. I advised the CIC that they should call the supervisor on duty to start a significant Mandatory Occurrence Report.Our tower display workstation is obstructing the east/west water lane. Recommend that it be moved to the west side of the stripboard.'

summary: A flight crew arriving at an airport noted that the ASOS was reporting a quarter-mile visibility and fog, despite the actual weather being clear with greater than 10 miles of visibility. The crew discovered from ground personnel that this ASOS frequently broadcasts wildly inaccurate weather data and is in dire need of repair or relocation.
rationale: The ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings classification applies because the automated weather observing system (ASOS), which is a ground-based navigational/informational facility, was actively broadcasting false data due to a sensor malfunction. This maps to the newly added "Automated Weather System (ASOS/AWOS) Failures" pattern. It is not classified as Weather because the actual meteorological conditions were perfectly clear; the hazard was the broken facility reporting a false weather event. It is not Chart Or Publication because the issue resides in live, dynamic sensor data rather than a static chart error.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Prior to the approach the ASOS was reporting 2 SM and fog. We were 40 miles out from the IAF and could see ZZZ. The visibility looked clear and no fog in the area. Center cleared us the approach 25 mile outs from the IAF and cleared us to change to advisory. I hesitated on calling the visual because of my experience with fog. After determining that there was no fog and it was clear and 10 SM visibility. We tried to contact Center to call for the visual approach; but were unable to reach Center due to a NOTAM that ground comm was out. After we landed my First Officer said that ASOS was reporting a 1/4 mile and fog; which was inaccurate. It was clear and with a visibility greater that 10 SM. I was informed by the ground crew that this has been an ongoing problem with inaccurate weather. ASOS needs to be relocated or repaired.'

summary: During an ILS approach, the aircraft's autopilot captured the glideslope but the signal erroneously indicated the aircraft was below the path before violently jumping to a half-scale high deflection. This false signal commanded an excessive 20-degree nose-up pitch, forcing the engines to spool up from idle to recover, which the pilot suspects was caused by interference from a nearby airport.
rationale: This incident is classified as ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because the primary hazard was a false or highly unstable glideslope signal generated by external facility interference. This exactly matches the "ILS / glideslope anomalies" and unreliable navigation signals listed in the reference guide. It is not classified as Aircraft because the plane's autopilot functioned exactly as designed by following the erroneous external signal it received. It is also not Software and Automation because the root cause was an external radio frequency signal anomaly, not an internal FMS database error.
text: Narrative 1 - 'FO Flying / AP on in Nav mode. Just as we were making our turn to transition from the arrival to the approach. FO selected APP mode on FCU the LOC & GS  captured simultaneously . Initially the GS indicated below us but as soon as the aircraft went to capture mode the GS indication jumped above the 1/2 scale deflection up. The aircraft followed the GS as it moved up to 1/2 scale high until the aircraft pitch was approximately 20 degrees nose up. Thrust had been at idle so the speed vector showed a negative trend while the engines were trying to accelerate.  The power finally caught up and the nose came back down . We were able to continue the approach and land. I talked to the crew from the day before and they had a similar thing happen to them.  There is an airport very close to this intersection. I wonder if there maybe some interference from the [other] airport. If there is some interference we need to add a note to the 10-7 pages to warn the crews this situation could occur.'

summary: While flying a coupled ILS approach, an aircraft began experiencing full-scale deflections to the left and right, forcing the Captain to disconnect the autopilot at 1,400 feet to stabilize the aircraft. The reporter noted a previous occurrence of this exact instability on the same approach, highlighting a systemic issue with the localizer/glideslope signal quality.
rationale: This report falls under ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings due to an unstable localizer/ILS signal emanating from the ground facility, which caused the aircraft's autopilot to bank wildly. This maps directly to the "ILS / glideslope anomalies" and false signal repeating issues. It is not classified as Aircraft because the aircraft's internal flight control computers were merely tracking a bending or degraded external ground facility signal. It is not Procedure because the core issue was the physical instability of the navigation signal rather than a crew intentionally violating charted autopilot minimums.
text: Narrative 1 - 'While flying a coupled ILS Z Runway 8 approach into BUR; the aircraft began a shallow left roll until going full-scale deflection; then began a right roll; quickly going nearly full-scale deflection to the right of course. At this point; the Captain; as the PF; disconnected the autopilot and completed an uneventful landing.During the flight; I mentioned that recently (one month prior) I filed a report after flying this same approach to minimums in IMC. On that date; our coupled ILS could not maintain course past 1200 ft. On that report; I indicated the approach briefing included reference to the approach note #4; stating; 'Autopilot coupled approach not authorized below 1027 ft.' The briefing continued with the stated plan to disconnect the autopilot at 1200 ft. During the actual approach; the aircraft was already rolling right; past half-scale deflection; in IMC. The response I received was confusing: it stated that they discovered that the approach note was a misprint. Apparently the note was intended to read that coupled approaches are not authorized below 1007 ft (the charted DA). While that might indeed be the case; it doesn't address the safety concern that the coupled approach could not remain stable at a higher altitude. In the case of that flight; the coupled approach was unstable at 1200 ft. Now; on this recent flight (in VMC); the aircraft went full-scale deflection; both left and right; starting at 1400 ft. Bottom line: Should the approach note read 'coupled ILS approaches not authorized below 1xxx ft'? This situation could be dangerous with a crew flying in IMC with fixed threats to include a congested area; steeply rising terrain; and a short runway.''

summary: During an RNAV approach, Tower control issued a Low Altitude Alert, prompting the pilot flying to verify that they were perfectly on the VNAV path and above all charted minimums. The pilot noted that the errant warning from the Tower's automated system caused a significant distraction, leading to a late flap configuration and a temporarily unstabilized approach.
rationale: This is classified as ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because the Tower's automated radar alerting system generated a false Minimum Safe Altitude Warning (MSAW), which severely distracted the crew. This aligns exactly with the "Radar and display failures" and phantom/false alert repeating issues for ATC equipment. It is not classified as Human Factors because the primary trigger for the crew's distraction was the facility's faulty automation system rather than an unprompted cognitive error. It is not Airspace Structure because the approach path itself was safe and valid; the ground-based monitoring system simply flagged it incorrectly.
text: Narrative 1 - 'We were cleared for the RNAV RNP Z 26 into ABQ from BRKBD. During the approach; Tower notified us of a 'Low Altitude Alert' and asked if we were still on the approach. I was the Pilot Flying and verified we were on the approach. I verified we were still on the VNAV PATH; I crosschecked our next waypoint in the FMC to verify we were still listed as 'At or Above' the specified altitude as well as the EFB Approach Plate. I scanned our Map Display for Terrain information and also visually acquired it.  Everything looked good as far as altitude and terrain was concerned. I got distracted enough that the Pilot Monitoring started to recommend flaps and gear as I got behind the aircraft and was slow to slow down. It wasn't until reviewing the post flight report did I realize the flaps were set below 1000 feet at approximately 800 feet; which was not a stabilized approach.  My only question is; if I were on the Approach and on the VNAV PATH; why did it trigger a Low Altitude Alert from Tower. The notification caused enough of a distraction for me to lose track of my airspeed and resulted into a potential and unrecognized unstabilized approach.'

summary: While on short final for a visual approach, an aircraft was instructed by Tower to execute a go-around and re-enter the pattern. The controller later explained that a highly sensitive new tracking system used by St. Louis control had errantly flagged the landing, forcing the controller to issue the mandatory go-around command.
rationale: This incident is classified as ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because a newly implemented ATC tracking system generated a false alarm that compelled the controller to issue an unnecessary go-around. This perfectly matches the "ASDE-X / surface automation errors" or faulty ATC alerting systems classification criteria. It is not classified as Procedure because the controller was forced by the system's strict parameters to issue the command, making the facility's hardware/software the root cause rather than a voluntary procedural choice. It is not Human Factors because neither the pilot nor the controller made an operational error.
text: Narrative 1 - 'When cleared for the visual approach for Runway 12L coming in to Saint Louis Lambert airport; I aimed for the numbers starting from the vector I was on. When I got to short final at a slight dog-legged angle I was instructed by Tower to go-around and join a left pattern for Runway 12L. After landing via the left pattern Tower informed me that a new system Saint Louis control was using had flagged my landing and the controller had no choice but to comply and give a go-around instruction. [Caused by] sensitivity of the new system being used by ATC. After the event I was instructed to submit this report in response. The system should be fixed as to not give errant flags. As for what I can do about it; I now recognize that the system is new and is prone to such errors so in the future I will make sure to square off a pseudo base leg in an attempt to avoid being flagged errantly.'

summary: During descent, an aircraft experienced a total loss of both ADS-B receivers and a temporary loss of the Terrain display. The crew queried ATC regarding their transponder status, and the controller confirmed that widespread 5G interference was occurring in the vicinity of the airport.
rationale: This is classified as ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because external electromagnetic interference (5G) temporarily disabled the aircraft's ability to receive external navigation and surveillance data. This maps directly to the "GPS jamming / GNSS outages" and external interference classification logic. It is not Environment - Non Weather Related because the interference specifically targets and degrades navigation facility signals rather than physically interacting with the aircraft's structure (like a bird strike or drone). It is not Aircraft because the onboard systems were functioning correctly but were starved of valid external signals.
text: Narrative 1 - 'During descent the aircraft experienced a loss of both ADS-B receiving 1 & 2 and a temporary Terrain display to standby on our NAV display. Loss was accompanied by a Class 2 message alerts which we began to run. Since the message alert requires determining the state of the transponder; I queried the ATC Controller as to the status of our transponder. He stated 'Everything is fine; 5G interference in the vicinity of DEN was reported'. We completed the approach.'

summary: While in cruise flight, an aircraft lost both GPS 1 and 2 signals for approximately 12 to 15 minutes, with GPS 2 failing to recover for the remainder of the flight. ATC advised the crew that multiple aircraft transitioning through the sector were simultaneously experiencing the same loss of navigation signals.
rationale: The ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings classification is applied because a widespread loss of external GPS signals disabled the primary global navigation facilities for multiple aircraft simultaneously. This aligns directly with the "GPS jamming / GNSS outages" repeating issue. It is not classified as Environment - Non Weather Related because the interference was strictly related to electromagnetic signal loss blocking the navigation infrastructure. It is not Aircraft because the internal GPS receivers were healthy but the external satellite signals were blocked or jammed.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Enroute FL350 near NEERO intersection when we began experiencing loss of both GPS 1 and 2. Advised ATC who replied that many aircraft transitioning the area were experiencing the same issues. Heard several aircraft on frequency report loss of signal as well. Event lasted approximately 12 - 15 minutes; after which GPS 2 was unable to recover its position for the remainder of the flight. Reported event to company Dispatch and made an entry in the maintenance logbook at destination.Cause: No mitigation possible really; just awareness.'

summary: While initiating an arrival procedure into Denver, ATC warned an incoming flight crew of a 5G event in the area. Shortly thereafter, the aircraft experienced an intermittent ADS-B reporting failure and received a GPS Lost message on the FMC, prompting the crew to clear the nuisance messages and continue an uneventful ILS landing.
rationale: This report fits ATC Equipment / Nav Facility / Buildings because external 5G interference caused a failure of the external navigation and surveillance data links (GPS and ADS-B). This fits perfectly within the "GPS jamming / GNSS outages" criteria established for facility and infrastructure interference. It is not Software and Automation because the failure was caused by external signal jamming rather than an internal software logic error or database corruption on the aircraft. It is not Environment - Non Weather Related because it involves interference with invisible navigation infrastructure rather than a physical external hazard.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Upon STAR initiation (Nixxes) into DEN landing north; ATC advised a 5G event at DEN.  At 10;000 feet on Localizer to 35L; ECAM intermittently dinged and displayed ADS-B 1&2 Reporting Failure. Cleared ECAM and emergency cancelled the repetitive nuisance messages. Additionally; received a GPS Lost message on FMC at 8;000 feet.  Continued CAT 1 ILS to landing uneventfully.  Notified maintenance of ADS-B system failure.  Maintenance informed me that that was the third such report of ADS-B failure in the last 15 minutes.'

summary: A TRACON and Center controller experienced confusion regarding the proper handoff altitude for southbound departures. The official Letter of Agreement (LOA) mandated 8,000 feet, but a supervisor found an unofficial laminated sheet indicating the altitude had been changed to 9,000 feet.
rationale: This is classified as Manuals because the core issue is contradictory and poorly documented ATC operational directives, specifically an unofficial laminated sheet conflicting with the official ERIDS and LOA. This maps directly to the "ATC Directives & Letters of Agreement (LOA)" repeating issue for text-based operational manuals. It is not classified as Company Policy because the specific hazard is the conflicting written documentation itself, rather than a broad management mandate. It is not Procedure because the controllers were attempting to follow the rules, but the written rules were inherently contradictory.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I had an aircraft enroute at 9000 feet. I took radar contact on an aircraft departing southbound. The temporary altitude showed the aircraft climbing to 8000 feet. I noticed the aircraft was going through the temporary altitude at 8300 feet. I called TRACON to turn the aircraft 20 degrees right and I turned my aircraft 20 degrees right. I looked up in our Letter of Agreement (LOA) with TRACON and it said departing aircraft going southbound should be climbing to 8000 feet. I asked the controller in TRACON about it and they showed that they can climb their departures to 9000 feet. My supervisor looked it up and found some paperwork; not in the Enroute Information Display System (ERIDS); but just on a laminated sheet that showed some changes. It said they can climb departures to 9000 feet.We have aircraft going that route of flight all the time at 9000 feet. Suggest more training so everyone is familiar with the procedure or change the departures to 8000 feet.'

summary: A Boeing 777 flight crew troubleshooting a fuel transfer issue consulted the Electronic Checklist (ECL) for a Fuel Imbalance. The crew realized that strictly following the ECL's logic tree would have dangerously commanded them to turn off all fuel pumps to an operating engine, contradicting the physical Flight Manual.
rationale: This incident falls under Manuals because the primary hazard was a flawed Electronic Checklist (ECL) and Flight Manual that directed the crew to configure the aircraft without fuel pumps. This directly aligns with the "Flight Operations Manuals (FOM/QRH) Deficiencies" sub-category. It is not classified as Software and Automation because the issue was the text-based logic of the checklist steps, rather than a spontaneous crash of the aircraft's internal flight management computer. It is not Human Factors because the crew correctly analyzed the written checklist to the letter and realized the checklist itself was the hazard.
text: Narrative 1 - 'ECL Non-Normal FUEL IMBALANCE checklist appears to be able to lead a pilot to turn off all main fuel pumps in the low quantity fuel tank with all cross-feed valves closed.We were dispatched and fueled with no center tank fuel and less than full main tanks. About three hours into the flight we got a FUEL IN CENTER advisory EICAS message.We turned on both center tank fuel pumps to the burn the center tank fuel. We noted that the right main tank had approximately 1;600 lbs less fuel than the left main tank. We determined the engines were burning evenly and suspected that fuel had transferred from the right tank to the center tank.We did not have a FUEL IMBALANCE advisory EICAS message but we went to the ECL FUEL IMBALANCE checklist to attempt to balance the fuel. We determined; per the checklist; there was no fuel leak and commenced the checklist.Page 3 of the checklist: there is a box that says'There is no indication of an engine fuel leak and the FUEL IMBALANCE message shows' We checked the NO box as we did not have the FUEL IMBALANCE EICAS message. Upon checking NO; that step blued out showing we completed that step and the very next step; FUEL CROSSFEED switch (either): ON; blued out as well. In other words; upon the FUEL CROSSFEED step bluing out there was no switching to occur per the checklist.Page 4 of the checklist: asks if the Left main tank quantity is low. We checked 'NO' which blued the rest of that page out.Page 5 of the checklist: asks if the Right main tank quantity is low. We checked 'YES'The next step directed and stated 'R fuel PUMPS FWD and AFT switches(both): OFFThis allows fuel from the higher quantity tank to feed both engines. The next and final step of Page 5 is to check when fuel balancing is complete. At no point does the ECL in our configuration direct us to open the crossfeed valves. We did NOT turn off the R main fuel pumps and elected to leave the imbalance as it was as we were not at the fuel imbalance limit. We did contact Maintenance Control and wrote up the fuel transfer issue.Guidance from our training is to follow the ECL without second guessing it. I completely understand the rational as some of the checklists are complicated but in this case had we follow the checklist verbatim we would have had the left tank fuel pumps supplying the left engine and the right engine being fed by no fuel pumps at all. Furthermore; I looked at the 777 Flight Manual Non-Normal FUEL IMBALANCE checklist. It is different from the ECL. Following that checklist would have led to proper fuel balancing.In conclusion: The ECL FUEL IMBALANCE checklist; under certain circumstances; can lead to an engine being fed by no fuel pumps. The 777 Flight Manual illustrates a discrepancy with the ECL. The 777 Flight Manual leads to configuration so the engine will be supplied with fuel pump pressure.'

summary: A mechanic working on a CL650 requested clarification on whether a specific 35% sensor accessed from the tail constituted a Required Inspection Item (RII). The General Maintenance Manual (GMM) only listed "access panels" as RII, creating significant ambiguity regarding the sensor's status.
rationale: This report is classified as Manuals because it highlights ambiguity within the General Maintenance Manual (GMM) regarding what qualifies as a Required Inspection Item (RII).
