The lack of a clear, text-based definition for access panels creates a compliance and safety hazard. It is not Company Policy because the issue resides in the specific wording of the maintenance document rather than a systemic management scheduling failure. It is not Procedure because the mechanic is seeking clarification on an ambiguous text rather than having committed a tactical execution error.
text: Narrative 1 - 'On the CL650 there is a 35% sensor which is accessed from the tail of the aircraft thru the hell hole - no tail tanks are opened and no wet bladder panels are opened or removed.Per our GMM (General Maintenance Manual) the only thing which is RII (Required Inspection Item) under the Fuel Chapter is access panels.  The sensor is not an access panel; 6 bolts and an O ring are only things which are removable.  The MM just calls out for an ops check and a leak check. Would this sensor be consider an access panel? Would this be a RII item?'

summary: During a departure in possible windshear conditions, a flight crew exceeded the maximum tire ground speed. The crew noted that they had strictly followed the recommended takeoff procedures published in their manual, which inadvertently induced the overspeed.
rationale: This fits the Manuals classification because the flight crew exceeded the maximum tire ground speed by explicitly following the published, recommended manual procedure for windshear conditions. This aligns with the newly updated "Performance & Minimums Documentation" criteria where a documented procedure inherently causes an exceedance. It is not Procedure because the pilot successfully executed the published procedure; the published steps themselves were flawed. It is not Weather because, although windshear was a threat, the specific exceedance was induced by the manual's prescribed airspeed guidance.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Exceeded the MAX tire ground speed on takeoff using the recommended procedure for takeoff in possible windshear conditions. After takeoff; we sent a report for the overspeed and discussed ways to mitigate this threat in the future.'

summary: A flight crew reviewing their pre-departure paperwork discovered that the text-based takeoff minimums and departure instructions for Chicago Midway (MDW) were entirely different between their LIDO charts and Jeppesen charts. The LIDO text also completely omitted a critical speed restriction that was present on the Jeppesen version.
rationale: The Manuals label is assigned because the primary hazard involves a contradiction in the text-based takeoff minimums and departure text between LIDO and Jeppesen documentation. This matches the "Performance & Minimums Documentation" repeating issue. It is not classified as Chart Or Publication because the issue lies in the text-based operational minimums and textual departure restrictions rather than a graphical error on a moving map. It is not Procedure because the crew is dealing with contradictory published rules rather than tactically failing to execute a single correct procedure.
text: Narrative 1 - 'It came to our attention while looking at our LIDO charts prior to departure that the takeoff minimums in MDW are different between the Jeppesen and LIDO charts.  The takeoff minimums for 31C in MDW for Jeppesen are TDZ 1000 Rollout 1000. LIDO shows takeoff minimums for 31C as 600R. The AFC chart does not indicate any CL for any runways and the Jeppesen chart indicates CL for runway 4R/22L. Also; the MDWAY1 Departure text on LIDO does not mention the 'maintain 250kts to advised'; it is only on the pictorial of the departure. This is unlike any other departure in LIDO where the text indicates all the restrictions.'

summary: During a runway change on descent, a flight crew noticed that their FMS/CDU approach identifiers did not match their updated EFB Jeppesen charts. The crew realized the aircraft's internal FMC database was out of date, forcing them to request radar vectors to a visual approach.
rationale: This is classified as Manuals because the root cause of the navigational discrepancy was an out-of-date FMC database, pointing to a failure in the documented administrative database update cycle. This aligns with the "Database Update Procedures" addition for administrative manual flaws. It is not Chart Or Publication because the EFB chart was actually correct; the failure was the administrative procedure to update the aircraft's internal FMC database. It is not Human Factors because the crew properly verified the discrepancy and safely mitigated the outdated data via radar vectors.
text: Narrative 1 - 'On descent into ICN; we were changing the FMS/CDU from a previously planned and entered south-runway; STAR; and approach; which was in the FMS database and EFB (Electronic Flight Bag) Jepp; to a different north-runway; STAR; and approach. At that time; we noticed most of the FMS/CDU north runway STAR names and identifiers were different than our EFB Jepp Data. Then; we verified EFB Jepp data dates. They were the new; updated today (expires yesterday). However; we identified the FMC database; that starts yesterday; was not selected.We verified and confirmed remaining FMC route waypoints for accuracy. They were the same as EFB Jepp waypoints. We decided to continue up to the verified route end waypoint; (before STAR begins). CRM alternatives and precautions were evaluated. Weather was CAVOK. Upon contact with ICN-Radar; we advised that our STAR/Approach database was inaccurate and requested radar vectors for an arrival to a visual approach; before STAR entry waypoint. We received radar vectors; prior to the last route end waypoint. We continued with normal radar vectors and descents to a visual approach and landing. [Causal factor was] FMC out of date.'

summary: A flight attendant repeatedly found heavy-duty lavatory seals installed on the Portable Breathing Equipment (PBE), which would require tools to cut off during a fire emergency. The reporter suspects maintenance is utilizing the wrong seals based on flawed maintenance directives to prevent moisture intrusion.
rationale: This falls under Manuals because maintenance personnel are repeatedly installing incorrect, heavy-duty seals on emergency equipment, indicating a flaw in the Illustrated Parts Catalog (IPC) or maintenance documentation. This maps to the "Maintenance & Parts Catalog Errors" repeating issue. It is not classified as Equipment / Tooling because the seals themselves are not defective; they are simply the wrong part being directed for installation by faulty maintenance paperwork. It is not Company Policy because it is a specific maintenance documentation error rather than an overarching management directive.
text: Narrative 1 - 'This aircraft; has a heavy duty seal on the Essex Portable Breathing Equipment (PBE) above the #2 jump seat. The seal is the same as the ones used in the lavatory. This is a safety issue because this type of seal will have to be CUT OFF in order to use the emergency equipment. The time it takes to do that could result in loss of lives in a fire. These seals should be the break-away kind that all flight attendants may access without too much difficulty in an emergency.I have brought this issue to managements' attention in the past. The seals were corrected of the specific aircraft at the time. However; I continue to find these maintenance seals on the Essex PBE's. I am hesitant to call Maintenance and cause delays; although I believe they are warranted. So I've decided to write them up whenever I find them.I believe that maintenance is using these heavy duty seals on the Essex PBE's to prevent moisture from getting to the units. However; they are also preventing US from getting to the units.I recommend that the heavy duty seals be removed from all emergency equipment. They need to be accessible.'

summary: During the drafting of a new Letter of Agreement (LOA) between a TRACON and ARTCC, a critical paragraph granting TRACON control for descents was inadvertently deleted. Controllers operating without knowledge of the omission continued the practice, resulting in dozens of operational deviations.
rationale: This incident is classified as Manuals because a critical paragraph governing descent control was inadvertently deleted during the drafting of a new Letter of Agreement (LOA) between TRACON and ARTCC. This maps exactly to the "ATC Directives & Letters of Agreement (LOA)" repeating issue. It is not Company Policy because the error was a typographical or drafting omission in a specific document rather than a deliberate management decision. It is not Procedure because the controllers were operating under the assumption the old rule still existed due to a lack of proper textual briefing.
text: Narrative 1 - 'New Letter of Agreement (LOA) between TRACON and ARTCC went into effect. LOA briefings were conducted for a simplified LOA that allowed for greater control on arrival and departure aircraft to minimize coordination between TRACON and ARTCC. However; a previous segment of the LOA; one giving TRACON control for descents outside the lateral boundaries of the airspace to 10;000 feet on a STAR was inadvertently dropped from the new LOA. While the intent was not to remove that section; it was. We didn't get briefed on the missing section and have been operating under the previous provision that gave us control for descents to 10;000 feet. This has likely resulted in many dozens of airspace Operational Deviations.We just now are getting pre-position face to face briefings to suspend this practice until a new; amended LOA containing the dropped provision is signed and put into effect.Develop a facility 'area team' to have a team of controllers and supervisors meet; at the same time; to review all LOA/SOP changes to minimize the risk of overlooked items of this nature. I would recommend a Front Line Manager/Controller/Staff person team be implemented and given official time to all meet in committee to review proposed changes.'

summary: A TRACON controller had to intervene and break noise abatement procedures to prevent a loss of separation between a slow-climbing Airbus and a trailing Boeing 737. The controller noted that the facility's Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) legally allows Tower to launch successive departures using visual separation, which is structurally unsafe for that specific Airbus model.
rationale: This fits Manuals because the facility's Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) and LOA text legally allow a practice that inherently creates a loss of separation hazard. The textual directive itself is the root cause of the operational compression. It is not Company Policy because the hazard stems from a specific ATC facility manual's written rule rather than a broad HR or management pressure tactic. It is not Human Factors because the controllers applied the SOP correctly; the SOP was structurally unsafe for that aircraft type.
text: Narrative 1 - 'The SOP allows Tower to launch successive departures utilizing pilot provided visual separation. The ATCT must modify the secondary scratchpad in the STARS data-tag to advise the TRACON that pilot provided visual separation is being used. However; in this case the first aircraft is an [Airbus] and that type of aircraft is notorious for climbing out much slower than most other aircraft. According to the FALCON replay the trailing B738 is 1.55 miles behind the [Airbus] with a 60 knot overtake. I restricted the B738 to 230 knots and requested the [Airbus] increase to 250 knots as soon as possible. Despite those efforts the situation continued to compress. While I was aware pilot provided visual separation was being utilized; in my judgment the situation had become unsafe and I decided to break our noise abatement procedures and initiate an early eastbound turn for the B738. After initiating the turn I made the supervisor aware of the situation. Both aircraft subsequently continued their departures without further incident.This is a common problem that we experience with [this type of Airbus departure]. I do not know why this specific aircraft type climbs out so differently; but it is common knowledge at the TRACON. I would propose that this situation could be corrected by changing the Letter of Agreement to exclude [this Airbus aircraft type departure] from the provision allowing the use of pilot provided visual separation between successive departures. If Tower had utilized the standard 3-5 mile in-trail spacing that would have been required during IMC; this event would have been completely avoided.'

summary: A pilot discovered that the Boeing 737 QRH contained inaccurate performance tables for unreliable airspeed, which had been noted in a company bulletin seven months prior but never physically corrected in the manual. The reporter warned that relying on an easily forgotten bulletin instead of updating the actual QRH text poses a severe risk of losing an airframe.
rationale: This report is classified as Manuals because a known error in the Boeing 737 QRH performance tables was left uncorrected in the physical manual for over six months. The reliance on an easily forgotten bulletin instead of updating the actual QRH text creates a severe hazard. It is not Company Policy because the issue is specifically about the maintenance and updating of a flight deck manual rather than a scheduling or dispatch pressure. It is not Human Factors because a crew would be acting on factually incorrect printed text rather than making an unprompted cognitive mistake.
text: Narrative 1 - 'B737NG QRH Errors. Threat: aircrew will use incorrect QRH procedures resulting in possible loss of airframe. Background: while looking up QRH unreliable airspeed power and pitch settings; I noted the performance table for the 737-700 CFM56 holding flaps up unreliable airspeed (table x.x) was incorrect as the lighter gross weights had higher airspeeds and N1s than the heavier gross weights. I submitted a [report] Flight Ops publication feedback form on it. The response was quick; but noted the correction had been put out via bulletin on (date from seven months ago) that the table was incorrect and it had also noted the speedbrake 'Do Not Arm' procedure was in error. I remembered reading the bulletin; but honestly thought it was letting me know the QRH was going to be updated. It has now been six plus months; and the QRH still contains inaccurate information. I have spoken to a half dozen pilots; including a Chief Pilot and a separate Check Airman; none knew the QRH was still incorrect nor recalled the bulletin. Recommend that if the QRH cannot be updated due to processes; finding a different way to remind aircrew the QRH is in error. A note to review the bulletin (or even the correct table and procedures) could be added to the front page of the weather packets or dispatcher note regarding the bulletin could be added to all releases.'

summary: A pilot was informed by management that improper maintenance had been performed on an aircraft they had recently flown. The pilot noted that the aircraft's maintenance log was perfectly clear and signed off, meaning the physical maintenance was conducted completely without documentation.
rationale: This is classified as Logbook Entry because the primary safety violation was the complete failure to document maintenance actions in the aircraft's official maintenance log. This directly matches the "Undocumented Maintenance" repeating issue. It is not Procedure because the report's focus is on the lack of a historical written record that blinded the pilot to the aircraft's true airworthiness, rather than the physical maintenance execution. It is not Manuals because the failure involved the specific aircraft's maintenance diary rather than a general company reference book.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I received a call from the Regional Aviation Director and the Regional Maintenance Director. They informed me of an incident in which I was involved concerning maintenance that had been performed; without my knowledge; on an aircraft that I had flown and was not properly documented in the maintenance log. [A few days prior] I arrived at our base maintenance hangar; MLE. I performed preflight checks on aircraft to include checking the maintenance log. The maintenance log was properly filled out; to include two write-ups dated [same day]; both were signed off per Company standards. I proceeded to pull the aircraft out of the hangar and returned to our base helipad. I departed at XA:38 and landed at XA:48 without incident. No other flights were conducted during the remainder of my shift; which ended at XJ:00 [the next day]. I understand that the aircraft was taken out of service [the next day]; after information that improper maintenance had been conducted on aircraft. Proper maintenance was then performed and the aircraft was returned to service the same day.'

summary: While in flight, a pilot reviewed the aircraft logbook and noticed a discrepancy in a previous entry regarding a tire replacement. The logbook entry listed the wrong tire number as the one being replaced to correct a worn tire discrepancy.
rationale: This incident falls under Logbook Entry because the core issue is a typographical error within the official written record of maintenance performed. This aligns with the category's focus on incorrect documentation and sign-offs. It is not classified as Equipment / Tooling because the tires themselves were physically fine and safely installed; the only defect was the written record. It is not Procedure because the physical maintenance was executed correctly, leaving only the administrative documentation in error.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Arrived at the aircraft; reviewed the logbook and performed preflight duties routinely. Only thing of note was two new tires on the aircraft. Approximately 30 [minutes] after takeoff I looked at the log book again and noted a discrepancy. The page for our current flight was normal as far as I could tell; to include AWR. The previous page detailed the two tire changes. One entry showed the following discrepancy. Discrepancy: MLG Tire 9; worn beyond limits. Corrective Action: R&R Tire 10. The tire described as worn was not the one documented as replaced. Not sure what I can say; I just missed this error.'

summary: A dispatcher rushing to get a flight crew out before they timed out filled out an MEL log item but forgot to sign the logbook page. Additionally, a flight crew missed a requirement to include specific text with a miscellaneous logbook entry because the Airworthiness Directive compliance instructions were confusing.
rationale: This fits the Logbook Entry classification because the primary hazards were a missing signature on a logbook page and missing required text for an AD compliance entry. These map perfectly to the "Missing Signatures & Forms" and incomplete documentation criteria. It is not classified as MEL, despite an MEL being involved, because the specific failure highlighted by the reporter was the omitted signature on the log page rather than a misinterpretation of the deferral's physical restrictions. It is not Human Factors because, within this taxonomy, specific failures of official aircraft maintenance record-keeping are explicitly routed to this label.
text:
Narrative 1 - 'While dispatching two aircraft at the same time last night we had an APU door fault on one of the aircraft. While filling out the MEL log item and placard for the APU door message I filled out the log page and started to sign off item and got involved adding a statement to the yellow placard requested by maintenance control. I forgot to come back to that log page and put my signature; I had the date filled out but missed the signature. It was an error on my part trying to get a flight crew out before they timed out. I was trying to comply with too many tasks at one time. Had two departures at the same time and a deadline. Take more time and attention to task at hand.'
Narrative 2 - 'A Fuel door recycling Airworthiness Directive (AD) was complied with but the misc. 91 was not sent correctly. We were unaware that there was required text that was supposed to be sent along with the misc 91. This was a relatively new AD and the specifics of compliance were confusing. Since neither the First Officer or I had flown since this AD came out; the fact that wording was supposed to accompany the misc 91 was missed. It would be nice if the exact steps required were readily available in the logbook or somewhere handy during the preflight.'

summary: A pilot assigned to a reposition flight noticed a logbook sign-off indicating the aircraft was operating under a special flight permit (ferry permit) for engine damage. The pilot discovered that the actual physical permit was missing from the logbook and dispatch had failed to properly document the ferry status on the release.
rationale: This report is classified as Logbook Entry because the critical safety hazard was the absence of the physical Special Flight Permit within the aircraft's logbook, masking the aircraft's true airworthiness status. This explicitly matches the "Missing Signatures & Forms" criteria added to the reference guide. It is not Company Policy because the hazard was a specific missing document in the aircraft's official records rather than a systemic management directive to fly illegally. It is not Procedure because the pilot successfully caught the error before departing; the failure was the prior administrative preparation of the logbook.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Was given a two hour callout to reposition a plane. I asked the scheduler assigning me the assignment if it were a reposition flight or an actual ferry flight to which I was informed it was a reposition flight. I report to the airport as scheduled and on the ride to meet the airplane at the handstand I review the logbook and notice a sign off in it mentioning the plane was safe to operate under a special flight permit. I asked the operations agent driving us to the plane if this was operating as a ferry or repositioning flight and she was under the impression it was to be a repositioning flight. I look on the release for any indication that the plane was to be ferried on a ferry permit and no remark was noted at the time. I call Dispatch to speak with Maintenance Control for clarification. They proceed to tell me it is an actual special flight permit ferry flight for a small dent in the number one engine cowling. I ask where the ferry permit was as it was not in the maintenance logbook and am told they could email it to me. I asked for a printed copy to be sent to the plane as is required per the FOM as well as to leave in the logbook to be abundantly clear the plane was operating on a ferry permit. I called Dispatch to inform them that we were indeed operating a ferry flight and requested they add a remark in the release for it. I was told multiple times by both Dispatch and Maintenance that they could just email me the ferry permit so I could review it. It was surprising to me how I was handed a signed off maintenance log and told the plane was a normal repositioning flight. If I had not looked at previous write ups I would not have noticed the sign off indicating the plane was to operate under a ferry permit. I easily could have just taken the plane with no ferry permit and flown up to ZZZ1 in violation of our FOM and FARs. We received the paper copy of the permit as well as the required printout. I called the Chief Pilot to ensure they were aware of this ferry flight as the FOM required the Chief Pilot or one of the designees to be informed before a line crew could fly it. Seeing how disorganized and misinformed everything was I wanted to ensure they were aware of the flight; which they were. After a thorough review to ensure there were no operating limitations we flew to ZZZ1 with no further issues.I think there needs to be better policies in place requiring Maintenance; Dispatch; CSS; and Station operations to communicate more effectively. I feel as a reserve I have been assigned a significant number of repositioning flights that are always very disorganized and misinformed. If I had just done the minimum required logbook check I would not have noticed the sign off mentioning the special flight permit. If I had not noticed that we would have simply preflight the airplane and departed illegally and very possibly unsafely should there have been operating limitations. Luckily today we had no operating limitations but that may not always be the case.'

summary: An A&P mechanic with Inspection Authorization signed off an annual inspection on an aircraft they had known for 30 years, assuming all historical paperwork was in order. An FAA review later revealed that the Form 337 and field approval paperwork for the engine installation was completely missing from the aircraft's logbooks.
rationale: This is assigned the Logbook Entry label because the safety and regulatory violation was the absence of a required FAA Form 337 and installation paperwork in the aircraft's historical logs. This aligns directly with the "Missing Signatures & Forms" repeating issue. It is not Procedure because the physical engine installation had functioned safely for 30 years; the defect was entirely within the historical documentation. It is not Manuals because the missing forms are specific historical records for that individual aircraft, rather than a general maintenance reference guide.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Acting as an A&P with Inspection Authorization; I signed off an annual inspection for a Client. The aircraft was [later] involved in a hand propping occurrence.As a result; the FAA requested to see the aircraft logbooks. Upon review; they noticed that the form and field approval paperwork for the installation of the engine was not in the Logbooks.The engine has been on that aircraft for 30 plus years; and I have been familiar with said aircraft all of those years; so I assumed that the paperwork had been done; and the installation was legal.'

summary: A pilot grounded an aircraft due to a hydraulic fluid leak, but the next day noticed the mechanics had cleared the discrepancy simply by wiping the line clean with alcohol and re-securing it. The pilot expressed frustration that signing off a logbook by merely removing evidence of a leak rather than fixing the root cause is becoming a dangerous cultural trend.
rationale: This falls under Logbook Entry because the primary issue is a dangerously inadequate corrective action entry signed off in the aircraft's official maintenance log. This perfectly matches the "Inadequate Corrective Action Entries" criteria where mechanics use the logbook to administratively clear a fault without repairing it. It is not Aircraft because the core focus of the pilot's safety report is the falsified/inadequate nature of the maintenance documentation rather than the leak itself. It is not Company Policy because the specific manifestation of the poor culture occurred via the formal logbook sign-off mechanism.
text: Narrative 1 - 'We arrived in ZZZ airport and discovered during post flight that the emergency brake and gear pneumatic vent lines were dripping hydraulic fluid onto the ground. The aircraft was AOG'ed. The next morning we were assigned the aircraft again as the discrepancy was cleared by technicians from ZZZ1 Service Center. The summary of the corrective action is that the lines were disconnected; vacuumed free of hydraulic fluid cleaned with alcohol and re-secured. Three flights were performed on the following day; ending our day in ZZZ2. On post flight it was discovered that the leak had returned in the gear pneumatic vent line.How it can be said that removing evidence of a discrepancy is an appropriate corrective action is ridiculous. This is an ever increasing trend at this company. Who directly is responsible in this case would be the individual who signed this off; but more importantly it appears to me to be an increasing cultural trend that is spreading from our Operator to all maintenance contractors.'

summary: A flight crew preparing for a ferry flight realized the Aircraft Maintenance Logbook (AML) lacked a required MEL reference for disabled lavatory motors requested by a previous crew. The Mechanic On Duty became belligerent and tried to pressure the pilot into accepting the incomplete logbook before finally agreeing to add the proper MEL reference to the Logbook Deferral Sheet.
rationale: This incident is classified as Logbook Entry because the primary operational roadblock and dispute centered on missing MEL references within the official Aircraft Maintenance Logbook (AML) deferral sheets. The pilot refused to sign the flight plan until the logbook accurately reflected the maintenance status. It is not MEL because the dispute was about the *documentation* of the deferral in the logbook, not the physical application or legal limitations of the deferral itself. It is not Company Policy, despite the bullying, because the technical resolution required fixing the written logbook entry.
text: Narrative 1 - 'My First Officer and I arrived at the jet we were to ferry with no real information as to what ailed it. Once local techs arrived; I learned another crew had attempted the ferry earlier in the day. The apparent issue with the jet was a lingering aroma of vomit. My First Officer and I confirmed this but believed the smell wasn't overwhelming. That previous crew; as well; requested the lav vacuum motors be pulled and collared. That presented a problem. There was no MEL reference in the AML regarding these entries. To my knowledge at that moment; I was looking at nearly 3 hours in the jet with no bathroom. I shared this concern with Dispatch. He agreed this was a problem. Shortly after; I received a call from 737 Tech. The gentleman was only there to relay information he received from a previous tech. He referenced an MEL for me that indicated the lavs could be used above 16;000 feet. So we'll have lavs; but why don't I have that reference in the AML? Again; passing along information; he said word is the EA covered the lack of an MEL reference. We ended that call. I checked the FOM and it seemed to indicate that was correct.Now to the EA. It indicated the flight crew requested the lav motors be disabled and an EA be issued for this maintenance ferry flight. The thing is; when I sign the flight plan; I believe I am signing the totality of the documents. And I was not the flight crew who requested the maintenance action or the EA. Soon after; I received a call back from the Technician with whom I had spoken earlier and he asked if I had called [name]; yet. No. I didn't know a [name]. The Technician gave me his number; so I called. He answered. I told him I had been asked by Technician to call; but wasn't sure why.[Name] was apparently the Mechanic On Duty that evening. He wanted to know why I was having problems getting underway. I explained the problem I was having with paperwork. In particular I was still leery of the AML; I was hoping to understand why the previous crew requested Maintenance on the lavs before they left (I had no doubt they must've had a good reason); plus I didn't feel as if I could sign the flight plan because the EA wasn't reflective of my actions; it was reflective of another crews actions. [Name] explained that we ferry airplanes all the time and I needed to understand he knew what he was doing. Further; he offered to get a Fleet Captain on the phone to help explain that to me. I shared with him that wouldn't be necessary. He asked what I needed in order to get going. I suggested he correct the EA; if that was possible; to reflect I wasn't responsible for requesting maintenance on the lavs. I also asked him what benefit that maintenance action offered. Regarding the previous crews maintenance request on the lavs; flight crews; he told me; make mistakes in the AML all the time. That was pretty much it for me. I told him that telling me about mistakes; in his opinion; flight crews made routinely with the AML wasn't a particularly great way to promote good will. He started to say something else. I said good bye and ended the call.During all this time; the local maintenance team was doing their level best to support us. As well; my Dispatcher was nothing short of extremely helpful. So; I give Dispatch a call to ask where we go from here. Unfortunately; the way forward was a three-way with [name]; Dispatch and me. [Name] agreed to change the EA; send it and then the spotting message; Dispatch would unlock the flight plan; we'd get fuel and be underway.Taxiing away; we couldn't get a closeout. That took a call to Loads who was still waiting on maintenance to unlock something or other. A few moments later; we had a closeout. Further along in the taxi; Tower said we needed to call company. We called Operations. They needed info on catering carts. Finally; number one for take-off; Tower called again to let us know company had called to say we couldn't leave. We received taxi instructions down the runway and clear; where I could call dispatch. Dispatch had been receiving calls from Mechanic On Duty first saying we couldn't go; then we could; and now; finally; we needed to return to the maintenance ramp because our paperwork wasn't correct. We taxied back. Local Maintenance came on board; asked for the AML; at which point; over the phone; [name] advised the local maintenance manager that an MEL reference needed to be added to each Logbook Deferral Sheet in the AML - the same MEL reference I had been previously told was covered instead by the EA. Huh. Go figure. You know; it seems that if we really do this all the time; we really ought to know what we're doing. I certainly don't need a bully trying to convince me to do something about which we clearly aren't so good at after all.I remain disgusted by the bullying I received. It is my responsibility to sign federal documents attesting to the legal nature of each flight. Clearly; we have a lot to learn about ferry permits. As well; our FOM needs to more clearly assist flight crews with regards to these unusual situations. Obviously; our Mechanics on Duty - at least this Mechanic - do not have our back. This guy was more concerned with moving metal than he was about moving metal safely and legally. Honestly; should he be in charge of a maintenance ferry I'm assigned to work in the future; I expect I'll walk away from the flight long before we start engines.'

summary: A pilot was unable to access their electronic personal logbook because their phone broke during the COVID-19 pandemic and could not be repaired for months. The pilot subsequently flew an IFR flight assuming they were current through airline requirements, but later realized they could not legally verify their approach currency.
rationale: This is classified as Logbook Entry because the root cause of the regulatory deviation was the pilot's inability to access and track their personal flight logbook records. This maps directly to the "Personal Logbook Currency" repeating issue. It is not Company Policy because the airline's training cycle was legal; the failure was the pilot's personal record-keeping for Part 91 operations. It is not Human Factors because the specific regulatory failure stems from missing or inaccessible historical documentation rather than a physical flying error.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I work for a Part 121 Airline and I'm kept current through them; but instead of six month check rides because of AQP we now do check rides over 9 months. So I keep a log to record approaches so I'm current for General Aviation. My friends who work for the airline tell me I'm always current as long as I work for the airline. I use a electronic logbook but my phone broke in March because of COVID all the apple stores where closed and I did not get it repaired until May. So I was not able to record my approaches. With the reduced schedules at work and my phone being broke I can't verify I have enough approaches to be current I talked it over with several people and they said I was current because of the airline requirements.Which makes sense that if I can go fly an airliner I should be able to fly Part 91. Which in most cases is less restrictive. But I filed IFR flight on date. Afterwards I tried to find where I was still legal for Part 91 and could not find it. So on the date I got an IPC. To make sure I was legal in the future. But I might not have been current the day before.'

summary: A mechanic completed a taxi light installation and all physical inspections before a flight, but failed to sign the Inspection Authorization (IA) block on the Form 337 until the following day. A colleague preflighting the aircraft assumed the logbook paperwork was complete because it was sitting on the mechanic's desk.
rationale: This incident fits Logbook Entry because the sole safety and regulatory violation was a missing signature on a Form 337 prior to the aircraft returning to service. This matches the "Missing Signatures & Forms" criteria perfectly. It is not Procedure because the physical installation, inspection, and aircraft configuration were all executed perfectly. It is not Human Factors because this taxonomy explicitly categorizes failures of formal maintenance documentation and sign-offs under this specific label.
text: Narrative 1 - 'On DATE; Company Maintenance Technicians had replaced the existing OEM Taxi Light Installation with Whelen Aerospace Technologies Boom Beam Taxi light System IAW STC# ST02893AT on G-550 X. I was intermittently involved in the installation and had inspected the completed installation by request of the installing technician. I was notified on DATE1 of the intent to fly the aircraft in the evening so the pilots could observe the performance of the new lights. On the day of the flight I had been working on other projects and was preparing to depart for the day when the pilots arrived and asked if we had completed our maintenance pre-flight inspection. I discovered that the preflight had not been completed and proceeded to perform the preflight with the installing technician. Part of our preflight includes a review of the maintenance records and after reviewing the documents in Gulfstream's Computerized Maintenance Program (CMP) determined that there were no outstanding maintenance items. I asked the installing technician if the 337 had been completed and he responded with 'It's on Mechanic's (the Certifying IA) Desk' which I assumed to mean that it was complete.I signed the maintenance release and preflight form and departed for the day. The aircraft flew under part 91 rules that evening without incident.On DATE2 I spoke with Mechanic and discovered that although he had completed the inspection and all other associated paperwork before the flight; he had not signed the IA block of the 337 until the evening of the DATE3.Overall there were no unsafe conditions; only an oversight in paperwork. Upon discovery of the documentation error I self reported to my Supervisor and filed this report. An internal investigation has been initiated by my supervisor.'

summary: During scheduled maintenance, mechanics removed and inspected fuel probes but became rushed and returned the aircraft to service without reinstalling the panels. The mechanics also completely failed to document any of the fuel probe work in the aircraft's logbook.
rationale: This report is classified as Logbook Entry because a primary and critical safety failure was the complete lack of documentation in the maintenance logbook for work performed on the fuel system. This directly aligns with the "Undocumented Maintenance" repeating issue. While leaving the panels off is a Procedure error, the reporter heavily emphasizes the failure to write the logbook entries as the reason the uninstalled panels went unnoticed during the handover. It is not Aircraft because the root cause of the unairworthy release was the rushed, undocumented maintenance process.
text: Narrative 1 - 'We had performed scheduled maintenance on a BK117C2. We also performed troubleshooting of a fuel quantity fluctuation issue. 2 main tank fuel probes were removed and inspected; one was replaced. After the fuel probes were reinstalled the aircraft was moved outside for refueling and leak checks. During this time I was doing all the log book entries that were told to me. I did not enter any fuel probe entries as I wasn't performing the MX and didn't have any paperwork at that time. As time went on we got in a hurry to return the aircraft to service and none of the fuel probe work was documented by myself or the mechanic that performed the MX. The aircraft was returned to service without the correct paperwork and flew back to [the] hospital at ZZZ where it is based. I received a call from one of the mechanics asking if the panels had been reinstalled and I told him that I didn't know as I didn't perform the maintenance. It was then that we discovered that the panels were in fact not reinstalled. I immediately called my supervisor and reported the undocumented maintenance and we took the aircraft back out of service and sent mechanics to reinstall the panels and complete the paperwork correctly. I believe that we were rushing maintenance to get the aircraft in service and crucial steps were missed.Perform a more detailed passover between mechanics; Communicate better between Mechanics.'

summary: A company mask mandate was implemented to ensure safety during the pandemic, but management at the reporter's location failed to enforce it. The reporter noted that almost an entire shift was working without masks, creating an unsafe environment and highlighting management's complicity.
rationale: This is classified as Company Policy because the hazard stems from management's systemic failure to enforce an established occupational health and safety mandate. It matches the category's focus on organizational decisions and management oversight failures. It is not Human Factors because it involves an administrative failure of leadership enforcement rather than a single individual's momentary cognitive lapse. It is not Procedure because the issue is the lack of overarching administrative accountability, not a tactical flight or ramp operation error.
text: Narrative 1 - 'When company announced the requirements for wearing a mask anytime we could not practice 6 feet of distance; I was happy and felt coming into work would be more safe. Problem is this policy has been in place and it seems like management is not enforcing employees to wear the mask. I haven't seen it myself but I've been told an entire shift doesn't wear mask except maybe two employees. I do feel like it's hard coming into work because I don't feel safe with co-workers who aren't following the new policy and management who allows it to happen.'

summary: Station operations posted a 58-minute delay and rapid turnaround for a flight without informing or consulting Dispatch. The reporter emphasized that this organizational structure usurps Dispatch's operational control and potentially compromises crew legality and weather minimum planning.
rationale: This is classified as Company Policy because it involves a systemic organizational conflict over operational control, where station agents are structurally allowed to override Dispatch authority. This aligns directly with the sub-category of organizational directives and systemic operational pressures. It is not Procedure because the root problem is the company's administrative IT structure that grants gate agents inappropriate access to flight dispatch parameters. It is not Human Factors because it represents an organizational power conflict, not a misunderstanding of a task.
text: Narrative 1 - 'Operations delayed our Aircraft X without informing Dispatch prior to posting the delay. They ended up putting a 58 minute turn on the flight which is way more than [company policy]. Once again; [company] gate agents do not exercise operational control and should not have access to post delays on our flights. Who is to say that crew was legal to operate the flight given the delay? Who is to say the TAF in ZZZ1 at arrival was legal with the new ETA? Operations has no idea about these issues and thus should not have a say in when a flight departs. [Company] must change this policy. It goes against the very fabric of Dispatch the FAR's created.'

summary: A contract vendor performed fuel tank maintenance without following required safety checklists or establishing proper safety perimeters. Despite the reporter raising concerns about the unsafe environment, company supervisors and managers explicitly ignored the warnings and directed the crew to work directly next to the open fuel tanks.
rationale: The Company Policy classification is applied because company management explicitly overrode safety concerns and forced employees to work in a hazardous environment. This directly maps to the "Management pressure to fly/accept unairworthy aircraft" and management overriding safety criteria. It is not Procedure because the critical failure was the supervisor's deliberate administrative command to ignore safety protocols, rather than a simple mechanic's accidental omission. It is not Human Factors because the exposure to the hazard was mandated by leadership, not caused by inattention.
text:
Narrative 1 - 'I was assigned to work Aircraft X while contract vendor was working on the RH wing fuel tanks and fuel tank panels were open. Myself and Crew Member informed our Manager and Supervisor of many safety concerns we had on the work being performed by the vendor. Example of [these] concerns are: Fuel tank pre-entry check list was never filled out by vendor or Supervisor; no fuel-gas concentration readings were recorded. Per A320 fleet AMM (Aircraft Maintenance Manual) 28-10-00-910-002-A. Aircraft supposed to have a rope barrier around the entire perimeter of Aircraft and warning sign on four areas per fig. sheet 1 per AMM. The fuel vendor only roped just one section of Aircraft while having open fuel tank covers. Supervisor and Manager ignored our safety concerns of the vendor improper maintenance procedures and directed us to work next to the open fuel tank areas ( L/H pack Bay ). I felt that Management was making me work under unsafe work environment. Contract vendor not following safety procedures per AMM.Contract vendors and Management need to follow all safety precautions and not ignore them.'
Narrative 2 - 'I was assigned to work Aircraft X while contract vendor was working on the RH wing fuel tanks and fuel tank panels were open. Myself and Crew Member informed our Manager and Supervisor of many safety concerns we had on the work being performed by the vendor. Example of [these] concerns are: Fuel tank pre-entry check list was never filled out by vendor or Supervisor; no fuel-gas concentration readings were recorded. Per A320 fleet AMM (Aircraft Maintenance Manual) 28-10-00-910-002-A. Aircraft supposed to have a rope barrier around the entire perimeter of Aircraft and warning sign on four areas per fig. sheet 1 per AMM. The fuel vendor only roped just one section of Aircraft while having open fuel tank covers. Supervisor and Manager ignored our safety concerns of the vendor improper maintenance procedures and directed us to work next to the open fuel tank areas ( L/H pack Bay ). I feel like the company ignored our safety concerns and put pressure and directed us in working on an unsafe work environment per Aircraft AMM.Contract vendor was not following safety procedures per aircraft maintenance manuals.Contract vendors are required to follow each steps on AMM including completing all documentation of all maintenance performed on Aircraft.'

summary: A pilot raised severe safety concerns regarding a newly mandated company taxi procedure that requires the First Officer to be heads-down in the FMC entering weight and balance data during active taxi. The reporter argued that this procedure drastically reduces situational awareness and introduces unnecessary risks to achieve minimal fuel savings.
rationale: This fits Company Policy because the hazard is a direct result of a newly implemented Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) designed by corporate management. The organizational directive itself inherently reduces safety margins by prioritizing financial performance over heads-up taxiing. It is not Procedure in the tactical sense, because the crew is actually adhering perfectly to the mandated checklist; the checklist itself is the hazard. It is not Human Factors because the distraction is administratively forced rather than accidental.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I am writing to express my extreme concern over the new procedures for taxi before receiving the final weight and balance. Even during a simple taxi; the procedures add significant distraction and uncertainty to our flight. I have been trying to give these procedures a chance; but every time I find them cumbersome; distracting; and; as they are presently written; I find them categorically unsafe. They add significant and unnecessary risk to the taxi environment; and they must be revoked or revised immediately to prevent a taxiway or runway incursion or worse. The potential fuel and engine wear savings are simply not worth this risk. Some of my most pressing concerns with the procedures include: 1. They drastically increases heads-down time for the FO. a. Completing the procedure and checklist can take two minutes or more under ideal circumstances; without interruption from ATC or others (which requires restarting the checklist). I encourage the company and FAA to consider that if this is safe; how can it be considered unsafe to contact operations or maintenance control while on the ground? b. The 737 FM advises First Officers to 'Avoid heads-down in high-risk areas such as ramps; intersections; hot spots; and runways.' At many airports; particularly ORD; EWR; SFO; and others; this is nearly impossible to accomplish under normal taxi scenarios. It was this provision that dictated our past operating practices where taxi was the ONLY focus for both pilots. Now; the FO is taken out of the loop for significant amounts of time; exposing the flight to unnecessary risk. c. The 737 FM also says the First Officer 'must maintain situational awareness at all times while the aircraft is moving.' Personal experience has shown this to be challenging at best for first officers completing these procedures; especially when compounded with single engine taxi procedures; even at airports with relatively simple taxi routings. Only by stopping the aircraft can this be done safely. 2. This procedure prioritizes minimal fuel and engine wear savings at the expense of increased risk during the taxi phase of flight. a. While I agree in concept with the intent of the procedure; crews on the B737 should not be required to obtain new takeoff data if the weight and balance shows the actual aircraft weight is less than or equal to that of the planned weight on the flight plan. This is what the Airbus fleet does; and it would eliminate significant risk. 3. Issues with the Before Takeoff Checklist. (To The Line) a. Checklist is non-linear flow. The FO moves in and out of the FMC; checking various items on several pages; often leading to the FMC not being set correctly (Legs Page or Takeoff Page) for takeoff.b. It is excessively complicated (evidenced by numerous parentheses to remind FO what to check--the only checklist even using parentheses.)c. It is read/do; not flow/verify as all other checklists are. (I realize it is intended to be flow/verify; but the nature of the checking requires it to basically be treated as read/do). d. It is difficult if not impossible for the captain to adequately follow and crosscheck inputs/responses without stopping the aircraft on the taxiway. The push to do the checklist during the taxi encourages a 'texting while driving' effect of weaving along taxiway; distracting the captain from his/her primary task of taxiing the plane and listening to ATC; substantially increasing the risk of hitting an obstacle or having a ground incursion. e. It eliminates effective verbalize; verify; monitor of critical changes; requiring captain to simply 'trust' FO made correct changes. f. It encourages the flight crew to complete the checklist prior to second engine start; because of time constraints. Doing so causes the flight crew to skip between and around multiple checklists. g. It should be titled the Taxi With Weights checklist.I am not normally resistant to procedural changes as I understand there isa lot of discussion that goes into them and that sound business fundamentals are at their heart. But this procedure is the first change I find to be incredibly dangerous as the procedures are presently written. Planning; critical FMC entries; and crew briefings should be done at the gate; not on the taxiway; except for the most abnormal situations. This procedure flips that years-long practice on its head in order to achieve financial performance. And I'm sincerely hopeful the company or the FAA will recognize that these procedures need to be revoked and revised immediately.'

summary: Airline cleaning crews boarded an aircraft during deplaning and began spraying toxic cleaning chemicals while passengers and crew were still present. The flight attendant reported that the spray burned their lungs and demanded the airline change its turnaround policy to wait until the aircraft is fully empty.
rationale: This is Company Policy because the hazard is generated by a systemic corporate cleaning directive that mandates spraying chemicals during active deplaning to accelerate turnaround times. This aligns with occupational health and workplace safety policy flaws. It is not Environment - Non Weather Related because the toxic substance was intentionally introduced by the company's own servicing schedule rather than an external event. It is not Procedure because the cleaners were following the airline's aggressive timeline rather than making an unprompted mistake.
text: Narrative 1 - 'The cleaners came on during deplaning and started spraying the toxic cleaner. I was trying to stand and say goodbye to passengers; but the spray was burning my lungs; through my mask. I saw one cleaner wet their rag with multiple sprays as a child walked by right next to it. I was hoping with some fresh air my lungs would feel better; but they don't.This practice of bombarding crew and passengers with this spray during deplaning is hazardous to everyone's health. Toxic cleaning spray being sprayed during deplaning both in First Class and the bathroom and galley shrouded my area during deplaning.Burned my lungs. Hoping fresh air will help. Giving it 24 hours to feel better.  Otherwise will see a Doctor. Airline needs to let people off the plane before they start with the spraying of the cleaner. I have (had) great lungs; so if it's affecting me; then anyone with any lung issue is going to be in big trouble.'

summary: An aircraft climbed into a controller's airspace without a point-out from the adjacent sector, followed shortly by two more aircraft handed off with minimal separation. The controller filed the report to highlight the adjacent sector's persistently poor coordination practices.
rationale: This is classified as Company Policy because the systemic lack of coordination points to local ATC facility policy flaws or differing operational standards between adjacent sectors. The organizational failure to enforce strict point-out policies creates recurrent airspace hazards. It is not Airspace Structure because the geometric boundaries of the sectors are not the hazard; the administrative rules governing handoffs are. It is not Human Factors because it points to a normalized deviation or facility-wide practice rather than a single controller's isolated mistake.
text: Narrative 1 - 'A data block auto acquired on my scope on the northwest side of the sector. It was Aircraft X. The aircraft was climbing above my airspace without a pointout. The adjacent Center sector never called with a pointout and they never called to say sorry so I don't think they even knew they did it. 10 to 20 minutes later the same sector handed off two aircraft at 35000 feet coming together one to two minutes inside our sector's airspace. I didn't get the callsigns for those aircraft but if you watch the Falcon replay you will know the two aircraft I am talking about. One aircraft was descended to 33000 feet. The other aircraft just finished climbing and was picking up speed. I put a 5 mile ring up on the arrival. When they crossed they had 5.1 miles so it would have worked but I think that's too close to hand the next controller. This should probably be stated on another report with more accurate information; but I just wanted to make sure it was at least reported.I would recommend that the controller working this sector pay more attention to their surroundings. Also let the controller know that aircraft typically speed up when they level off unless there is a dramatic change in wind speed.'

summary: An aggressive passenger demanded alcohol, threatened the crew, and filmed the flight attendants while other passengers intervened to protect the crew. The flight attendant requested explicit corporate guidance on company policy regarding passengers videotaping crew members during security incidents to better handle future occurrences.
rationale: This falls under Company Policy because the core objective of the safety report is a request to close a corporate policy gap regarding crew privacy and customer conduct enforcement. The lack of clear administrative guidance leaves the crew vulnerable during security events. It is not Environment - Non Weather Related (Passenger Misconduct) because the reporter's primary focus in the narrative is resolving the overarching corporate policy ambiguity, rather than solely documenting the passenger's behavior. It is not Human Factors as no operational error occurred.
text: Narrative 1 - 'I was the lead FA. Passenger in [seat] XXB asked me if he could have wine I told him it was available for purchase in economy during the service. He asked me again for wine while I was walking back to the back to help them with the cart. I told him the same thing. He also asked the FAs in the back during boarding for wine. They both explained he could purchase wine in the air and both gave him water. During the service; he asked one of them for wine. She saw he had cash and told him we accepted debit and credit only. Passenger became aggressive. Passenger then stood up and starting asking people to pay for him. I told him he needed to sit down. Passenger became argumentative. He demanded wine; I tell him no and he needs to lower his voice. Passenger then says he wants to talk to the (expletive) captain and he wants to know who the director of the airline is. I tell him I am the lead flight attendant on this flight. He is cussing and raising his voice and tells me he will pay me one million dollars for wine. No one serves him any alcohol. I go to the front and call the captain and inform him of what is happening. Captain declares threat level 1 and asks to monitor situation. Passenger in seat XXC is a woman. She comes to the front galley and asks if she can stay up here for a minute as she is uncomfortable. I tell her yes. I then see passenger in XXA has moved seats and is crying. I approach her and ask her if she is okay. She says yes but that she was scared and felt trapped because he wouldn't let her out of her seat. I continually check on her. I continue service. Passenger [16 rows behind row XX] tells me he saw him standing up and arguing and said he would help handle the situation if needed. Passenger [5 rows behind row XX] is a male. He also offered his help. He and another gentlemen moved to the seats next to the problem passenger as we were full and the two women did not feel comfortable sitting next to him. He boarded the plane with a boxing helmet on. He had this helmet as well as boxing gloves with him at his seat. He was taking pictures of us and filming the entire interaction during the flight. I kept the captain informed. Law enforcement met the airplane. We deplaned normally. Supervisor met the airplane as well. I am concerned that it is a rule that passengers cannot video tape or take photos of the flight attendants however it seems hard to enforce. With social media being the way it is today; it makes me uncomfortable that my face may be on his phone videos. As flight attendants are we allowed to tell them to delete the material? Or do we just advise them that it is against the rules to film us? I would like guidance on this issue so that if it were to happen again in the future; I would understand the best approach. I felt very uncomfortable during the interactions with the passenger and I feel strongly that the affected passengers are contacted by [company] to ensure we do not lose valuable customers.'

summary: A flight crew at a foreign station was provided an inadequate, tiny room for flight planning that lacked proper communication links to Dispatch. The poor physical setup and access hurdles systematically discourage crews from conducting proper dispatch briefings, incentivizing them to simply accept the paperwork at the gate.
rationale: This is classified as Company Policy because the airline's failure to provide adequate administrative facilities at the station creates a systemic disincentive for safe flight planning. This administrative and resource allocation decision degrades the safety culture. It is not Airport because the planning room is a corporate resource issue provided by the airline or its vendor, not an FAA or runway infrastructure problem. It is not Procedure because crews are responding logically to an administratively broken process.
text: Narrative 1 - 'The so-called flight planning area in ZZZZ is unsuitable. This is my 4th report on this station in the last year. The room is 9x9 room on the ramp level that is used by the ramp personnel to load the baggage scanner info; etc. There is a desk with a company computer; a printer and a phone. The phone to call dispatch does not go to dispatch. You have to call the number for maintenance control and get transferred over. There is one chair and not enough room for laying out a plotting chart; marking up flight plans; etc. The very nice (did I mention very nice?!?) Foreign Airline agents print off the paperwork for us and hand it to us as we walk down the jetway. If we were to go to the planning room we would need a ramp agent to come to the airplane and escort us down to the room and unlock the door. Most of the crews don't do this so most of the crews wind up taking what the dispatcher plans and probably never calling for a dispatcher briefing. Flight planning in the cockpit is not the best; safest practice - way too many distractions and not enough room.'

summary: A pilot reported that severe understaffing and scheduling to the absolute limits of Part 121 rules were creating extreme fatigue during long, multi-time-zone international pairings. The reporter noted that the existing fatigue management program is failing to prevent these scientifically destructive scheduling practices.
rationale: The Company Policy label perfectly fits this incident as it details systemic crew fatigue caused by corporate scheduling practices pushing legal limits. This is a primary repeating issue explicitly defined as "Crew fatigue and scheduling" under this category. It is not Staffing because, while understaffing is mentioned, the critical hazard is the corporate scheduling policy that legally but unsafely exploits the remaining crew. It is not Human Factors because the fatigue is a direct output of the airline's administrative scheduling matrix, not poor personal rest habits.
text: Narrative 1 - 'We are now grossly understaffed and are being scheduled to the extent of 121 rules. The unique situation [here] is that we have pairings that go around the world for an average of 17 days at one time. We constantly change times zones; sleep cycles (day/night); and now endure innumerable schedule changes that also disrupt not only food planning but sleep planning as well.This has led to extremely unhealthy habits that are unavoidable and damaging to our abilities to operate safely. I am an average aged pilot; fairly fit with generally healthy eating habits. After coming back from a short pairing with three 20 hour overnights in a row; it took a better part of the week to recover from it. I am not alone. What we are being asked to do is undoubtedly scientifically destructive to our persons let alone the potential to the operation.Here is a very brief example of a summary of just one pilot's schedule:Day 1: Commercialed in from the states to the Near East. Rest before flight 13h.Day 2: 10h duty day flying Near East to Far East. Originally a short overnight was assigned.Day 3: 50h overnight due to a broken airplane. Delays kept rolling. A 24/7 was assigned.Day 5: 14h duty day flying Far East to Australia. 20h overnight assigned.Day 6: 11:30h duty day flying Australia to Mid East. 18:30h overnight assigned.Day 7: 6:30h duty day flying Mid East to Far East. 21h overnight assigned.Day 8: Commercialed home.To endure the '20h' overnights and prevent two sleep opportunities in favor of one big sleep opportunity; we stayed up as much as possible upon landing. Though this helped to feel rested for the flights; fatigue accumulated and by the time I got home my body crashed.This is an example of just one week of this unhealthy; unsustainable schedule.They say knowing is half the battle. I wanted to document this continual; no-end-in-sight situation to ensure the knowledge was there that this situation IS happening and not changing despite us having a fatigue program in place that is supposed to help prevent the continuation of these types of situations when documented.'

summary: A contract maintenance employee noted that they and their peers are denied direct access to the airline's safety reporting system and must rely on company supervisors to log reports for them. The reporter warned that this policy severely undermines the integrity of the company's safety culture and creates a massive blind spot.
rationale: This fits Company Policy because the hazard is a direct result of an organizational administrative rule that excludes contract workers from the corporate safety management system. This systemic policy flaw actively suppresses critical safety reporting. It is not Procedure because the exclusion is a mandated corporate structure, not a tactical failure to fill out a form. It is not Human Factors because the mechanics are actively trying to report safety issues but are administratively blocked from doing so.
text: Narrative 1 - '[The Company employs] a disproportionate amount of contact Maintenance personnel. The contract maintenance personal work very hard and under much stress being dictated to performing multiple maintenance tasks at the behest of a very small cadre of [Company] Maintenance personal. It also appears that this small cadre of [Company] Maintenance employees work in a supervisory role. As a contracted maintenance employee; it has come to my attention; that they are not afforded direct access to the company safety reporting system in either [of the two available] systems. The only way a contracted mechanic can report a safety concern of the [Company] operations is to go to a [Company] employed Mechanic (Supervisor) and ask for access into the reporting system; presumably under that supervisors name access. If my understanding is correct; this approach could severely undermine the integrity of the overall safety reporting system and would create a gaping hole embedded within the [Company's] Safety System. If the [Review Board] can verify that contracted maintenance personnel who work for and on the aircraft that service the certificates under [Company] have unimpeded access to the safety reporting system all would be better served and a better safety culture can coexist. Please if the [Review Board] can address the potential safety issues that potentially exists the safety system would be the better for the effort.'
